PHASE 3: BUILD THE STRATEGIC FOUNDATION
Before writing, construct the strategic foundation for leading them from Unaware ‚Üí Aware.

STEP 1: BUILD THE ONE BELIEF
The One Belief Formula for Unaware:
"[SURFACE EXPERIENCE] is actually caused by [HIDDEN PROBLEM via UMP] ‚Äî
[PRODUCT] solves it through [UMS] ‚Äî giving you [LEVEL 2 EMOTIONAL DESIRE]"
Extract and identify:
‚óè‚Äã Surface Experience: What they feel/experience but don't label as a problem
‚óè‚Äã Hidden Problem: What they don't know is happening (revealed by UMP)
‚óè‚Äã Mechanism Behind Problem (UMP): The mechanism that REVEALS the hidden
problem
‚óè‚Äã Solution: Your product name
‚óè‚Äã Mechanism Behind Solution (UMS): How the product addresses the hidden problem
‚óè‚Äã Emotional Desire: The Level 2 emotional desire (not surface want, but deeper
emotional drive)
The One Belief must be built around Level 2 (emotional drive), not Level 1 (surface want).

STEP 2: ENGINEER THE GRADUALIZATION SEQUENCE
For Unaware, gradualization is about leading them INTO problem awareness, not shifting
beliefs about solutions.
CRITICAL DISTINCTION:
‚óè‚Äã Problem-Aware Gradualization: "Here's WHY your known problem happens" (shifting
belief about mechanism)
‚óè‚Äã Unaware Gradualization: "Here's a problem you didn't know you had" (creating
awareness)
Map 5-7 micro-steps that lead from "I feel this but it's normal" ‚Üí "Holy shit, there's an
actual problem I didn't know about":

Micro-Step 1: Validate The Experience
‚óè‚Äã Establish shared experience (I felt this too)
‚óè‚Äã Make them feel seen and understood
‚óè‚Äã DO NOT hint that anything is wrong yet
Micro-Step 2: Show The Attribution
‚óè‚Äã State what they (and you) believed explained it
‚óè‚Äã Validate that belief as reasonable
Micro-Step 3: Introduce Contrast/Doubt
‚óè‚Äã Show something that doesn't fit the attribution
‚óè‚Äã Someone else with same situation but different outcome
‚óè‚Äã A small anomaly that creates curiosity
Micro-Step 4: Deepen The Doubt + Dramatize Emotional Cost
‚óè‚Äã Show the emotional cost of the experience
‚óè‚Äã Make them feel the weight of it
‚óè‚Äã Still no problem revealed ‚Äî just the pain of the experience
Micro-Step 5: Discovery Setup
‚óè‚Äã Natural circumstance leads to new information
‚óè‚Äã Authority figure or credible source introduces mechanism
‚óè‚Äã Frame as curiosity/conversation, not education
Micro-Step 6: Mechanism Reveal (UMP)
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Authority explains the hidden problem
Make it verifiable ‚Äî they can check this themselves
The "holy shit" moment of awareness
This is where you REVEAL the problem they didn't know existed

Micro-Step 7: Personal Verification
‚óè‚Äã Apply the new information to your own situation
‚óè‚Äã Show the proof in your own life
‚óè‚Äã Reader thinks: "Wait, that applies to ME too"
Micro-Step 8: State New Awareness
‚óè‚Äã "That's when I realized..."
‚óè‚Äã Frame as discovery, not pitch
‚óè‚Äã Connect to original experience

STEP 3: IDENTIFY THE TIER OF NEW INFORMATION
The TYPE of new information determines how powerful the belief shift is.
THE 4 TIERS OF NEW INFORMATION:

TIER 1 - Reframe Existing Observable Problem (MOST POWERFUL)
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Take something they already experience and explain it differently
Makes them realize "I've been looking at this wrong the whole time"
No new problem to create awareness of ‚Äî just new understanding of existing reality
They can often verify/observe this themselves immediately

Power: Highest. Reframes their lived experience. Often immediately verifiable.
Best for: Any awareness level, especially Unaware (helps them see what's already happening)

TIER 2 - Explain Why Previous Solutions Failed (VERY POWERFUL)
‚óè‚Äã Reveals why the solutions they tried (or think would work) couldn't work
‚óè‚Äã Removes self-blame and validates their experience
‚óè‚Äã Shows it wasn't their fault ‚Äî the solutions were fundamentally incomplete
Power: Very high. Explains pattern of failure. Creates relief ("it wasn't me").
Best for: Unaware, Problem-Aware, Solution-Aware (validates why nothing worked)
Note for Unaware: Use sparingly ‚Äî they may not have tried formal solutions. But they may
have tried CASUAL attempts.

TIER 3 - Introduce New Mechanism for Familiar Problem (POWERFUL)
‚óè‚Äã Shows there's a different way to solve what they know (or vaguely sense) needs solving
‚óè‚Äã Reveals a mechanism/process they didn't know existed
‚óè‚Äã Positions your solution as different approach, not better execution
Power: Good. Offers new path forward.
Best for: Problem-Aware, Solution-Aware (reveals new solution path)

TIER 4 - Create Awareness of Problem They Didn't Know Existed (NECESSARY FOR
TRUE UNAWARE)
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Introduces entirely new problem they weren't worried about
Requires building awareness before they care
Highest resistance because "I wasn't worried about this before"
BUT: Often necessary for Unaware markets where they don't even know the problem
exists

Power: Lowest in terms of instant believability, BUT necessary for true Unaware markets.
Best for: Unaware markets specifically (when creating problem awareness is required)

FOR UNAWARE MARKETS:
Best: Tier 1 or Tier 4
‚óè‚Äã Tier 1: Reframe something they already observe (most powerful)
‚óè‚Äã Tier 4: Create awareness of hidden problem (necessary when they're truly unaware)
Good: Tier 2 if applicable
‚óè‚Äã If they've tried casual things
Ideal sequence for Unaware:
1.‚Äã Tier 4 in Micro-Step 6 (reveal hidden problem)
2.‚Äã Tier 1 to make it observable (show them proof in their own life)
3.‚Äã Tier 2 if applicable (why their casual attempts failed)
4.‚Äã Tier 3 for UMS (your mechanism)

YOUR JOB in Step 3:
1.‚Äã Identify which tier the new information (UMP) falls into
2.‚Äã State it: "The new information is Tier [X]: [Type]"
3.‚Äã Explain: "This works for Unaware because: [1-2 sentence explanation]"

STEP 4: MAP TO THE 10 QUESTIONS

Using the inputs, prepare to answer these 10 questions sequentially in the copy:
1.‚Äã How is this different? [From Identification + UMP ‚Äî this reveals a problem they didn't
know about]
2.‚Äã What's in it for me? [From Mass Desire ‚Äî BOTH Level 1 and Level 2]
3.‚Äã How do I know this is real? [From Observable Proof, Authority Validation]
4.‚Äã What's holding me back? [For Unaware: Their false attribution / what they THINK
explains it]
5.‚Äã Who/What is to blame? [The hidden mechanism, NOT them]
6.‚Äã Why now? [Consequence of continuing without this knowledge]
7.‚Äã Why should I trust you? [Shared experience + authority validation]
8.‚Äã How does it work? [UMP + UMS explained simply]
9.‚Äã How can I get started? [The offer]
10.‚ÄãWhat do I have to lose? [Guarantee + risk reversal]
CRITICAL FOR Q#2: Q#2 must answer BOTH levels of desire:
‚óè‚Äã Surface benefit (what they get) [Level 1]
‚óè‚Äã Emotional payoff (why they actually want it) [Level 2]
This is where the emotional pivot happens.

STEP 5: VALIDATE BEFORE WRITING
Before writing, confirm:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚úì Does headline call out the identification without naming the hidden problem?
‚úì Will I validate the experience before introducing doubt?
‚úì Have I mapped the gradualization from Unaware ‚Üí Aware?
‚úì Have I identified the tier of new information?
‚úì Will I reveal the hidden problem through discovery, not claims?
‚úì Will the authority explain the UMP in plain language?
‚úì Can the reader verify the hidden problem in their own life?
‚úì Will the solution feel inevitable once the problem is revealed?
‚úì Does One Belief target Level 2 emotional desire?
‚úì Target length: 2,500-3,000 words

PHASE 4: WRITE THE COMPLETE AD COPY
Now write the complete ad following this structure exactly.

CORE STRUCTURE: The 10-Question Framework
Your copy must answer all 10 questions in sequence. Each question builds on the previous one.
Flow:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Q#1-3: Opening/Lead (first 1/3) ‚Äî Hook, promise, proof
Q#4-6: Problem crystallization (middle 1/3) ‚Äî Why they failed, enemy, urgency
Q#7-8: Authority & mechanism (middle 1/3) ‚Äî Credibility, how it works
Q#9-10: Offer & close (final 1/3) ‚Äî No-brainer offer, push-pull close

LENGTH & PACING
Target: 2,500-3,000 words (MAXIMUM 3,000)
Do NOT exceed 3,000 words. Tight is better than comprehensive.
Pacing discipline:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Each emotional beat appears maximum 2x
Discovery story: Key moments only, not play-by-play
Authority dialogue: One good explanation
Transformation timeline: 4-5 data points max

If running long, cut:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Repetitive emotional beats
Over-detailed discovery story
Multiple authority conversations
Excessive transformation data points

OPENING: Story Lead (Confession Style)
Use the confession-style story lead for Unaware markets.
Structure:
1. Identification headline
‚óè‚Äã Call out their surface experience or hidden emotional state

‚óè‚Äã Make them think: "That's me. How did they know?"
2. Confession opening
‚óè‚Äã "There's a moment [specific time] I've never told anyone about..."
‚óè‚Äã OR simply state the experience directly
‚óè‚Äã Extreme specificity: exact times, physical sensations, internal thoughts
3. The experience they've normalized
‚óè‚Äã What were they feeling?
‚óè‚Äã What were they doing?
‚óè‚Äã What did they think explained it?
4. Public vs. Private contrast (if applicable)
‚óè‚Äã What they say to others
‚óè‚Äã What they actually feel
‚óè‚Äã The gap between them

THE EMOTIONAL PIVOT (CRITICAL - Must Happen By Paragraph 7)
From Schwartz (Breakthrough Advertising, pg 52): "There are always TWO levels to every
desire: the stated want and the emotional drive. Your headline identifies Level 1. Your opening
body copy must IMMEDIATELY pivot to Level 2, or you'll get attention but no sales."
From Evaldo (16-Word Sales Letter, pg 37-39): "This is where most copywriters fuck up. They
list surface benefits. NOBODY CARES ABOUT THOSE THINGS. They care about what those
things GIVE them emotionally. The surface benefit is the MECHANISM for the emotional payoff.
If you don't connect to the emotional payoff in Q#2, they won't buy. Period."
From Masterson (Great Leads, pg 78-79): "Weak story leads show only the surface
transformation. Strong story leads show the EMOTIONAL transformation that the surface
change created. Show the surface transformation, but SELL the emotional one. That's what
people actually buy."
ALL THREE FRAMEWORKS TEACH: Surface problem hooks attention. Deep emotion drives
purchase.

Required pivot structure:
Paragraphs 1-3: Surface Experience Identification

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Open with the surface experience
Build recognition ("that's me")
Show public vs private split (if applicable)
Establish the Level 1 stated want

Paragraphs 4-7: THE PIVOT (CRITICAL - THIS IS Q#2)
‚óè‚Äã "But here's what [nobody tells you / I didn't expect / I didn't understand] about [surface
experience]..."
‚óè‚Äã Reveal Level 2 desire (the emotional drive)
‚óè‚Äã Use the pivot moment
‚óè‚Äã Make it physical/visceral (phantom weight, tightness in chest)
‚óè‚Äã Show specific moments that illustrate the emotional loss
‚óè‚Äã This is what they're actually buying for
‚óè‚Äã This answers Q#2: "What's in it for me?" (BOTH surface AND emotional)
After the pivot, everything serves the emotional desire:
‚óè‚Äã All proof should show emotional transformation, not just surface results
‚óè‚Äã Mechanism should explain how it enables emotional payoff
‚óè‚Äã Offer should emphasize emotional benefit

Pivot language examples:
‚óè‚Äã "But here's what nobody tells you about [surface experience]: You don't just lose
[obvious thing]. You lose [deep emotional thing]."
‚óè‚Äã "I thought it was about [surface experience]. It wasn't. It was about [deeper emotional
truth]."
‚óè‚Äã "[Surface experience] was manageable. What I couldn't handle was [emotional
consequence]."
‚óè‚Äã "For [timeframe], I told myself it was just about [surface experience]. But the real
problem was [emotional truth]."
The pivot must:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Happen by paragraph 7 LATEST (preferably paragraph 4-5)
Use sensory/physical details (not intellectual explanation)
Show what they're actually losing (not just inconvenienced by)
Make them FEEL it (chest tightness, throat lump, visceral recognition)
Reference specific relationship/life moments that are lost

IDENTIFICATION TYPE DETERMINES OPENING TONE

If Primal Fear: Open with the fear manifesting in daily life
If Primal Desire: Open with the desire unfulfilled
If Universal Experience: Open with the experience normalized
If Normalized Behavior: Open with the behavior without explanation
If Hidden Resentment: Open with the comparison/frustration

PARAGRAPHS 1-10: VALIDATE THE EXPERIENCE (Identification)
Open by establishing shared experience.
The reader must think: "That's exactly how I feel."
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

State the surface experience in their language
Show you feel/felt it too
Validate their current attribution ("I thought it was just...")
Show the emotional weight of this experience
DO NOT hint that anything is "wrong" yet ‚Äî just shared struggle

PARAGRAPHS 11-15: DRAMATIZE THE EMOTIONAL COST
Show what this experience is costing them.
Not the hidden problem yet ‚Äî just the weight of living with this experience.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Specific moment that captures the cost
What they're missing/losing
The private shame or frustration
Build the emotional stakes before the reveal

PARAGRAPHS 16-35: THE DISCOVERY (Gradualization into Awareness)
This is the CRITICAL section. Execute the gradualization sequence.

You are leading them from "I feel this but it's normal" ‚Üí "There's a hidden problem I didn't know
about."
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Contrast/doubt introduction (someone with different outcome)
Natural discovery setup (conversation, circumstance)
Authority introduces mechanism (UMP)
Make it verifiable
Personal application and verification
New awareness stated as discovery

PACING DISCIPLINE:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

DO NOT reveal the hidden problem before paragraph 25
Each micro-step = 3-5 paragraphs
Frame as investigation/discovery, not education
The reader should feel like they're discovering WITH you

FORBIDDEN patterns:

‚ùå "What I didn't know was..." ‚ùå "The truth is..." ‚ùå "Here's what's really happening..." ‚ùå
Jumping straight to mechanism without building doubt ‚ùå Lecturing or educating before earning
curiosity ‚ùå Stating the hidden problem before paragraph 25
REQUIRED patterns:
‚úì "But I noticed something..." (doubt) ‚úì "How are you not [experiencing this]?" (curiosity) ‚úì
"She looked at me and said..." (authority dialogue) ‚úì "I started tracking/checking/paying
attention..." (verification) ‚úì "That's when I realized..." (discovery)

MICRO-STEP EXECUTION GUIDE:
Micro-Step 1 (Validate Experience) - Paragraphs 1-5:
‚óè‚Äã State the surface experience
‚óè‚Äã Show you feel/felt it too
‚óè‚Äã Reader should think: "That's exactly how I feel"
Micro-Step 2 (Show Attribution) - Paragraphs 6-10:

‚óè‚Äã State what they believe explains it
‚óè‚Äã Validate that belief as reasonable
‚óè‚Äã Reader should think: "Yeah, that's what I thought too"
Micro-Step 3 (Introduce Contrast/Doubt) - Paragraphs 11-15:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Show something that doesn't fit the attribution
Someone else with same situation, different outcome
A small anomaly that creates curiosity
Reader should think: "Huh, that IS weird"

Micro-Step 4 (Deepen Doubt + Dramatize Cost) - Paragraphs 16-20:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Show the emotional cost of the experience
Make them feel the weight of it
Still no problem revealed ‚Äî just the pain
Reader should think: "This is really affecting my life"

Micro-Step 5 (Discovery Setup) - Paragraphs 21-24:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Natural circumstance leads to new information
Authority figure or credible source appears
Frame as curiosity/conversation, not education
Reader should think: "Maybe they know something"

Micro-Step 6 (Mechanism Reveal - UMP) - Paragraphs 25-30:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Authority explains the hidden problem
Make it verifiable ‚Äî they can check this themselves
The "holy shit" moment of awareness
Use authority dialogue to explain mechanism
Reader should think: "Holy shit, I didn't know that"

TIER-SPECIFIC EXECUTION:
If Tier 4 (Create Awareness of Hidden Problem):
‚óè‚Äã Authority reveals problem they didn't know existed
‚óè‚Äã Must make it feel urgent and real
‚óè‚Äã Immediately show how to verify it
If Tier 1 (Reframe Existing Observable):
‚óè‚Äã Show them what to look at in their own life right now
‚óè‚Äã Guide them to see something familiar differently
Micro-Step 7 (Personal Verification) - Paragraphs 31-33:

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Apply new information to your own situation
Show the verification moment
Include concrete details (tracking, checking, testing)
Reader should think: "Wait, that applies to ME too"

Micro-Step 8 (State New Awareness) - Paragraphs 34-35:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"That's when I realized..."
Frame as discovery, not pitch
Connect back to original experience
Reader should think: "Of course! That explains everything!"

SCHWARTZ'S TEST at each micro-step: "Could the reader reject this thought?"
‚óè‚Äã If YES ‚Üí add more setup, slow down, provide more proof
‚óè‚Äã If NO ‚Üí the step is small enough, continue to next step
By paragraph 35, the new awareness should feel like the reader's conclusion, not your pitch.

PARAGRAPHS 36-45: SECOND AUTHORITY VALIDATES + DEEPENS
Optional but powerful: Second authority (doctor, vet, expert) confirms the mechanism.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Seek professional validation
They confirm and deepen the explanation
Connect mechanism to the emotional cost
Remove self-blame ("It's not your fault / your age / your lifestyle")

PARAGRAPHS 46-55: SOLUTION MECHANISM (UMS)
Now introduce the solution through the same discovery frame.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Ask "what do I do?" / "how do I fix this?"
Authority or friend explains the solution mechanism
Make it simple and inevitable
Connect UMS directly to UMP

The logic must feel inevitable:
‚óè‚Äã Hidden problem = [UMP]
‚óè‚Äã Solution = [UMS]
‚óè‚Äã "Of course ‚Äî if THAT'S the problem, then THIS is the solution"

THE ENEMY SECTION (Q#5)
For Unaware, the enemy is usually NOT a competitor or failed solution. It's:
‚óè‚Äã The false attribution ("it's just your age")
‚óè‚Äã The hidden mechanism itself
‚óè‚Äã What kept them from knowing
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Acknowledge the false attribution
Show why it seemed reasonable
Reveal what was actually happening
Remove self-blame

URGENCY SECTION (Q#6)
Create urgency through CONSEQUENCE, not fake scarcity.
For Unaware, urgency comes from:
‚óè‚Äã The hidden damage that's accumulating
‚óè‚Äã The time/moments being lost
‚óè‚Äã The progressive nature of the problem
Pattern:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"I'm not trying to scare you, but..."
State the progressive nature of the hidden problem
Show window of opportunity
Emphasize time lost can't be recovered

PARAGRAPHS 56-65: TRANSFORMATION TIMELINE

Show the results with specific metrics and emotional transformation.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

First night / Day 1: Initial experience
Week 1: Observable pattern
Week 2-4: Consistent results
Month 1-3: Full transformation
Emotional milestone (the moment that captures the transformation)

Required:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Specific timeframes (not "soon" but "Day 3")
Observable changes (not "felt better" but specific result)
External validation (what others noticed)
Behaviors that stopped/started
Emotional shifts through actions

PARAGRAPHS 66-70: REFLECTION + BRIDGE TO READER
Reflect on what was lost, then bridge to the reader.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Acknowledge time/moments lost
Shift to direct address
Connect their experience to your discovery
Restate the hidden problem in simple terms

OFFER SECTION (Q#9)
Structure the offer simply for Unaware.
For Unaware, you've just revealed a problem they didn't know they had. The offer should feel
like natural next step, not hard sell.
Pattern:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Acknowledge the mechanism made sense
State the offer (price, included, guarantee)
Make it accessible
Remove risk

For higher-priced offers, use value stack:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

[Main Product] - $[Value]
[Bonus 1] - $[Value]
[Bonus 2] - $[Value]
Total Value: $[Total]
Your Price Today: $[Price]
Backed by [X]-Day Guarantee

CLOSING: Push-Pull Technique (Q#10)
CRITICAL: Your closing must be ORIGINAL.
FORBIDDEN patterns:

‚ùå Using identical phrases from reference ads ‚ùå "X months ago... Today..." (overused time
contrast structure) ‚ùå Future predictions about what they'll do ‚ùå Redemption clich√©s ‚ùå
Template feeling ‚Äî if you could swap 5 words for another product, you failed
ACCEPTABLE elements:
‚úì Direct address (vary the phrasing) ‚úì Present moment description (without formulaic
predictions) ‚úì Reflection on past self ‚úì Insight about what was really happening ‚úì Soft
product mention with trial/guarantee ‚úì Brief timeframe contrast (just don't make it the entire
structure)
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Give them control ("I can't force you...")
Present options (do nothing, try something else, get yours)
Be honest about what each option means
No fake scarcity, but real consequence
Soft invitation, not hard push

SCHWARTZ PRINCIPLES (Apply Throughout)
Principle 1: Extreme Specificity
Generic = sounds fake
Schwartz-level = sounds real

Required details:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Specific times (but natural ‚Äî see timestamp authenticity below)
Exact numbers ("fourth time," "three months")
Physical sensations ("tightness in chest," "heart racing")
Concrete actions ("staring at phone," "checking if breathing")
Direct internal dialogue

CRITICAL - TIMESTAMP AUTHENTICITY:

‚ùå Too specific (feels calculated):

‚óè‚Äã "11:34pm" (exact minute)
‚óè‚Äã "forty-seven minutes" (hyper-precise)
‚óè‚Äã "3:17am" (oddly specific)
‚úì Specific but natural:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"Late at night" or "After 11pm"
"Almost an hour" or "close to an hour"
"Middle of the night" or "early morning"
"Tuesday evening" (day + general time)

Rule: Use round numbers ("fifteen minutes," "half an hour") or general time windows.
Down-to-the-minute precision feels performative.

Principle 2: ABT (And-But-Therefore) Structure
Use for proof sections and story beats:
‚óè‚Äã AND: Set up background, create momentum
‚óè‚Äã BUT: Introduce conflict/tension (the critical element)
‚óè‚Äã THEREFORE: Present solution

Principle 3: Intensification
Make them feel the experience MORE before offering solution.
Three methods (use all three):
Verbalization ‚Äî Make them SAY internally what they feel
Multiplication ‚Äî Show it's happening MORE than realized

Dramatization ‚Äî Show future pain if unsolved

Principle 4: Gradualization
Lead them step-by-step into awareness. Each step is small and undeniable.
DO NOT jump from: "I feel X" ‚Üí "You have a hidden problem"
DO lead through:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Step 1: "I feel this too" (identification)
Step 2: "I thought it was normal" (validate attribution)
Step 3: "But something didn't fit" (doubt)
Step 4: "I learned something" (discovery)
Step 5: "I verified it myself" (proof)
Step 6: "Now I understand" (awareness)
Step 7: "Here's what fixed it" (solution)

Principle 5: Authority Validation (NOT Testimonials)
Authority figure INSIDE the story explains mechanism in plain language.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Authority enters naturally (friend, doctor, expert)
They explain WHY it works (mechanism)
In plain language
Reframes from "character flaw" to "solvable problem"

Principle 6: The Accidental Discovery
Never: "I searched for a solution and found this" Always: "I stumbled on this by accident..."
Pattern:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Unexpected circumstance (friend mentions something, overheard conversation)
Surprising result
Confusion (what's different?)
Investigation (aha moment)
Verification test (proves causation)

Principle 7: Transformation Timeline with Metrics
Vague (weak): "Things got better and I felt great"
Schwartz-style (strong): Specific timeframes, observable changes, external validation,
emotional shifts through actions.
Required:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Specific timeframes (not "soon" but "Day 3")
Observable changes (not "felt better" but measurable result)
External validation (what others noticed)
Behaviors that stopped/started
Emotional shifts through actions

FRAMEWORK DISCIPLINE (CRITICAL)
Do NOT add extra emotional beats between framework sections.
FORBIDDEN patterns:

‚ùå Establishing experience ‚Üí Attribution ‚Üí Experience AGAIN ‚Üí Discovery ‚úì Establishing
experience ‚Üí Attribution ‚Üí IMMEDIATELY to contrast/doubt
‚ùå Discovery ‚Üí Extra dramatization ‚Üí THEN mechanism ‚úì Discovery ‚Üí IMMEDIATELY to
mechanism

‚ùå Research at discovery ‚Üí Return home ‚Üí Research AGAIN ‚úì Research ONCE (either at
discovery OR after returning, not both)
‚ùå Story ‚Üí Break into criticism/blame ‚Üí Back to story ‚úì Stay IN story, make it personal
("nobody told me"), not systemic ("they know but don't tell you")

Rule: Each beat appears ONCE, maybe twice if critical. Three times = you're circling.

BACKSTORY WARNING (CRITICAL)
Backstory = telling how you got here Present story = what's happening now

Backstory KILLS momentum. Avoid it.
When backstory is acceptable: ‚úì In the opening (before main crisis starts) ‚úì As a single
sentence callback mid-story ‚úì When it's literally the discovery moment

‚ùå Middle of emotional beat ‚ùå Right after emotional peak ‚ùå
‚ùå More than 50 words

When backstory kills your ad:
To justify why something matters

RULE: If subject is universally understood (baby, parent, spouse) ‚Üí NO backstory needed. If
subject needs justification (pet, hobby, job) ‚Üí ONE line maximum, or cut it.

VOICE & TONE
Required voice:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

First person ONLY (I/me/my)
Confessional ‚Äî like telling a secret to a close friend
Vulnerable ‚Äî admitting you didn't know
Detailed ‚Äî extreme specificity signals truth
Conversational but serious
NEVER "marketery" or sales-y

Unaware voice is DIFFERENT from Problem-Aware:
‚óè‚Äã Problem-Aware: Educational mechanism revelation ("here's WHY your known problem
happens")
‚óè‚Äã Unaware: Discovery journey ("I didn't know I had a problem until...")
Sentence structure: Mix short and long for rhythm.
‚óè‚Äã Short for impact: "I froze."
‚óè‚Äã Long for immersion: Extended sentences with specific details that pull them into the
moment.
Paragraph length: Vary dramatically.
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Single sentences for gut punches
3-5 sentences for story flow
Occasional longer paragraphs for immersion
Use white space for emphasis

COPYRIGHT COMPLIANCE (CRITICAL)
HARD LIMITS - NEVER VIOLATE:

Limit 1: Quote Length
‚óè‚Äã 15+ words from any single source = SEVERE VIOLATION
‚óè‚Äã If quote would be longer, extract key phrase (under 15 words) OR paraphrase entirely

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

After quoting once, that source is CLOSED
All additional content must be fully paraphrased
2+ quotes from one source = SEVERE VIOLATION

Limit 3: Complete Works
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

NEVER reproduce song lyrics (not even one line)
NEVER reproduce poems (not even one stanza)
NEVER reproduce haikus
NEVER reproduce article paragraphs verbatim

Default to paraphrasing. Quotes should be rare exceptions.
Self-check before responding:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Have I quoted this source already? ‚Üí PARAPHRASE
Is this a complete creative work? ‚Üí DON'T REPRODUCE
Am I mirroring original phrasing? ‚Üí REWRITE ENTIRELY

FORBIDDEN (Will Kill The Ad)

‚ùå
Never do these things: ‚ùå Name the hidden problem in the headline ‚ùå Name the product
category in the headline ‚ùå Start revealing the problem in first 50% of copy ‚ùå Mention product
name in headline ‚ùå Use generic marketing language ‚ùå Break story frame into "sales mode"
‚ùå Use bullet points or feature lists in body copy ‚ùå Include testimonials (authority validation is
different) ‚ùå Create urgency through fake scarcity ‚ùå Make health claims without evidence ‚ùå

Never use these words:
Revolutionary, game-changing, amazing, incredible, breakthrough,
miracle, limited time, act now

Promise specific results as guarantees
Lecture instead of discover

‚ùå Jump to mechanism before building doubt ‚ùå

6 - Creating Angles

PROMPT #6: CREATING ANGLES
1. ATTACH YOUR RESEARCH DOCUMENTS:
I'm going to give you the research documents I've already created:
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Document: [ATTACH FILE]
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Language Document (if you have it): [ATTACH FILE]

2. TELL ME WHAT YOU'RE TARGETING:
Here's what I'm working with:
‚óè‚Äã Winning Mass Desire: [The desire that converted in testing]‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Example: "I want my dog to live longer"
‚óã‚Äã Example: "I want to save my marriage"
‚óã‚Äã Example: "I want to scale my business past $50K/month"‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã Product: [What you're selling]‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Joint supplement for dogs"
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Couples therapy program"
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Business coaching system"
The user has already done deep avatar research and desire testing. They found a desire that
converts. Now they need you to generate different angles to test against that desire to find
which belief-shift creates the highest conversion rates.
What's an angle?
An angle is the new information we give prospects about their problem that makes them finally
take action.
It makes them believe that our product is the only way to solve their desire, and it‚Äôs only possible
through our product‚Äôs unique mechanism.
It's not just describing their pain more dramatically. It's introducing new information that shifts
what they believe about their problem - making the solution suddenly feel necessary and urgent.
Example:
‚óè‚Äã Desire: "I want my cat to drink more water"

‚óè‚Äã Angle 1: "Plastic fountains create biofilm bacteria cats can smell but you can't - that's
why they avoid 'clean' fountains"
‚óè‚Äã Angle 2: "Your cat's 'laziness' is actually early dehydration you can't detect until kidney
damage is permanent"
‚óè‚Äã Angle 3: "Cats don't have a thirst drive - they have a prey-moisture detection system
that bowls don't trigger"
Same desire. Different new information. Different belief-shift.
Why generate multiple angles?
Because the user doesn't know which belief-shift will resonate strongest until they test. One
avatar might convert best when you explain why their previous solutions failed (Tier 2). Another
might convert when you reframe what they're already observing (Tier 1).
You will generate 5-7 different angles using a proven framework, so they can test
systematically instead of guessing.

THE 4 TYPES OF NEW INFORMATION (TIERS)
Not all new information is equally powerful. There's a hierarchy:

Tier 1 - Reframe What They're Already Seeing
They observe something every day but misunderstand what it means.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã "Your dog isn't slowing down from old age - they're in chronic pain you can't see"
‚óè‚Äã "You're not 'growing apart' - you're experiencing oxytocin withdrawal from reduced touch"
‚óè‚Äã "You're not bad at sales - you're losing deals in the 23-minute attention gap between
calls"
Why it works: They can verify it RIGHT NOW in their own life. No trust required first.

Tier 2 - Explain Why Their Attempts Failed
They tried things that didn't work, but don't know the real reason why.
Examples:

‚óè‚Äã "Front-clip harnesses trigger the opposition reflex - that's why training doesn't stop
pulling"
‚óè‚Äã "Date nights don't fix it because the problem isn't time together - it's that your bodies
forgot each other"
‚óè‚Äã "Hiring VAs didn't help because you have a delegation problem, not a capacity problem"
Why it works: Removes self-blame. Creates relief ("it wasn't me - the method was flawed").

Tier 3 - Reveal The Hidden Mechanism
They know the problem exists but don't understand what's actually causing it.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã "Joint pain isn't wear-and-tear - it's micro-inflammation from cytokine buildup"
‚óè‚Äã "Cash flow problems aren't about revenue - they're about the 45-day gap between ad
spend and LTV realization"
‚óè‚Äã "Intimacy loss isn't about attraction - it's cortisol making touch feel threatening instead of
comforting"
Why it works: Explains the "why" behind their struggle. Opens door to different solution
approach.

Tier 4 - Create Awareness of Hidden Problems
They don't even know this problem exists or is happening to them.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã "Your cat is in Stage 1 dehydration right now - 75% of kidney function is lost before
bloodwork shows it"
‚óè‚Äã "Sleeping separately for 6+ months rewires your nervous systems - 73% never return to
the same bed"
‚óè‚Äã "Every day without email segmentation costs 40% of potential revenue you can't
recover"
Why it works: Creates new urgency around invisible damage happening right now.

HOW YOU'LL DO THIS

You'll follow a 3-step process called Belief-Gap-Bridge Framework:
Step 1: Understanding Belief Structure You'll extract from the user's research documents
what their avatar currently believes about their problem, solutions, themselves, and timing.
Step 2: Finding The Gap You'll identify what the avatar DOESN'T know (using the 4 tiers) that
would change their behavior if they knew it.
Step 3: Building The Bridge You'll construct 5-7 complete angles that bridge the avatar's
current belief to the new information, creating the "aha moment" that drives action.
Each angle you generate will include:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

What they currently believe
The new information that contradicts it
How this reframes their understanding
What they'll naturally conclude
Why this belief-shift works for this specific avatar

Everything below this section is instructions for the LLM. DO NOT EDIT OR CHANGE
ANYTHING BELOW THIS LINE.

YOUR INSTRUCTIONS FOR GENERATING ANGLES
[Rest of the prompt continues exactly as before with all the Step 1, 2, 3 instructions, output
format, quality criteria, examples, etc.]

DEFINITION
An angle is the new information we give them about their problem that makes them
finally take action.

CONTEXT
The user has already:
1.‚Äã Completed deep avatar/market research (Macro Avatar Document)
2.‚Äã Conducted desire testing and identified a winning mass desire

Now you need to generate 5-7 different angles to test against that winning desire using Eno's
Belief-Gap-Bridge Framework. Each angle will introduce different new information that reframes
how the prospect sees their problem, making the solution suddenly feel necessary and urgent.

THE 4-TIER HIERARCHY OF NEW INFORMATION
Different types of new information have different power levels for shifting beliefs:
Tier 1 - Reframe Existing Observable Problem
They can see/experience the problem, but misunderstand what's really happening.
What it does: Changes interpretation of what they already observe
Examples across niches:
‚óè‚Äã Pet health: "Your dog isn't slowing down from old age - they're in chronic pain you can't
see"
‚óè‚Äã Relationships: "You're not 'growing apart' - you're experiencing oxytocin withdrawal from
reduced physical touch"
‚óè‚Äã Business: "You're not bad at sales - you're losing deals in the 23-minute attention
residue gap between calls"
Power: Highest for creating immediate belief shifts. They can verify in their own life right now.

Tier 2 - Explain Why Previous Solutions Failed
They've tried things that didn't work, but don't know the real reason why.
What it does: Validates their experience and removes self-blame
Examples across niches:
‚óè‚Äã Pet health: "Plastic fountains create biofilm within 48 hours that cats smell but you can't"
‚óè‚Äã Relationships: "Date nights don't fix it because the problem isn't time together - it's that
your nervous systems forgot each other"
‚óè‚Äã Business: "Hiring another VA didn't help because you have a delegation problem, not a
capacity problem"
Power: Very high. Explains pattern of failure. Creates relief ("it wasn't me").

Tier 3 - Introduce New Mechanism for Familiar Problem
They know the problem exists but don't understand the underlying cause/mechanism.
What it does: Reveals hidden cause they couldn't have known
Examples across niches:
‚óè‚Äã Pet health: "Joint pain isn't wear-and-tear - it's micro-inflammation from cytokine buildup"
‚óè‚Äã Relationships: "Intimacy loss isn't about attraction - it's about cortisol levels making touch
feel threatening instead of comforting"
‚óè‚Äã Business: "Cash flow problems aren't about revenue - they're about the 45-day gap
between ad spend and customer LTV realization"
Power: Good. Offers new path forward by revealing the real mechanism.

Tier 4 - Create Awareness of Problem They Didn't Know Existed
They're completely unaware something problematic is happening.
What it does: Introduces entirely new problem awareness
Examples across niches:
‚óè‚Äã Pet health: "Your cat is in chronic Stage 1 dehydration even with a fountain - 75% of
kidney function is lost before bloodwork shows it"
‚óè‚Äã Relationships: "Sleeping separately for 6+ months rewires your nervous systems to
sleep alone - 73% of couples never return to the same bed"
‚óè‚Äã Business: "Every day without email segmentation costs you 40% of potential revenue and you can't recover lost time"
Power: Creates new urgency by revealing invisible problems, but requires strong proof.

ENO'S BELIEF-GAP-BRIDGE FRAMEWORK
You will follow this exact 3-step process:

STEP 1: UNDERSTANDING THE BELIEF STRUCTURE
What do they currently believe about their problem?

Extract from the user's research documents and map out:

About The Problem:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

What do they think causes it?
How serious do they think it is?
What gives them permission to ignore it or delay action?
What do they observe that reinforces their current belief?

About Solutions:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

What do they think will solve it?
What have they already tried?
Why do they think previous attempts failed?
What solutions do they believe "should work" but haven't tried yet?

About Themselves:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Do they blame themselves for the problem?
Do they feel powerless to change it?
What identity beliefs are involved? ("I'm just not the type of person who...")
What excuses do they give themselves?

About Timing:
‚óè‚Äã Why haven't they acted yet?
‚óè‚Äã What makes "later" feel acceptable?
‚óè‚Äã What would need to change for them to act now?
Output this as a clearly organized belief map before moving to Step 2.

STEP 2: FINDING THE GAP
What don't they know that would change their behavior?
For each tier, identify if there's a gap between their current belief and reality:

Tier 1 Gap - Observable Problem Reframe:
Ask yourself:
‚óè‚Äã Is their explanation for what's happening wrong?

‚óè‚Äã Are they misinterpreting something they can see/experience right now?
‚óè‚Äã What are they observing but explaining incorrectly?
‚óè‚Äã Can they verify the real explanation immediately in their own life?
If YES to any ‚Üí Tier 1 gap identified

Tier 2 Gap - Failed Solutions:
Ask yourself:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Do they not know why their previous attempts actually failed?
Is there a technical/mechanical reason their "solutions" couldn't work?
Are they blaming themselves when the method itself was flawed?
Would knowing the real reason remove their guilt and create openness to new solutions?

If YES to any ‚Üí Tier 2 gap identified

Tier 3 Gap - Hidden Mechanism:
Ask yourself:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Are they missing the underlying cause or mechanism?
Is there a biological/technical/psychological process they don't understand?
Would knowing this mechanism completely change their approach?
Does this mechanism explain why nothing else has worked?

If YES to any ‚Üí Tier 3 gap identified

Tier 4 Gap - Unknown Problem:
Ask yourself:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Is there a problem occurring that they don't even know exists?
Are there invisible consequences happening right now?
Would awareness of this problem create immediate urgency?
Is this problem progressive (getting worse over time without intervention)?

If YES to any ‚Üí Tier 4 gap identified
List all identified gaps organized by tier. Each gap = one angle opportunity.

STEP 3: BUILDING THE BRIDGE

What new information connects their current belief to the solution?
For each gap you identified in Step 2, construct an angle using this formula:
[Current Belief] ‚Üí [New Information] ‚Üí [Reframe] ‚Üí [Natural Conclusion]

Construction Guidelines:
Current Belief:
‚óè‚Äã State it exactly as they would say it
‚óè‚Äã Use their language from the research documents
‚óè‚Äã Keep it specific to this desire
New Information:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Must be factual, verifiable, or backed by authority
Should be something they genuinely don't know
Must directly contradict or expand their current belief
Tier 1: Observable fact they can verify themselves
Tier 2: Technical reason their solution failed
Tier 3: Mechanism/process explanation
Tier 4: Problem existence + invisible consequences

The Reframe:
‚óè‚Äã How does this new information change their understanding?
‚óè‚Äã What does it make them realize about their situation?
‚óè‚Äã Frame as revelation, not accusation
Natural Conclusion:
‚óè‚Äã What will they logically conclude from this reframe?
‚óè‚Äã Should naturally point toward needing a solution like the user's product
‚óè‚Äã Should create urgency to act now vs. later

GENERATE 5-7 ANGLES
Ensure variety:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Use multiple different tiers (don't make all Tier 3)
Mix emotional impact levels
Include at least 1-2 immediately verifiable angles (Tier 1)
Include at least 1-2 that explain failed solutions (Tier 2)

‚óè‚Äã Each targets the winning mass desire from a different direction

OUTPUT FORMAT
For each angle, provide:
ANGLE [#]: [One-sentence contradictory statement]
Tier Level: [1, 2, 3, or 4]
Current Belief: [What they currently think - use their language]
New Information: [The specific insight/fact/mechanism you're introducing]
The Reframe: [How this changes their understanding]
Natural Conclusion: [What they'll logically conclude]
Observable Proof/Verification: [How they can verify this themselves OR what authority validates
it]
Why This Works: [2-3 sentences explaining the belief-shift psychology and why this angle is
powerful for this avatar]

QUALITY CRITERIA
Before finalizing each angle, test it against these criteria:

‚úÖ The Forehead Slap Test: Would they think "Of course! How did I not see this?"
‚úÖ The Permanence Test: Once they accept this, can they go back to their old belief?
‚úÖ The Urgency Test: Does this make inaction feel costly right now?
‚úÖ The Specificity Test: Is this concrete new information, not vague reframing?
‚úÖ The Natural Flow Test: Does this logically lead to needing a solution like the user's
product?

‚úÖ The Verification Test: Can they verify/observe this themselves, or is there strong authority
backing?

If an angle fails any test, revise or replace it.

CRITICAL RULES

‚ùå DO NOT:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Generate vague emotional appeals without concrete new information
Simply describe their problem more dramatically
Stack benefits or amplify pain points
Introduce the user's product mechanism (that comes later in copy)
Create false or misleading information
Make all angles the same tier
Ignore the avatar's actual language from the research documents
Use generic marketing language

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Extract actual beliefs from the research documents
Introduce genuinely new information from research/science/insider knowledge
Challenge existing beliefs with contradictory facts
Explain hidden mechanisms or causes
Reveal why previous solutions failed
Create "aha moments" through revelation, not persuasion
Use different tiers for variety
Ground every angle in the user's avatar research
Use the avatar's exact language and phrases

‚úÖ DO:

EXAMPLE OUTPUT
USER PROVIDED:
‚óè‚Äã Winning Mass Desire: "I want my cat to drink more water"
‚óè‚Äã Avatar: Avatar 2 (Hypervigilant owner, recent kidney scare, counts wet spots in litter box
daily)
‚óè‚Äã Product: Premium ceramic water fountain with prey-model electrolytes

STEP 1 OUTPUT - BELIEF STRUCTURE MAP:
About The Problem:

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

They think: "Cats naturally don't drink much water - it's just how they are"
They observe: Their cat ignoring water bowls, only drinking small amounts
Permission to delay: "I have a fountain and feed wet food, so I'm doing what I can"
Seriousness: Very worried (recent vet scare), but uncertain what more to do

About Solutions:
‚óè‚Äã They think will work: Better fountain, more wet food, multiple water stations
‚óè‚Äã Already tried: Plastic fountain ($40), switching to all wet food, moving bowls around
house
‚óè‚Äã Why they think it failed: "My cat is just picky" / "Maybe I need a better fountain"
‚óè‚Äã Haven't tried: Ceramic fountain, electrolyte additives (don't know these exist)
About Themselves:
‚óè‚Äã Self-blame: Yes - "I should have noticed sooner" / "I'm probably doing something wrong"
‚óè‚Äã Feel powerless: Somewhat - "I'm trying everything but nothing works"
‚óè‚Äã Identity: "Responsible pet owner who researches everything" / "Anxious about health"
About Timing:
‚óè‚Äã Why not acted: "I already have a fountain - feels wasteful to buy another"
‚óè‚Äã Makes later acceptable: "The vet said to monitor, so I'm monitoring"
‚óè‚Äã Would trigger action: Concrete evidence that current approach is insufficient

STEP 2 OUTPUT - GAP IDENTIFICATION:
Tier 1 Gaps Identified: ‚úì They observe their cat being "lazy" (sleeping 18+ hours) but interpret
it as normal cat behavior
‚óè‚Äã Gap: This is actually early dehydration symptom, not normal behavior
‚óè‚Äã Verifiable: They can observe behavior changes within 7 days of increased hydration
Tier 2 Gaps Identified: ‚úì They tried plastic fountain but cat still won't drink consistently
‚óè‚Äã Gap: Plastic creates biofilm bacteria cats can smell but humans can't
‚óè‚Äã Why they don't know: Fountain looks clean, so they blame cat's "pickiness"
‚úì They switched to all wet food but cat still got kidney concerns
‚óè‚Äã Gap: Wet food is only 78% moisture vs. 90% needed from prey
‚óè‚Äã Why they don't know: Vets say "wet food is better" without explaining the gap
Tier 3 Gaps Identified: ‚úì They think providing water access = solving hydration

‚óè‚Äã Gap: Cats don't have thirst drive - they have prey-moisture detection system
‚óè‚Äã Mechanism missing: Understanding of biological hydration triggers
Tier 4 Gaps Identified: ‚úì They think fountain + wet food = problem solved
‚óè‚Äã Gap: 60% of indoor cats in chronic Stage 1 dehydration even with these interventions
‚óè‚Äã Unknown problem: Damage happening invisibly before bloodwork shows it

STEP 3 OUTPUT - ANGLES CONSTRUCTED:

ANGLE 1: "Plastic fountains create biofilm bacteria within 48 hours that cats can smell,
which is why they avoid 'clean' fountains"
Tier Level: 2 (Explain Why Previous Solutions Failed)
Current Belief: "My cat is just picky about water" / "I keep the fountain clean but they still won't
drink much"
New Information: Plastic develops invisible biofilm bacterial colonies within 48 hours that cats'
superior sense of smell (14x more receptors than humans) detects, even after thorough
washing. The porous nature of plastic allows bacteria to colonize beneath the surface where
scrubbing can't reach. What appears clean to human senses triggers contamination warnings in
feline biology.
The Reframe: Your cat isn't picky - they're correctly avoiding contaminated water that you can't
detect but they can smell. The fountain IS clean by human standards, just not by feline biology
standards.
Natural Conclusion: I need a fountain material that doesn't harbor bacteria (ceramic/stainless
steel), not just better cleaning habits or a different plastic fountain.
Observable Proof/Verification: They can smell their plastic fountain after "cleaning" it - there's
often a persistent plastic/stale smell even when visually spotless. They can also test by
switching to ceramic for 3-5 days and observing drinking behavior changes. Veterinarians
confirm cats have 14x more scent receptors than humans (Cornell Feline Health Center).
Why This Works: Removes self-blame ("my cat is just difficult"), validates their experience
(fountain IS clean by their standards), and reveals a hidden technical problem with their current
solution they couldn't have known about. Creates urgency because continued use means
continued bacterial exposure. For Avatar 2 who already invested in a fountain and is frustrated
it's not working consistently, this explains why without making them feel stupid for the purchase it was the material choice, not their effort.

ANGLE 2: "Your cat's 'laziness' is actually early-stage dehydration you can't detect until
kidney damage is permanent"
Tier Level: 1 (Reframe Existing Observable Problem)
Current Belief: "My cat sleeps a lot but that's normal for cats" / "They're just naturally
calm/low-energy"
New Information: Cats naturally sleep 12-16 hours, but chronic dehydration causes lethargy
that looks identical to normal behavior. Early dehydration symptoms are: sleeping 17-20 hours,
less grooming frequency, decreased play drive, preferring to lie in one spot rather than move
around. These seem like "getting older" or "calm personality" but are actually dehydration
symptoms appearing 2-4 years before bloodwork shows kidney issues.
The Reframe: What you've been interpreting as "normal cat behavior" or "my cat's personality"
might actually be chronic dehydration symptoms you've been living with for months or years.
You're not observing a naturally lazy cat - you're observing early-stage dehydration happening in
slow motion.
Natural Conclusion: I need to verify if my cat's behavior is normal tiredness or dehydration
symptoms. If it's dehydration, I'm watching kidney damage accumulate without realizing it. I
can't wait for bloodwork to confirm - by then it's too late.
Observable Proof/Verification: They can observe their cat's behavior RIGHT NOW and
compare to the symptom list. They can also verify by increasing hydration (via any method) and
watching for behavior changes within 7-10 days - increased grooming, more play activity, more
movement around the house all indicate they were dehydrated. Their vet can confirm via urine
specific gravity test (measures hydration directly, unlike bloodwork which only shows damage
after it occurs).
Why This Works: Reframes something they observe every single day (cat sleeping, being
inactive) as potentially problematic instead of normal. Creates immediate urgency because they
can verify in their own home tonight - no waiting for vet appointments or test results. For Avatar
2's hypervigilance, this gives them something concrete to watch for and act on instead of
abstract future fear. Tier 1 angles are powerful because verification is immediate and doesn't
require trusting the claim first.

ANGLE 3: "Cats don't have a thirst drive - they have a prey-moisture detection system,
which is why bowls and fountains don't trigger proper hydration"
Tier Level: 3 (Introduce New Mechanism for Familiar Problem)

Current Belief: "Cats naturally don't drink much" / "If I provide enough water access, they'll
drink when thirsty"
New Information: Cats evolved as desert hunters getting 90% of hydration from prey
(mice/birds are 70% water). They lack the thirst mechanism that dogs and humans have instead they have a "prey moisture detection" system calibrated to recognize hydration through
specific biochemical markers: enzymatic water bound to tissue, blood, cellular fluid with natural
electrolytes. Their tongue structure, taste receptors, and cellular hydration triggers are designed
for moisture-rich food, not free-standing water. Moving water (fountains) is more interesting than
still water, but neither triggers the deep biological "hydration recognition" response that prey
moisture does.
The Reframe: Your cat isn't "not thirsty" - their biological hydration system doesn't recognize still
or moving water as a primary hydration source the way your body does. They're operating with
a completely different hydration mechanism that most cat owners don't know exists.
Natural Conclusion: I need to trigger their prey-moisture detection system, not just provide
water access or make water more appealing. Traditional fountains that just move water aren't
addressing the biological mechanism - I need something that mimics the biochemical markers
their biology is looking for.
Observable Proof/Verification: UC Davis School of Veterinary Medicine research on feline
hydration biology and evolutionary adaptation. They can also observe in nature/research: wild
cats almost never drink from water sources, getting nearly all moisture from prey. Indoor cats
fed dry kibble show chronic dehydration markers even with constant water access (Journal of
Feline Medicine and Surgery).
Why This Works: Explains the fundamental mechanism behind why cats don't drink from bowls
or fountains reliably, removing the "it's just natural for cats to not drink much" excuse for
inaction. Creates urgency around chronic dehydration as a biological mismatch between how
we provide water and how their bodies recognize hydration. For Avatar 2 whose hypervigilance
about kidney disease makes them deeply invested in understanding why their cat is at risk, this
mechanism provides the "why" that drives action.

ANGLE 4: "Wet food provides only 78% moisture when cats need 90% from prey - that
12% gap compounds daily into chronic dehydration"
Tier Level: 2 (Explain Why Previous Solutions Failed)
Current Belief: "I switched to all wet food, so hydration is covered" / "Wet food is enough - my
vet recommended it"
New Information: Premium wet food averages 78-82% moisture content, but cats in the wild
consuming whole prey get 90-95% of their hydration from food. That 12-17% moisture gap

seems small but compounds daily over months and years into cumulative dehydration stress on
the kidneys. Additionally, processed wet food loses "enzymatic water" (water bonded to living
tissue) that whole prey provides - only "free water" remains, which cats' biology recognizes and
processes differently. The missing enzymatic component means even the 78% moisture isn't
hydrating at the same cellular level as prey moisture.
The Reframe: Wet food is absolutely BETTER than dry kibble for hydration, but it's not enough
to fully protect against chronic dehydration and kidney disease. There's still a significant
moisture gap that accumulates as stress on the kidneys over time - you've reduced the risk, but
haven't eliminated it.
Natural Conclusion: Even with all wet food, I need additional hydration strategies to close the
gap. Wet food alone isn't the complete solution I thought it was when my vet recommended it.
Observable Proof/Verification: They can check any wet food label - even the most premium
brands max out at 82% moisture. Veterinary nutrition studies show cats need 90%+ moisture
intake for optimal kidney function (Tufts University Veterinary Nutrition). They can also observe:
cats on all-wet-food diets still get diagnosed with kidney disease at concerning rates - if wet food
alone was sufficient, this wouldn't happen.
Why This Works: Validates they were doing the "right thing" (wet food is genuinely better) while
explaining why it wasn't sufficient to eliminate their worry. Removes guilt ("I thought I was
protecting them") while creating urgency ("but I'm not doing enough"). For Avatar 2 who likely
already feeds exclusively wet food and is still anxious about kidney disease, this explains why
their precautions haven't eliminated the risk, creating openness to additional solutions without
making them abandon what they're already doing correctly.

ANGLE 5: "Your cat is in chronic Stage 1 dehydration right now even with a fountain 75% of kidney function is lost before bloodwork shows the damage"
Tier Level: 4 (Create Awareness of Problem They Didn't Know Existed)
Current Belief: "I have a fountain and feed wet food, so dehydration is prevented" / "If there
was a problem, the vet would detect it"
New Information: Veterinary research shows 60% of indoor cats exist in chronic Stage 1
dehydration (undetectable in standard bloodwork) even with constant water access and wet
food. Stage 1 dehydration is sub-clinical - not severe enough to show symptoms or affect
bloodwork, but severe enough to cause cumulative kidney damage over years. The critical
problem: by the time kidney issues appear in bloodwork (elevated creatinine/BUN), 75% of
kidney function is already permanently lost. Stage 1 dehydration is only detectable through urine
specific gravity testing, which most vets don't do unless symptoms already exist.

The Reframe: You're not preventing dehydration - you're just slowing it down compared to cats
on dry food with no fountain. The chronic low-level dehydration that causes kidney disease 8-12
years from now is happening invisibly right now, and your annual vet visits won't detect it until
it's too late to reverse.
Natural Conclusion: I need to actively solve dehydration at the Stage 1 level right now, not wait
for symptoms or bloodwork changes. The damage is happening in the background, and by the
time I can see it through traditional monitoring, most of the kidney function will be gone. I can't
rely on "wait and see."
Observable Proof/Verification: Journal of Feline Medicine and Surgery studies on subclinical
dehydration prevalence in indoor cats. They can request a urine specific gravity test from their
vet (most indoor cats on fountains still show concentrated urine indicating chronic mild
dehydration). The International Renal Interest Society (IRIS) staging system confirms 75%
kidney loss before bloodwork elevation.
Why This Works: Creates awareness of a hidden problem (Stage 1 dehydration) that's
happening right now, not in the future. Introduces urgency through the "75% loss before
detection" statistic - waiting for confirmation means waiting until it's too late. For Avatar 2 who
already had a scare and is hypervigilant, this validates that their fear isn't overreacting - there IS
an invisible threat they can't see through normal monitoring. Shifts them from "preventing
something that might happen" to "stopping something that's happening right now in Stage 1."

ANGLE 6: "The 'just get them to drink more water' advice fails because cats
physiologically cannot compensate for moisture-depleted diets through drinking alone"
Tier Level: 3 (Introduce New Mechanism for Familiar Problem)
Current Belief: "If I can just get my cat to drink more water, the kibble/dry food won't matter as
much"
New Information: Cats' kidneys evolved to concentrate urine heavily (desert ancestry
adaptation) because their biology expected 90% of hydration to come integrated with food
(prey). When fed moisture-depleted food (dry kibble = 10% moisture, even wet food = 78%),
their kidneys work overtime to concentrate urine while attempting to maintain hydration. The
critical mechanism most owners don't know: even if they drink additional water to compensate,
they cannot fully make up the deficit because the biological process expects moisture integrated
with nutrients (as in whole prey), not separated (dry food THEN water in a separate bowl). The
kidney metabolic load from processing 10-20% moisture food + processing separate water
intake creates cumulative stress their desert-adapted kidneys weren't designed to handle
long-term.
The Reframe: This isn't about the volume of water consumed - it's about a fundamental
biological mismatch between how cats evolved to hydrate (integrated moisture + nutrients in

prey) versus how we feed them (separated dry food + water). Increasing water intake helps, but
can't fully solve the metabolic stress from the separation.
Natural Conclusion: The problem isn't just "not enough water volume" - it's the fundamental
delivery method of how moisture enters their system. I need to change how moisture is
delivered in relation to nutrients, not just increase the amount they drink.
Observable Proof/Verification: Feline nutrition research from veterinary university studies on
kidney metabolic load and hydration mechanisms. They can observe a frustrating pattern: cats
who drink "enough" water volume (vet-approved amounts) still develop kidney disease at similar
rates to cats who drink less - if volume alone was the solution, this pattern wouldn't exist.
Why This Works: Challenges the standard vet advice ("just get them to drink more") which
Avatar 2 has almost certainly heard and tried to follow without eliminating their worry. Explains
WHY following that advice hasn't eliminated their fear or risk. Introduces a mechanism
(integrated vs. separated hydration) that opens the door for a fundamentally different solution
approach. Creates urgency by revealing their current strategy (increasing water consumption)
won't fully protect against kidney disease - they need to change the approach, not just increase
the effort.

ANGLE 7: "Water fountains can't solve dehydration because they only change delivery
method, not what cats' biology recognizes as hydration"
Tier Level: 2 (Explain Why Previous Solutions Failed)
Current Belief: "Moving water attracts cats, so a fountain should solve the drinking problem"
New Information: Fountains make water more appealing visually and auditorily (movement,
bubbling sounds) but don't address the core biological mismatch - cats' cellular hydration
triggers evolved for prey moisture which contains specific biochemical markers: enzymatic water
bound to tissue, natural electrolytes, blood proteins, cellular fluid. These markers tell their
biology "this is proper hydration." Free-standing water, whether moving or still, lacks these
markers. Moving water is more interesting than still water (novelty, mimics stream sounds), but
neither triggers the deep biological "hydration recognition" response at the cellular level that
prey moisture does. Result: cats drink more from fountains than bowls, but the water isn't
processed as "complete hydration" by their physiology.
The Reframe: Fountains make cats MORE LIKELY to drink (which is good), but don't make the
water BIOLOGICALLY RECOGNIZED as proper cellular hydration (which is the real problem).
You're increasing volume consumed without triggering the proper physiological hydration
response their desert-adapted bodies are looking for.

Natural Conclusion: I need something that mimics prey-model hydration at a biochemical level
(what their cells recognize), not just makes water more attractive to drink (what their eyes/ears
respond to).
Observable Proof/Verification: Studies on feline hydration physiology show cats with fountains
consume more water volume but still show dehydration markers - concentrated urine, elevated
kidney values over time (UC Davis). They can observe a frustrating pattern: their cat drinks from
the fountain regularly but still has symptoms like dry gums, skin tenting, concentrated dark urine
indicating the water isn't hydrating at the cellular level.
Why This Works: Explains why fountains help (increased consumption) but don't fully solve the
problem (biological recognition), validating their investment in a fountain while opening the door
for additional solutions that address the biochemical component. For Avatar 2 who likely already
has a fountain and is still worried about kidney disease, this explains why their worry is justified they're doing a good thing that's incomplete, not a wrong thing. Creates urgency without guilt:
the fountain is necessary but not sufficient on its own.

FINAL STEP: PRESENT TO USER
"I've generated 7 angles for you to test against your winning desire: 'I want my cat to drink more
water' for Avatar 2.
Breakdown by tier:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Tier 1 (Observable Reframe): 1 angle
Tier 2 (Failed Solutions): 3 angles
Tier 3 (New Mechanism): 2 angles
Tier 4 (Unknown Problem): 1 angle

Which angle resonates most with what you know about this avatar?
OR
Would you like me to explain which angles I'd prioritize testing first based on this
avatar's psychology (hypervigilant, recent scare, already tried fountain + wet food)?"

END OF EXAMPLE OUTPUT

NOW GENERATE ANGLES FOR THE USER'S INPUTS

Follow the exact same process:
1.‚Äã Extract and map belief structure from their documents
2.‚Äã Identify gaps across all 4 tiers
3.‚Äã Build 5-7 angles with complete formatting
Then present the angles to the user and offer to help prioritize testing order.

7.1 - Story Lead

PROMPT #7.1: PROBLEM-AWARE AD
COPY GENERATOR (STORY LEAD)
1. UPLOAD YOUR RESEARCH DOCUMENTS:
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Document: [ATTACH FILE]
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Language Document: [ATTACH FILE]
‚óè‚Äã Mass Desire Research Document: [ATTACH FILE]

2. SPECIFY YOUR TARGET:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Mass Desire You're Targeting: [INSERT MASS DESIRE]
Macro-Avatar You‚Äôre Targeting (Optional): [INSERT AVATAR # OR NAME]
Product Name: [YOUR PRODUCT]
What's Included: [Main product + included items]
Guarantee: [Terms - days, conditions, refund policy]
Bonuses (if any): [List with values]

3. YOUR ANGLE:
Current Belief: [What they currently believe that keeps them stuck]

Resulting Belief-Shift: [What they now realize/conclude]
I'm going to give you everything you need to write Problem-Aware ad copy using the Story Lead
approach.
Here's what's happening:
You've done your research. You know your avatar, their problem, and what desire you're
targeting. Now you need ad copy that converts.
This prompt is flexible - you can use it for:
‚óè‚Äã Testing angles - Write multiple versions with different belief-shifts to see which converts
best
‚óè‚Äã Testing desires - Same angle, different underlying desires
‚óè‚Äã Testing stories - Same angle and desire, but told from different real customer
experiences

‚óè‚Äã Scaling winners - Create variations of what's already working
What's Problem-Aware?
Problem-Aware prospects already know they have a problem. They recognize it immediately
when you mention it.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Cat owner knows their cat doesn't drink enough water
Dog owner knows their dog pulls on walks
Business owner knows their cash flow is inconsistent
Couple knows they're growing apart

They've maybe tried casual things on their own (moved the water bowl, tried a different leash,
adjusted pricing, scheduled date nights). But they haven't invested in formal solutions yet.
What's the Story Lead approach?
Story Lead = "I had this exact problem too. Here's what I discovered about WHY it happens.
Once I understood the mechanism, the solution became obvious."
It's educational and mechanism-focused. You're teaching them why the problem exists and why
the solution works.
What's an angle?
Your angle is the new information that shifts their belief about the problem, making your solution
suddenly feel necessary.
It's made of three parts:
1.‚Äã Current Belief - What they think now that keeps them stuck
2.‚Äã New Information - The specific insight that contradicts that belief
3.‚Äã Resulting Belief-Shift - What they realize after learning the new info
Examples across niches:
‚óè‚Äã Pet: Current belief: "Some cats just don't drink much" ‚Üí New info: "Cats have
prey-moisture detection, not thirst drives" ‚Üí Belief-shift: "Plain water doesn't register as
hydration to their biology"
‚óè‚Äã Business: Current belief: "I need more traffic to grow" ‚Üí New info: "Traffic without
conversion optimization burns money" ‚Üí Belief-shift: "My funnel is leaking before traffic
even matters"
‚óè‚Äã Relationships: Current belief: "We just grew apart naturally" ‚Üí New info: "Oxytocin
production stops after 6 months of reduced physical touch" ‚Üí Belief-shift: "Our bodies
literally forgot each other - this is biological, not emotional"

What I'm going to do:
1.‚Äã Extract your inputs (avatar, desire, angle, product)
2.‚Äã Generate 10-15 headline options
3.‚Äã You pick your favorite headline
4.‚Äã I write complete ad copy (2,000-2,500 words) using your angle
The copy follows Schwartz's Problem-Aware framework: Name the need ‚Üí Dramatize the need
‚Üí Reveal the mechanism ‚Üí Present product as inevitable solution.

Everything below this section is instructions for the LLM. DO NOT EDIT OR CHANGE
ANYTHING BELOW THIS LINE.

‚óè‚Äã Gradualization approach: Guide them to check/observe in their own life
‚óè‚Äã Examples: "Check your cat's water bowl", "Look at your conversion rate", "Notice when

If new information explains why their attempts failed:

‚óè‚Äã Examples: "Plastic develops biofilm bacteria", "Front-clip harnesses trigger opposition

‚óè‚Äã Gradualization approach: Introduce through authority/research

sleeping separately never return to same bed", "Average funnel loses 40% to cart
abandonment"

SCHWARTZ STAGE 4 (PROBLEM-AWARE) PATTERNS:
From Breakthrough Advertising - headlines that name the need:
Pattern 1: Name Problem Only
‚óè‚Äã "[Problem]?"
‚óè‚Äã Examples: "Corns?", "Cash Flow Problems?", "Dog Pulling On Walks?"
Pattern 2: Problem + Solution
‚óè‚Äã "[Solves Problem]"
‚óè‚Äã Examples: "Stops Maddening Itch", "Ends Constant Pulling", "Stabilizes Monthly
Revenue"
Pattern 3: Problem + Solution + Benefit
‚óè‚Äã "[Solves Problem] Without [Usual Limitation]"
‚óè‚Äã Examples: "Shrinks Hemorrhoids Without Surgery", "Stops Pulling Without Prong
Collars", "Grows Revenue Without Paid Ads"
Pattern 4: Promise Removal of Previously Unconquerable Limitation
‚óè‚Äã "Lets [Thing] Do [Previously Impossible Thing]"
‚óè‚Äã Examples: "Lets Portable Transistor Radios Play On Ordinary Household Current", "Lets
Reactive Dogs Walk Calmly Past Triggers"
Pattern 5: Substitute For Unpleasant/Expensive Task
‚óè‚Äã "Now! [Complex Task] In [Simple Form]"
‚óè‚Äã Examples: "Now! A Ring And Piston Job In A Tube!", "Now! Professional Copy In 30
Minutes!"

PART 3: CLAUDE PROCESSING INSTRUCTIONS
Claude, before writing the ad copy, you must:
Step 1: Build The One Belief
The One Belief Formula: "[PROBLEM] is caused by [NEW INFORMATION FROM ANGLE] [YOUR PRODUCT] solves it through [SOLUTION MECHANISM] - giving you [LEVEL 2
EMOTIONAL DESIRE]"
Extract:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Problem: What they recognize (from avatar doc)
Mechanism Behind Problem: The new information from the angle
Solution: The product name
Mechanism Behind Solution: How the product works (from product info)
Emotional Desire: Level 2 from avatar doc (the deep emotional drive)

Step 2: Engineer The Gradualization Sequence
Using the angle's belief shift, map out 5-7 micro-steps that lead from current belief ‚Üí new
information ‚Üí resulting belief-shift.
DO NOT allow the copy to state the new belief directly. Break it into discoverable micro-steps.
REQUIRED: Map out 5-7 micro-steps:
Micro-Step 1: Introduce Small Doubt
‚óè‚Äã What tiny anomaly challenges their current belief?

‚óè‚Äã Frame as confusion, not revelation
‚óè‚Äã Make it concrete with real details
Micro-Step 2: Show Pattern
‚óè‚Äã The anomaly isn't isolated‚Äîit happens to others
‚óè‚Äã Build from "weird coincidence" to "consistent pattern"
Micro-Step 3: Create Question
‚óè‚Äã What question does this pattern raise?
‚óè‚Äã Show you investigating, not knowing the answer yet
Micro-Step 4: Introduce New Information
‚óè‚Äã Present the new information from the angle
‚óè‚Äã Can they observe/verify themselves? Use authority?
‚óè‚Äã Make it undeniable
Micro-Step 5: Connect to Their Situation
‚óè‚Äã How does new information apply to their specific case?
‚óè‚Äã Show the verification moment
Micro-Step 6: Show Gap
‚óè‚Äã What's the difference between what they thought and what's true?
‚óè‚Äã Quantify if possible
Micro-Step 7: State New Belief as Discovery
‚óè‚Äã Frame as realization, not claim
‚óè‚Äã "That's when I realized [resulting belief-shift from angle]"
Each micro-step will become 3-5 paragraphs in the actual copy. Total pacing: Spend paragraphs
11-30 walking through these steps.
CRITICAL: The reader should feel like they're discovering this WITH you, not being sold TO.
Step 3: Map To Problem-Aware Structure
Schwartz's Problem-Aware Framework:
Step 1: Name The Need
‚óè‚Äã Headline names the problem
‚óè‚Äã Opening establishes "I had this too"

Step 2: Dramatize The Need
‚óè‚Äã Show how bad the problem is
‚óè‚Äã Emotional cost, what they can't do
‚óè‚Äã Make them realize how badly they need a solution
Step 3: Reveal The Mechanism + Deliver Belief-Shift
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Explain WHY the problem happens (the new information)
Show why casual attempts failed
Deliver the belief-shift from the angle
Blame the mechanism, not them

Step 4: Present Product As Inevitable Solution
‚óè‚Äã Show how your mechanism solves their mechanism
‚óè‚Äã Proof of transformation
‚óè‚Äã Make solution feel inevitable

PART 4: WRITING INSTRUCTIONS
CORE STRUCTURE: The Mechanism Revelation
Your copy must reveal WHY the problem happens (using the angle's new information), then
present the product as the inevitable solution.

‚óè‚Äã Make solution feel inevitable

‚óè‚Äã They already recognize the problem (no need to build awareness)

‚óè‚Äã

Structure:

From Great Leads - Story Lead for Problem-Aware:

casual attempts they relate to

Level 1 (surface problem)

‚ùå Stay at
