THE MECHANISM REVELATION SECTION (WITH GRADUALIZATION)
GRADUALIZATION EXECUTION (Paragraphs 11-30 - CRITICAL)
You mapped the gradualization sequence in Step 2. Now execute it in the copy.
Paragraphs 11-30: Walk through your 5-7 micro-steps
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Each micro-step = 3-5 paragraphs
Use ABT structure within each step (And-But-Therefore)
Frame as investigation/discovery, not claims
Include physical details, dialogue, specific moments
Use their actual language from the avatar doc

PACING DISCIPLINE:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

DO NOT state the new belief before paragraph 28
If you're explaining the full mechanism before paragraph 25, you're going too fast
Each step must feel like natural progression of thought
The doubt must build gradually across 15-20 paragraphs

‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

FORBIDDEN patterns in paragraphs 11-30:
"I was wrong about [old belief]"
"The truth
is [new belief]"
"Here's what I discovered: [new belief]"
Jumping from doubt ‚Üí new belief
in under 10 paragraphs
Stating the mechanism before building sufficient doubt

‚ùå

REQUIRED patterns: ‚úì "But I noticed something weird..." (doubt) ‚úì "That didn't make
sense..." (confusion) ‚úì "So I started investigating..." (curiosity) ‚úì "Found a study that stopped
me cold..." (new information) ‚úì "I checked my own [situation]..." (personal application) ‚úì
"That's when it clicked..." (realization)

MICRO-STEP EXECUTION GUIDE:
Micro-Step 1 (Introduce Small Doubt) - Paragraphs 11-13:
Share a specific anomaly that challenges their current belief.
Examples across niches:
‚óè‚Äã Pet: Friend's cat on wet food still got kidney disease
‚óè‚Äã Business: Store doing $500K/year with only 1,000 email subscribers

‚óè‚Äã Relationship: Couple doing weekly date nights still divorced
‚óè‚Äã Fitness: Person eating 1,200 calories/day couldn't lose weight
Make it concrete with real details. Reader should think "Huh, that IS weird."

‚ùå

‚ùå

DON'T:
Use vague generalizations
Criticize their old belief directly

‚ùå Make it sound made up ‚ùå Jump to explaining it yet

DO: ‚úì Use specific person/situation ‚úì Include concrete details ‚úì Frame as genuine confusion
‚úì Make it relatable to their experience

Micro-Step 2 (Show Pattern) - Paragraphs 14-16:
Show this isn't isolated‚Äîhappened to others too.
Build from "weird coincidence" to "consistent pattern."
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Pet: "Started asking around. Lot of cats on wet food still get kidney disease."
‚óè‚Äã Business: "Checked other successful stores. Same pattern - low email lists, high
revenue."
‚óè‚Äã Relationship: "Looked at divorced couples I knew. Most had been doing 'all the right
things.'"
Reader should think: "Why does this keep happening?"

‚ùå

DON'T:
Explain the pattern yet
Make it feel like you already know

‚ùå Introduce research/studies yet ‚ùå State conclusions ‚ùå

DO: ‚úì Show multiple examples ‚úì Build curiosity ‚úì Frame as ongoing confusion ‚úì Use their
actual observations

Micro-Step 3 (Create Question) - Paragraphs 17-19:
Articulate the question the pattern raises.
Show you investigating, not knowing the answer yet.
Include failed attempts to explain it with old belief.
Examples:

‚óè‚Äã Pet: "If wet food prevents kidney disease, why are so many cats on wet food still getting
sick?"
‚óè‚Äã Business: "If email list size matters, how are stores with tiny lists outperforming huge
ones?"
‚óè‚Äã Relationship: "If quality time together is the answer, why do couples doing that still split
up?"
Reader should think: "Yeah, what DOES explain this?"

‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

DON'T:
Answer the question yet
Introduce your angle yet
completely wrong
Make it rhetorical

‚ùå Suggest they were

DO: ‚úì Frame as genuine question ‚úì Show failed attempts to explain ‚úì Build tension ‚úì Make
reader want the answer

Micro-Step 4 (Introduce New Information) - Paragraphs 20-23:
Present the new information from the angle.
This is the CRITICAL moment. Handle it carefully.
If the new information is observable/verifiable:
Guide them to check something themselves:
‚óè‚Äã Pet: "Check your cat's water bowl right now. How much is gone?"
‚óè‚Äã Business: "Look at your conversion rate. How many visitors actually buy?"
‚óè‚Äã Relationship: "Think about the last time you had extended physical touch. Not sex. Just
sustained contact."
Make it something they can verify in 60 seconds = belief shift is instant and sticky.
If the new information is mechanism/research-based:
Use authority dialogue to explain:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Present research/study finding
Use authority figure to explain mechanism
Connect to their pattern of experience
Show why this explains what they've been seeing

‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

DON'T:
State it as fact without proof
Make it complicated or jargon-heavy
implications yet
Say "this is the answer" directly

‚ùå Jump to

DO: ‚úì Make it verifiable or authority-backed ‚úì Keep it simple and clear ‚úì Frame as discovery
‚úì Let it sink in (one insight per paragraph)
Reader should think: "That would explain it..." OR "Holy shit, I can see that right now"

Micro-Step 5 (Connect to Their Situation) - Paragraphs 24-26:
Apply new information to your specific case.
Show the verification moment.
Include concrete details (checking labels, doing math, testing, observing).
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Pet: "Checked my cat's wet food label: 42% moisture. Even premium brands: 45%. Cats
need 70%+."
‚óè‚Äã Business: "Ran the numbers. My conversion rate: 0.8%. Industry average: 2-3%. The
leak wasn't traffic‚Äîit was conversion."
‚óè‚Äã Relationship: "Realized we hadn't had sustained physical touch‚Äîjust quick hugs,
passing kisses‚Äîin over 4 months."
Reader should think: "Wait, that applies to ME too"
DON'T:

‚ùå Keep it general ‚ùå Skip the verification ‚ùå Move too fast ‚ùå Introduce solution yet

DO: ‚úì Show specific checking/testing ‚úì Include numbers if relevant ‚úì Make it personal and
real ‚úì Let them connect dots themselves

Micro-Step 6 (Show Gap) - Paragraphs 27-29:
Reveal the difference between assumption and reality.
Quantify the gap if possible.
Explain why old belief seemed right but wasn't complete.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Pet: "Wet food IS better than dry. But it still leaves a 25-30% moisture gap."
‚óè‚Äã Business: "More traffic IS helpful. But without fixing conversion, I was just burning
money faster."

‚óè‚Äã Relationship: "Date nights ARE important. But without physical touch, oxytocin
production stops‚Äîdates can't fix that."
Show why their casual attempts failed (connect to what they tried in opening).
Reader should think: "Oh my god, I didn't realize..."

‚ùå

DON'T:
Make them feel stupid
Overcomplicate it

‚ùå Criticize what they tried ‚ùå Make it their fault ‚ùå

DO: ‚úì Validate what they tried was smart ‚úì Show the mechanism that limited it ‚úì Quantify the
gap ‚úì Remove self-blame

Micro-Step 7 (State New Belief as Discovery) - Paragraph 30:
"That's when I realized [resulting belief-shift from angle]"
Frame as shared discovery.
Connect back to their original problem/fear from opening.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Pet: "That's when I realized: wet food alone isn't enough. The moisture gap is still there."
‚óè‚Äã Business: "That's when I realized: I didn't have a traffic problem. I had a conversion
problem."
‚óè‚Äã Relationship: "That's when I realized: we hadn't grown apart emotionally. Our bodies
had literally forgotten each other."
Reader should think: "Of course! That's why nothing worked!"

‚ùå

‚ùå

DON'T:
Say "I was right" or "you were wrong"
product yet
Rush past this moment

‚ùå Make it sound like conclusion ‚ùå Introduce

DO: ‚úì Frame as "aha" moment ‚úì Use language from angle ‚úì Connect to original fear ‚úì Let
it breathe
SCHWARTZ'S TEST at each micro-step: "Could the reader reject this thought?"
‚óè‚Äã If YES ‚Üí add more setup, slow down, provide more proof
‚óè‚Äã If NO ‚Üí the step is small enough, continue to next step
By paragraph 30, the new belief should feel like the reader's conclusion, not your pitch. The next
sections (mechanism, offer) simply provide the solution to the belief they now hold.

THE DISCOVERY & SOLUTION SECTION
Paragraphs 31-43: THE DISCOVERY STORY
Pattern for Problem-Aware Discovery:
1.‚Äã Someone mentions the solution exists
2.‚Äã Immediate disbelief/curiosity
3.‚Äã They explain the mechanism
4.‚Äã Realization: "That explains everything"

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

They explain in plain language
It clicks - connects to the new information
You test/investigate further

‚ùå

sound too convenient
Skip the skepticism

SCHWARTZ PRINCIPLES (Apply Throughout)
Principle 1: Name The Need Clearly
‚óè‚Äã Be direct about the problem they recognize
‚óè‚Äã Don't dance around it
‚óè‚Äã Make them think "Yes, that's exactly my problem"
Principle 2: Dramatize The Need Vividly
‚óè‚Äã Show emotional cost from avatar doc
‚óè‚Äã Show what they can't do/enjoy
‚óè‚Äã Make them FEEL how bad this problem is
Principle 3: Blame The Mechanism, Not Them
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

The problem isn't their fault
The mechanism (the new information) is to blame
"You didn't know about [new information]"
Removes guilt, builds trust

Principle 4: Present Product As Inevitable Solution
‚óè‚Äã Once mechanism is understood, solution is obvious
‚óè‚Äã Don't "sell" it, make it feel inevitable

‚óè‚Äã "Of course - [resulting belief-shift]. That's what [product] provides."
Principle 5: Deliver The Belief-Shift
‚óè‚Äã Use the angle to structure the revelation
‚óè‚Äã Make it feel like a natural discovery, not a sales pitch
‚óè‚Äã Reader convinces themselves

Use specific, concrete details. Not vague "felt better."

‚óè‚Äã Pet: Specific drinking frequency, coat changes, vet bloodwork results

7.2 - Secret Lead

PROMPT #7.2: PROBLEM-AWARE AD
COPY GENERATOR (SECRET LEAD)
1. UPLOAD YOUR RESEARCH DOCUMENTS:
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Document: [ATTACH FILE]
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Language Document: [ATTACH FILE]
‚óè‚Äã Mass Desire Research Document: [ATTACH FILE]

2. SPECIFY YOUR TARGET:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Mass Desire You're Targeting: [INSERT MASS DESIRE]
Macro-Avatar You‚Äôre Targeting (Optional): [INSERT AVATAR # OR NAME]
Product Name: [YOUR PRODUCT]
What's Included: [Main product + included items]
Guarantee: [Terms - days, conditions, refund policy]
Bonuses (if any): [List with values]
Price + Offer: [Listing price + Discount]

3. YOUR ANGLE:
Current Belief: [What they currently believe that keeps them stuck]
New Information: [The specific insight/fact that contradicts their belief]
Resulting Belief-Shift: [What they now realize/conclude]

I'm going to give you everything you need to write Problem-Aware ad copy using the Secret
Lead approach.
Here's what's happening:
You've done your research. You know your avatar, their problem, and what desire you're
targeting. You've tested angles and found one that works. Now you need ad copy that converts
using the power of exclusivity and insider knowledge.
This prompt is flexible - you can use it for:
‚óè‚Äã Testing secret packaging - Write multiple versions with different neologisms/exclusivity
angles

‚óè‚Äã Testing desires - Same secret, different underlying desires
‚óè‚Äã Testing stories - Same secret, told from different insider perspectives
‚óè‚Äã Scaling winners - Create variations of what's already working
What's Problem-Aware?
Problem-Aware prospects already know they have a problem. They recognize it immediately
when you mention it.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Cat owner knows their cat doesn't drink enough water
Person knows they get razor burn from shaving
Business owner knows their cash flow is inconsistent
Someone knows their skin looks dull and aged

They've maybe tried casual things on their own (moved the water bowl, tried different razors,
adjusted pricing, used different creams). But they haven't invested in formal solutions yet.
What's the Secret Lead approach?
Secret Lead = "There's this exclusive method/system/formula that only [small group] knows
about. Here's why it's been hidden. Here's why you haven't heard of it. And here's how to
access it."
It's mysterious and exclusive. You're teasing insider knowledge they can't get anywhere else.
When to use Secret Lead vs. Story Lead:
Use SECRET LEAD when:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

The solution can be positioned as exclusive/hard-to-come-by
There's a credible reason it's been hidden or suppressed
A small group has been using this successfully
The "secret" creates intrigue and desire
Industry suppression or commercial interests explain why it's unknown

Use STORY LEAD when:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

The solution is more about mechanism understanding
There's no exclusivity angle
It's educational rather than mysterious
The appeal is "finally understanding why"

What I'm going to do:
1.‚Äã Extract your angle and research

2.‚Äã Generate 3-5 "secret packaging" options - different ways to frame your angle as
exclusive insider knowledge
3.‚Äã You pick which secret framing you like best
4.‚Äã Generate 10-15 headline options based on your chosen secret
5.‚Äã You pick your favorite headline
6.‚Äã I write complete ad copy (2,200-2,800 words) using the velvet pouch technique
The copy follows Masterson's Secret Lead framework: Tease the secret ‚Üí Build exclusivity ‚Üí
Show what they've missed ‚Üí Offer access to the secret.

Everything below this section is instructions for the LLM. DO NOT EDIT OR CHANGE

Step 2: Engineer The Velvet Pouch Sequence

Secret Leads use a DIFFERENT type of gradualization than Story Leads. You're not gradually
revealing information‚Äîyou're gradually building DESIRE through withholding.
This is the "Velvet Pouch" technique from Masterson: like a salesman massaging expensive
items through velvet without revealing them, you create tension by talking AROUND the secret
without fully disclosing it.
REQUIRED: Map out 6-8 micro-steps that build desire through teasing:
Micro-Step 1: Name The Secret (Neologism)
‚óè‚Äã Introduce the proprietary term
‚óè‚Äã Don't explain what it is yet
‚óè‚Äã Just plant the seed: "What IS this?"
Micro-Step 2: Hint At Who Knows
‚óè‚Äã Reveal the exclusive small group
‚óè‚Äã Make it feel inaccessible
‚óè‚Äã Create "insider knowledge" feeling
Micro-Step 3: Tease The Benefit (Without Revealing How)
‚óè‚Äã Show the massive result
‚óè‚Äã Don't explain the mechanism yet
‚óè‚Äã Make them want to know more
Micro-Step 4: Explain Why It's Secret
‚óè‚Äã Reveal why they haven't heard of it
‚óè‚Äã Industry suppression, exclusivity, scarcity
‚óè‚Äã Make the secrecy credible
Micro-Step 5: Massage Without Revealing (Velvet Pouch)
‚óè‚Äã Talk AROUND how it works
‚óè‚Äã Give hints but withhold details
‚óè‚Äã Build more curiosity
Micro-Step 6: Show Proof Without Teaching
‚óè‚Äã Demonstrate it works through results
‚óè‚Äã Don't explain HOW it works
‚óè‚Äã Transformation timelines, before/after
Micro-Step 7: Increase Urgency For Access

‚óè‚Äã Make them want the secret NOW
‚óè‚Äã Exclusivity might close
‚óè‚Äã Limited availability
Micro-Step 8: Offer Access (Secret Revealed After Purchase)
‚óè‚Äã How they get the secret
‚óè‚Äã What's included (overview only)
‚óè‚Äã Full reveal happens inside product
Each micro-step will become 4-6 paragraphs in the actual copy. Total pacing: Spend paragraphs
11-50 building desire through teasing.
CRITICAL: The reader should feel desperate to know the secret, not satisfied by your
explanation. Withhold, tease, hint‚Äînever fully reveal until they purchase.
Step 3: Map To Secret Lead Structure
Masterson's Secret Lead Framework:
Step 1: Tease The Secret
‚óè‚Äã Name the secret (neologism)
‚óè‚Äã Hint at massive benefit
‚óè‚Äã Create curiosity
Step 2: Build Exclusivity
‚óè‚Äã Who knows this?
‚óè‚Äã Why is it secret?
‚óè‚Äã Why haven't they heard about it?
Step 3: Show What They've Missed
‚óè‚Äã Cost of not knowing
‚óè‚Äã What they've tried that didn't work
‚óè‚Äã The velvet pouch - massage the secret without revealing
Step 4: Offer Access To The Secret
‚óè‚Äã How they get access
‚óè‚Äã What they'll receive
‚óè‚Äã The secret is revealed after purchase

MASTERSON PRINCIPLES (Apply Throughout)
Principle 1: The Neologism Creates Curiosity
Invent a name for the secret that sounds:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Proprietary
Exclusive
Professional
Intriguing

Make them ask: "What IS that?"
Principle 2: The Velvet Pouch - Massage Without Revealing
Talk AROUND the secret without explaining it:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"It's based on five specific metrics..."
"When all five align in a specific pattern..."
"That's your signal..."
"Then you execute the sequence..."

They know it exists. They know it works. They don't know HOW.
That's the tension.
Principle 3: Exclusivity Builds Desire
The fewer people who know, the more valuable it seems:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"Only 47 people knew this"
"Kept private for 15 years"
"Can't work if everyone knows"
"Limited release"

Principle 4: Show Proof Without Teaching
Results prove it works. But don't explain how:
‚óè‚Äã Show transformations
‚óè‚Äã Show timeline
‚óè‚Äã Show multiple examples

‚óè‚Äã Still withhold the actual system
Principle 5: The Secret Is Revealed After Purchase
From Masterson: "Usually, you get the best mileage when ordering the product reveals the
secret"
The full system, exact steps, complete formula - that's ONLY in the product.
This creates the final push to buy.

SCHWARTZ PRINCIPLES (Adapted for Secret Lead)
Principle 1: Name The Need (But Make It Mysterious)
‚óè‚Äã They recognize the problem
‚óè‚Äã But the solution is secret/exclusive
‚óè‚Äã Creates double intrigue
Principle 2: Dramatize What They're Missing
‚óè‚Äã Not just the problem they have
‚óè‚Äã But what they've missed by not knowing the secret
‚óè‚Äã "15 years of [problem] because you didn't know this existed"
Principle 3: Blame The Suppression
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Not their fault
The secret was hidden from them
Industry/authority suppressed it
Removes guilt, creates anger at gatekeepers

Principle 4: Make Access Feel Like Privilege
‚óè‚Äã Not "buying a product"
‚óè‚Äã "Getting access to secret knowledge"
‚óè‚Äã "Joining the exclusive group who knows"

OFFER SECTION
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã Acknowledge they want to know the secret

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"By now you're probably asking: how do I access [neologism]?"
Explain what they get (overview of included materials, not full reveal)
State the offer (price, guarantee)
Emphasize the secret is inside
"The complete [neologism] - all exact details, steps, criteria - is revealed inside [product]"

Example structure:
"By now you're probably asking: how do I access [neologism]?

Here's how:

[Product name and brief description]

Inside, you'll get:
- [Main component]
- [Complete technique guide - overview only]
- [Support materials]
- [Bonuses]

Everything you need to [achieve result using the secret method].

[Justify price if needed]

[Guarantee details - remove all risk]

Why [price point]?
[Brief explanation]

The catch?
[Real scarcity/urgency]

[CTA]"

‚ùå

‚ùå

DON'T:
Reveal the complete secret here
Use fake urgency
Oversell

‚ùå Make it sound expensive or complicated ‚ùå

DO: ‚úì Make access clear ‚úì Overview what's included (not details) ‚úì Emphasize guarantee
‚úì Remind them secret is revealed inside ‚úì Use real scarcity if applicable

CLOSING: The Secret Awaits
Secret Lead closing emphasizes: "The secret is within reach - but you have to take action to
discover it"
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Remind them of exclusivity
The secret cannot be shared here
Full reveal is inside the product
No pressure, just access
The choice is yours

Example structure:

[Reason - manufacturer won't allow it, would be compromised if public, etc.]

So the complete [details] - [specific components they want to know] - is only revealed with
[product].

Option 1: Keep doing what you've been doing.
[Current state continues]

Get [product]. Learn the technique. [Achieve result].

$[price]. [Guarantee]. [Product lifespan/value].

If it doesn't work - if [specific failure condition] - you get your money back.

If it does work - if [specific success condition] - you'll have access to something most people
don't even know exists.

The choice is yours.

[CTA]

[Signature]

P.S. ‚Äî [Reinforce guarantee and/or urgency]"

DON'T:

‚ùå Pressure them ‚ùå Use fake urgency ‚ùå Make them feel guilty ‚ùå Reveal the secret

DO: ‚úì Give them control ‚úì Restate what they learned ‚úì Make access feel exclusive ‚úì
Emphasize risk-free guarantee ‚úì Remind them of the secret inside

7.3 - Proclamation Lead

PROMPT #7.3: PROBLEM-AWARE AD
COPY GENERATOR (PROCLAMMATION
LEAD)
1. UPLOAD YOUR RESEARCH DOCUMENTS:
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Document: [ATTACH FILE]

‚óè‚Äã Example: "The $2.4 Billion Razor Industry Is Built On A Lie: You Don't Need Blades To

‚óè‚Äã Example: "87% Of Women Are Using A Hair Removal Method That Causes Chronic

The Research/Proof (CRITICAL - MUST BE REAL):

‚öÝÔ∏è ALL RESEARCH MUST BE VERIFIABLE
‚óè‚Äã

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Do NOT make up study names, journal names, or statistics
If citing research, provide: Journal name, year, authors, title, DOI or link
If citing lawsuits, provide: Case name, filing date, court, case number
Making up studies/research is fraud and potentially illegal (FTC violations)
Better to have NO studies than FAKE studies

[Paste your real, verifiable research here - studies, lawsuits, industry reports, etc.]

I'm going to give you everything you need to write Problem-Aware ad copy using the
Proclamation Lead approach.
Here's what's happening:
You've done your research. You know your avatar, their problem, and what desire you're
targeting. You've found (or can make) a bold, shocking claim about their problem that's backed
by research or verifiable facts. Now you need ad copy that converts using the power of
truth-telling and righteous revelation.
This prompt is flexible - you can use it for:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Testing proclamations - Write multiple versions with different bold claims
Testing desires - Same proclamation, different underlying desires
Testing proof angles - Same claim, different research emphasis
Scaling winners - Create variations of what's already working

What's Problem-Aware?
Problem-Aware prospects already know they have a problem. They recognize it immediately
when you mention it.

Examples:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Cat owner knows their cat doesn't drink enough water
Person knows they get razor burn from shaving
Business owner knows their cash flow is inconsistent
Someone knows their skin looks dull and aged

They've maybe tried casual things on their own (moved the water bowl, tried different razors,
adjusted pricing, used different creams). But they haven't invested in formal solutions yet.
What's the Proclamation Lead approach?
Proclamation Lead = "Here's a bold, shocking truth backed by research. Here's the proof. Here's
why it matters to you. Here's the solution."
It's truth-revealing and research-backed. You're exposing industry lies or hidden dangers they
didn't know about.
When to use Proclamation Lead vs. Story Lead vs. Secret Lead:
Use PROCLAMATION LEAD when:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

You have (or can make) a bold, shocking claim
You have verifiable research/data to back it up
There's an industry scandal, lawsuit, or suppression angle
The claim creates righteous anger or urgency
You can position yourself as truth-teller vs. industry liar

Use STORY LEAD when:
‚óè‚Äã The solution is about mechanism understanding
‚óè‚Äã No bold/shocking angle exists
‚óè‚Äã It's educational rather than revelatory
Use SECRET LEAD when:
‚óè‚Äã Focus is on exclusivity and insider knowledge
‚óè‚Äã The appeal is "only few people know this"
‚óè‚Äã Mystery matters more than proof
What's an angle?
Your angle is the new information that shifts their belief about the problem, making your solution
suddenly feel necessary.
It's made of three parts:

1.‚Äã Current Belief - What they think now that keeps them stuck
2.‚Äã New Information - The specific insight that contradicts that belief
3.‚Äã Resulting Belief-Shift - What they realize after learning the new info
For Proclamation Leads, the angle often becomes the foundation for the bold statement.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Beauty: Current belief: "I just have sensitive skin" ‚Üí New info: "Blade razors cause 34%
barrier function loss over 5 years (study-backed)" ‚Üí Belief-shift: "My 'sensitive skin' is
actually industry-caused damage"
‚óè‚Äã Pet: Current belief: "Some cats don't drink much water" ‚Üí New info: "60% of indoor cats
exist in chronic Stage 1 dehydration that's invisible until kidney damage is permanent
(vet research)" ‚Üí Belief-shift: "My cat's 'laziness' is actually dehydration I can't detect"
What I'm going to do:
1.‚Äã Extract your inputs (avatar, desire, angle, proclamation, research)
2.‚Äã Generate 10-15 headline options based on your proclamation
3.‚Äã You pick your favorite headline
4.‚Äã I write complete ad copy (2,000-2,500 words) using proof-stacking technique
The copy follows Masterson's Proclamation Lead framework: Make bold statement ‚Üí Prove it
with research ‚Üí Show why they should care ‚Üí Deliver the solution.

PART 3: CLAUDE PROCESSING INSTRUCTIONS
Claude, before writing the ad copy, you must:
Step 1: Build The Proclamation Structure
The Proclamation Formula: "[BOLD STATEMENT] (proclamation) - here's the research that
proves it (proof) - here's why you should care (relevance) - here's what this means for you
(implicit promise) - here's the solution (product)"
Extract:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Problem: What they recognize (from avatar doc)
The Proclamation: The bold statement
The Proof: The research/data (ONLY if verifiable)
Relevance: Why they should care personally
Implicit Promise: What solution this implies
Product Connection: How your product delivers on the promise
Emotional Desire: Level 2 from avatar doc

Step 2: Engineer The Proof-Stacking Sequence
Proclamation Leads use a DIFFERENT type of gradualization than Story Leads. You're not
gradually revealing new information about a mechanism‚Äîyou're gradually PROVING a bold
claim by dismantling skepticism piece by piece.
From Masterson: "The Proclamation Lead must prove the bold statement gradually. You can't
just state it and dump all the proof at once. The reader's skepticism must be dismantled piece
by piece."
REQUIRED: Map out 6-8 micro-steps that build credibility through proof-stacking:
Micro-Step 1: Make The Bold Statement
‚óè‚Äã State the proclamation clearly
‚óè‚Äã Arrest attention immediately

‚óè‚Äã Create disbelief + curiosity
Micro-Step 2: Address Initial Skepticism
‚óè‚Äã Acknowledge their doubt
‚óè‚Äã "I know what you're thinking..."
‚óè‚Äã Show you understand their disbelief
Micro-Step 3: Present First Proof
‚óè‚Äã Introduce the first study/data point (if real)
‚óè‚Äã Make it credible (journal, date, specifics)
‚óè‚Äã Don't overwhelm yet‚Äîjust plant the seed
Micro-Step 4: Show Pattern With Second Proof
‚óè‚Äã Add another data point
‚óè‚Äã Build from "interesting" to "pattern"
‚óè‚Äã Different source, same conclusion
Micro-Step 5: Add Authority Validation
‚óè‚Äã Industry/authority response
‚óè‚Äã Shows the claim is credible
‚óè‚Äã Even skeptics are responding
Micro-Step 6: Make It Personally Relevant
‚óè‚Äã Connect to their daily experience
‚óè‚Äã Show what this means for THEM
‚óè‚Äã Remove self-blame
Micro-Step 7: Show What They're Losing
‚óè‚Äã Cost of continuing
‚óè‚Äã Quantify the damage
‚óè‚Äã Create urgency
Micro-Step 8: State The Solution As Obvious
‚óè‚Äã Given the proclamation is true...
‚óè‚Äã The solution becomes inevitable
‚óè‚Äã Connect product to proclamation
Each micro-step will become 3-5 paragraphs in the actual copy. Total pacing: Spend paragraphs
11-30 building proof gradually.

CRITICAL: Each piece of proof must reduce skepticism incrementally. By paragraph 30, the
proclamation should feel undeniable.
Step 3: Map To Proclamation Lead Structure
Masterson's Proclamation Lead Framework:
Step 1: Make The Bold Statement
‚óè‚Äã Headline proclaims something shocking
‚óè‚Äã Opening reinforces the proclamation
‚óè‚Äã Arrests attention immediately
Step 2: Prove The Proclamation
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Show research/data
Demonstrate validity
Build credibility
Address skepticism

Step 3: Show Why They Should Care
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Make it personally relevant
Show what's at stake
Create urgency
Connect to their frustration

Step 4: Deliver The Implicit Promise
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Reveal the solution exists
Connect product to proclamation
Make solution feel inevitable
Offer access

Your copy must make a shocking statement, prove it with research, show why it matters, and
deliver the solution.
Paragraphs 1-10: MAKE THE BOLD STATEMENT
‚óè‚Äã Open with the proclamation
‚óè‚Äã Arrest attention immediately

‚óè‚Äã Create disbelief + curiosity
‚óè‚Äã "Is this even possible?"

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Authority responses

Before/After
The offer

Proclamation: 2-3 variations/reinforcements
Proof: 4-6 specific data points (if available)
Relevance: 3-5 personal connections
Solution: Clear, direct connection

HEADLINE: The Bold Statement
From Masterson: "They must not only grab attention, but also stir up thoughts and excite
emotions"
Use the headline selected from Part 2.
CRITICAL: The headline must be bold enough to arrest attention but credible enough to make
them read on.

OPENING: The Proclamation
Structure:
Paragraphs 1-3: STATE THE PROCLAMATION BOLDLY
From Great Leads - Proclamation Lead:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Opens with emotionally-compelling statement
Bold, not reasonable
Startling, intriguing, tempting
Immediately arrests attention

Opening structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

State the proclamation directly
Make it shocking
Provide initial credibility marker (study, lawsuit, statistic)
Create "is this even true?" reaction
Promise to show proof
"Let me show you the research..."

‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå Make it

DON'T:
Soften the claim
Start with a personal story (that's Story Lead)
mysterious (that's Secret Lead)
State it without any initial credibility marker

DO: ‚úì Lead with the bold statement ‚úì Make it shocking enough to arrest attention ‚úì Include
one credibility marker immediately ‚úì Promise to prove it ‚úì Create "I need to know if this is
true" urgency
Paragraphs 4-10: REINFORCE THE PROCLAMATION
Don't back down. Strengthen the claim:
Structure:

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"I know what you're thinking..."
Address obvious skepticism
Provide another credibility marker
Show you're serious
Build more intrigue
Still don't dump all proof yet

Examples across niches:
‚óè‚Äã Beauty: "I know‚Äîif blades were that damaging, dermatologists would have warned us.
Except some did. And they're being sued for it."
‚óè‚Äã Pet: "I know‚Äîif this were true, every vet would mention it. Except the research has been
published for decades. In journals most vets never read."
‚óè‚Äã Business: "I know‚Äîif platforms were hiding this, someone would have exposed it.
Except they did. In a leaked internal memo from 2019."

‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

DON'T:
Back down from the claim
Dump all research at once
conspiracy theory
Skip addressing skepticism

‚ùå Make it sound like

DO: ‚úì Double down on the bold claim ‚úì Tease more proof to come ‚úì Acknowledge and
deflect doubt ‚úì Build credibility gradually

THE PROOF-STACKING SECTION (Paragraphs 11-30 - CRITICAL)
You mapped the proof-stacking sequence in Step 2. Now execute it in the copy.
This is NOT like Story Lead (revealing mechanism) or Secret Lead (building desire). You're
dismantling skepticism through credible proof, piece by piece.
PACING DISCIPLINE FOR PROCLAMATION LEADS:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå

DO NOT dump all research at once
Build credibility gradually
Each proof point must reduce skepticism incrementally
Address doubt explicitly before adding more proof
By paragraph 30, the proclamation should feel undeniable

‚ùå
‚ùå
‚ùå

‚ùå

FORBIDDEN patterns in Proclamation Lead:
Stating the proclamation without any proof
Dumping 5 studies in one paragraph
Not addressing skepticism directly
Making it feel
like conspiracy theory
Softening the bold claim
Using fake or unverifiable research

‚ùå

REQUIRED patterns: ‚úì "I know what you're thinking..." (address skepticism) ‚úì "Here's the
data..." (present proof) ‚úì "When this study was published..." (show response) ‚úì "This explains
why..." (connect to their experience) ‚úì "Let me be very specific..." (add detail for credibility)

MICRO-STEP EXECUTION GUIDE FOR PROCLAMATION LEAD:
Micro-Step 1 (Make Bold Statement) - Already done in opening
Micro-Step 2 (Address Initial Skepticism) - Paragraphs 11-13:
Acknowledge their doubt explicitly.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"I know what you're thinking..."
State their obvious objection
Show you understand why they're skeptical
Promise to prove it

Reader should think: "Yeah, this DOES sound unbelievable"
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã "I know what you're thinking: 'If this were true, everyone would know.'"
‚óè‚Äã "Your first reaction is probably: 'That can't be right.'"
‚óè‚Äã "I understand the skepticism. This contradicts everything you've been told."

‚ùå

DON'T:
Ignore their skepticism
them feel stupid for doubting

‚ùå Act offended they doubt you ‚ùå Skip this step ‚ùå Make

DO: ‚úì Name their doubt directly ‚úì Validate their skepticism ‚úì Promise to prove it with
evidence ‚úì Show you're not asking for blind faith

Micro-Step 3 (Present First Proof) - Paragraphs 14-17:
Introduce the first credible piece of evidence.
IF YOU HAVE REAL, VERIFIABLE RESEARCH:
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Introduce the study/lawsuit/data
Full citation (journal, year, authors, sample size)
What it measured
What it found
Keep it clear and specific

Example:
"Let me be very specific about what the research shows.

Journal of Dermatological Science (2022):
- 5-year longitudinal study
- 2,400 women who shave regularly
- Measured transepidermal water loss (TEWL) - gold standard for barrier function

Results:
Year 1: 7% increase in TEWL
Year 3: 19% increase
Year 5: 34% increase

The more you shave, the weaker your barrier becomes."

IF YOU DON'T HAVE REAL RESEARCH:
Use observable patterns and logical arguments:
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

State the observable pattern
Explain the mechanism logically
Use their own experience as proof
Make it undeniable through logic

Example:
"Think about your own experience:

When you first started shaving at 15, did you get razor burn?

Probably not as badly as you do now.

By 20: Occasional irritation
By 25: More frequent bumps
By 30: Almost every time

Your skin isn't getting more sensitive.
Your barrier is getting more damaged.

Every shave creates thousands of microscopic cuts.
Each cut triggers inflammation.
Before it fully heals, you shave again.

Over years, this prevents full recovery.
The damage accumulates.

That's not theory.
That's the pattern you can observe in your own skin."

Reader should think: "Okay, that's credible evidence" OR "That's exactly what happened to me"

‚ùå Make up studies ‚ùå Cite fake research ‚ùå Use vague "research shows" without
‚ùå Make it too complicated

DON'T:
specifics

DO: ‚úì Use real research with full citations OR ‚úì Use logical arguments based on observable
patterns ‚úì Keep it clear and specific ‚úì Make it verifiable or relatable

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Different source/methodology
Same conclusion

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Micro-Step 5 (Add Authority Validation) - Paragraphs 22-25:
Show how industry/authority figures have responded.

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

What actions did they take?
What does this prove?

Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Company reformulated product after study
‚óè‚Äã Industry issued statement (notice what they didn't deny)
‚óè‚Äã Lawsuit was filed

‚óè‚Äã Guidelines were updated
‚óè‚Äã Advertising changed
IF YOU DON'T HAVE AUTHORITY RESPONSE:
Use industry behavior as evidence:
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã What does industry practice reveal?
‚óè‚Äã What economic incentive explains their silence?
‚óè‚Äã What would they lose if people knew?
Example:
"Notice what the razor industry does:

They spend $280 million annually advertising 'sensitive skin' razors.
They create dozens of 'gentle' blade variants.
They add soothing strips and lubricating bands.

But they never‚Äînot once‚Äîsuggest a blade-free alternative.

Why?

Because their business model depends on blade refills.
$2.4 billion annually from replacement blades.

If you knew you didn't need blades at all?
That revenue disappears."

DON'T:
theories

‚ùå Make up lawsuits or statements ‚ùå Invent industry responses ‚ùå Create conspiracy

DO: ‚úì Use observable industry behavior ‚úì Point out what they DON'T say ‚úì Explain
economic incentives ‚úì Make suppression credible

Micro-Step 6 (Make It Personally Relevant) - Paragraphs 26-29:
Connect the proclamation to their daily experience.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"This explains why..."
Connect to their specific frustration
Show what this means for THEM
Remove self-blame
Quantify their personal exposure

Reader should think: "Oh my god, that's what's happening to ME"
Examples across niches:
‚óè‚Äã Beauty: "This explains why your razor burn gets WORSE over time. You thought you
were doing something wrong. You weren't. It's cumulative damage."
‚óè‚Äã Pet: "This explains why your cat sleeps more each year. You thought it was age. It's
dehydration."
‚óè‚Äã Business: "This explains why more traffic didn't help. You thought you needed better
targeting. You needed better conversion."
Include quantification:
"Think about it:

If you've been shaving twice a week for 10 years:
- 1,040 shaving sessions
- 1,040 instances of micro-trauma
- 1,040 opportunities for barrier damage

Is it any wonder your skin is more 'sensitive' now than at 20?

It's not sensitivity.
It's damage."

‚ùå

DON'T:
Stay theoretical
vague language

‚ùå Skip the personal connection ‚ùå Make them feel guilty ‚ùå Use

DO: ‚úì Use their language from avatar doc ‚úì Quantify their exposure ‚úì Remove self-blame ‚úì
Make it about their specific situation

Micro-Step 7 (Show What They're Losing) - Paragraph 30:
Quantify the cost of continuing.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Time lost to this problem
Money spent on ineffective solutions
Emotional toll
What opportunities they've missed
Cumulative damage

Reader should think: "I need to stop this NOW"
Example:
"Add it up:

15 years of razor burn = 780 episodes of irritation
780 times choosing pants over skirts
780 times avoiding intimacy after shaving
780 times feeling like your body failed you

$15/month on razor blades √ó 180 months = $2,700
On a method that's been damaging your skin the entire time

And every month you continue?
More damage.
More barrier weakening.
More permanent change.

The question isn't 'why didn't I know this sooner?'
The question is: 'how much longer will I keep doing it?'"

‚ùå

Use fake urgency
emotional cost

‚ùå Make it feel manipulative ‚ùå Exaggerate ‚ùå Skip the

Micro-Step 8 (State Solution As Obvious) - Will come in next section
SCHWARTZ'S TEST at each micro-step: "Could the reader still doubt this claim?"
‚óè‚Äã If YES ‚Üí add more proof, be more specific
‚óè‚Äã If NO ‚Üí the proof is sufficient, move to relevance
By paragraph 30, the proclamation should feel undeniable.

THE SOLUTION SECTION (Paragraphs 31-50)
Paragraphs 31-43: CONNECT PRODUCT TO PROCLAMATION
Show how your product addresses the proclamation directly.
Pattern:
‚óè‚Äã "So if [proclamation is true], what's the solution?"

‚óè‚Äã Introduce your product
‚óè‚Äã Show it delivers on the implicit promise
‚óè‚Äã Make solution feel inevitable given the proclamation
Structure:
"So if [problem from proclamation], what's the alternative?

[Introduce product and method]

[Explain how it addresses the root cause]

[Connect to the research/proof]

Given what you now know about [proclamation], this isn't just an 'alternative.'
It's the obvious choice."

‚ùå
‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

DON'T:
Introduce product without connecting to proclamation
Make it feel like a sales
pitch
Skip the inevitable logic
Forget the research connection
DO: ‚úì Make solution feel like logical conclusion ‚úì Reference the proclamation proof ‚úì Show
how product delivers on implicit promise ‚úì Keep focus on solving the revealed problem

Paragraphs 44-50: TRANSFORMATION & PROOF
Show what happens when someone learns the truth and uses the solution.

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Month 3: Full transformation

‚ùå

‚ùå

DON'T:
Use vague "felt better"
Make results unbelievable
Forget the "spreading truth" element

‚ùå Skip the emotional arc ‚ùå

DO: ‚úì Include both physical and emotional changes ‚úì Show validation and vindication ‚úì Use
concrete metrics ‚úì End with them sharing the truth

MASTERSON PRINCIPLES (Apply Throughout)
Principle 1: Make It Bold, Not Reasonable
From Masterson: "To be effective, Proclamation Leads must be big and bold"
‚óè‚Äã Don't soften the statement
‚óè‚Äã Don't hedge with qualifiers
‚óè‚Äã "The industry is built on a lie" not "Some think it might not be ideal"
Principle 2: Prove It With Research
From Masterson: "Great Proclamation Leads almost always come from research"
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Specific studies with dates OR
Observable patterns with logic
Named institutions OR
Verifiable industry behavior
Not invented - found or deduced

Principle 3: Make It Relevant
From Masterson: "The subject must be relevant"
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Connect to their daily experience
Show personal stakes
Remove self-blame
Create urgency

Principle 4: Deliver The Implicit Promise

From Masterson: "All leads must make or imply a promise"
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Proclamation reveals problem
Implies solution exists
Product delivers the solution
Make connection explicit

Principle 5: Return To Proclamation In Close
From Masterson: "Underscore the main theme"
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Restate the bold claim
Show how product addresses it
Connect guarantee to it
"If the proclamation is true (and it is), then this solution is obvious"

OFFER SECTION
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Acknowledge the proclamation changed everything
"Now that you know [proclamation]..."
State the offer (price, included, guarantee)
Connect guarantee to proclamation
"This is the [solution] that addresses [proclamation]"

Example:
"Now that you know the truth about [proclamation], the choice is obvious.

Stop [old method].
Get [product].

$[price] for the [solution].

Includes:
- [Main component]

- [Support materials]
- [Truth report if relevant]

[X]-day money-back guarantee: If [specific failure related to proclamation], full refund.

This isn't [old inferior approach].
This is [solution that addresses proclamation].

Given what you now know, can you really go back to [old method]?

[CTA]"

CLOSING: Return To The Proclamation
Proclamation closing emphasizes: "The bold statement was true - here's your solution"
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Restate the proclamation
Summarize the proof
Make the choice obvious
Connect guarantee to proclamation
Give them control

Example:
"[Restate proclamation].

[It's backed by research / observable pattern].

The research is clear / The pattern is undeniable:
- [Key proof point 1]
- [Key proof point 2]
- [Key proof point 3]

This isn't [what they thought].
This is [what it actually is].

You have two choices:

Option 1: Keep [old method].
[Consequences continue]
[Industry profits from your ignorance]

Option 2: [Switch to solution].
[Problem solved]
[Freedom from the lie]

$[price]. [X]-day guarantee.

If [proclamation-related failure], full refund.

The proclamation is true.
[Research/pattern] is undeniable.

The solution exists.

The choice is yours.

[CTA]

[Signature]

P.S. ‚Äî [Reinforce guarantee and/or reference proclamation one more time]"

VOICE & TONE
Required voice:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Bold (making shocking claims)
Research-backed (proving every statement) OR logic-backed (if no research)
Righteous anger (exposing industry lies)
Empowering (removing self-blame)
"You've been lied to, here's the truth"

Proclamation Lead voice is DIFFERENT from:
‚óè‚Äã Story Lead: Educational, mechanism revelation
‚óè‚Äã Secret Lead: Mysterious, exclusive
‚óè‚Äã Proclamation Lead: Bold, truth-revealing, righteously angry
Sentence structure: Short, punchy, declarative statements

PART 5: OUTPUT
Claude, now write the complete ad copy following all instructions above.
CRITICAL - BEFORE WRITING:
‚úì Verify Research Integrity:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

If research was provided, does it appear real and verifiable?
If it seems fake or unverifiable, DO NOT use it
Instead, use observable patterns and logical arguments
NEVER fabricate studies, lawsuits, or statistics

Before writing, internally confirm: ‚úì Headline makes bold, shocking statement ‚úì I will prove it
with specific research (ONLY if verifiable) OR logical arguments ‚úì I have mapped the
proof-stacking sequence with 6-8 micro-steps ‚úì I will execute proof-stacking in paragraphs
11-30, building credibility gradually ‚úì I will show why it's personally relevant ‚úì I will deliver the
implicit promise through product connection ‚úì I will show industry response OR industry
behavior ‚úì I will target Level 2 emotional desire ‚úì Voice is bold and truth-revealing, not soft ‚úì
I will return to proclamation in closing ‚úì I will include CITATIONS section if I referenced any
studies ‚úì Target length: 2,000-2,500 words ‚úì I will NOT copy example structures exactly - I
will adapt to this specific niche, avatar, angle, and proclamation
Write the ad now.

CITATIONS SECTION (REQUIRED IF YOU CITED RESEARCH)
If you cited any studies, lawsuits, statistics, or research in the ad copy above, you MUST include
full citations here:

Format:
CITATIONS & SOURCES

[1] Study/Source Name
Authors. (Year). "Title of Study." Journal Name, Volume(Issue), pages. DOI: [link]
[Brief description of what this study showed]

[2] Lawsuit Name (if cited)
Case Name, Case Number, Court (Filing Date)
[Brief description of lawsuit claims]

[3] Industry Statistic (if cited)
Source organization/report name (Year). "Title." [Link if available]
[What the statistic measures]

CRITICAL REMINDER:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

All citations must be real and verifiable
If you cannot verify a source, do not include it in the copy
Making up research is fraudulent and potentially illegal
Users can face FTC violations for false advertising if fake studies are used
When in doubt, use general industry facts or logical arguments instead

END OF PROMPT

7.4 - Problem Solution

PROMPT #7.4: PROBLEM-AWARE AD
COPY GENERATOR
(PROBLEM-SOLUTION LEAD)
1. UPLOAD YOUR RESEARCH DOCUMENTS:
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Document: [ATTACH FILE]
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Language Document: [ATTACH FILE]
‚óè‚Äã Mass Desire Research Document: [ATTACH FILE]

2. SPECIFY YOUR TARGET:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Mass Desire You're Targeting: [INSERT MASS DESIRE]
Macro-Avatar You‚Äôre Targeting (Optional): [INSERT AVATAR # OR NAME]
Product Name: [YOUR PRODUCT]
What's Included: [Main product + included items]
Guarantee: [Terms - days, conditions, refund policy]
Bonuses (if any): [List with values]
Price + Offer: [Listing price + Discount]

3. YOUR ANGLE:
Current Belief: [What they currently believe that keeps them stuck]
New Information: [The specific insight/fact that contradicts their belief]
Resulting Belief-Shift: [What they now realize/conclude]

4. THE POINT OF MAXIMUM ANXIETY:
The Worst Moment: [When do they feel this problem most acutely? What's the specific
moment that keeps them up at night?]
‚óè‚Äã Example: "3 AM, lying awake, wondering if this will ever get better. The moment they
realize they've tried everything and nothing worked."
The Hidden Emotion: [What do they FEEL in that moment that they won't say out loud?]

‚óè‚Äã Example: "Hopelessness. Shame about not being able to fix something so 'simple.' Fear
that they're broken."
The Private Thought: [What have they never told anyone?]
‚óè‚Äã Example: "I've never told anyone this, but I'm starting to think this is just how my life is
going to be."

I'm going to give you everything you need to write Problem-Aware ad copy using the
Problem-Solution Lead approach.
Here's what's happening:
You've done your research. You know your avatar, their problem, and what desire you're
targeting. Most importantly, you understand their pain‚Äînot just the surface problem, but the
deep emotional cost they're carrying. Now you need ad copy that shows you feel what they're
feeling before revealing the solution.
This prompt is flexible - you can use it for:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Testing angles - Write multiple versions with different belief-shifts
Testing desires - Same angle, different underlying desires
Testing empathy approaches - Same angle, different emotional entry points
Scaling winners - Create variations of what's already working

What's Problem-Aware?
Problem-Aware prospects already know they have a problem. They feel it deeply. It keeps them
up at night.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Cat owner watching their cat lose weight, meal after meal, feeling helpless
Dog owner embarrassed to walk their reactive dog, avoiding neighbors
Parent watching their kid struggle with focus, feeling like they're failing them
Business owner in constant cash flow panic, unable to plan ahead

They've tried casual things on their own (different cat foods, training techniques, study methods,
budgeting approaches). Nothing has worked. They're starting to lose hope.
What's the Problem-Solution Lead approach?
Problem-Solution Lead = "I see your pain. I feel what you're feeling. Here's WHY this is
happening (it's not your fault). Solutions DO exist. Here's what's possible."

It's empathy-first and hope-offering. You validate their struggle, explain the mechanism, and
show them a path forward.
When to use Problem-Solution Lead vs. Story Lead vs. Secret Lead vs. Proclamation
Lead:
Use PROBLEM-SOLUTION LEAD when:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

They KNOW they have a problem and feel it deeply
They DON'T know solutions exist or believe it's solvable
They're worried, frustrated, or desperate
You need to show empathy BEFORE revealing solution
The problem carries emotional weight (fear, shame, guilt, helplessness)

Use STORY LEAD when:
‚óè‚Äã Focus is on mechanism understanding
‚óè‚Äã Less emotional weight, more intellectual curiosity
‚óè‚Äã "Here's why this happens" without deep pain
Use SECRET LEAD when:
‚óè‚Äã Focus is on exclusivity and insider knowledge
‚óè‚Äã Less about pain, more about missing out
Use PROCLAMATION LEAD when:
‚óè‚Äã Focus is on shocking truth/bold statement
‚óè‚Äã Less about empathy, more about revelation
What's an angle?
Your angle is the new information that shifts their belief about the problem, making your solution
suddenly feel necessary.
It's made of three parts:
1.‚Äã Current Belief - What they think now that keeps them stuck
2.‚Äã New Information - The specific insight that contradicts that belief
3.‚Äã Resulting Belief-Shift - What they realize after learning the new info
For Problem-Solution Leads, the angle often removes blame and creates hope that solutions
exist.
Examples:

‚óè‚Äã Pet: Current belief: "My cat is just picky" ‚Üí New info: "It's whisker pain from bowl
contact (24 whiskers √ó 200 nerve endings each)" ‚Üí Belief-shift: "She's not picky‚Äîshe's
avoiding pain. This is fixable."
‚óè‚Äã Parenting: Current belief: "My kid lacks discipline" ‚Üí New info: "It's attention
residue‚Äîbrain needs 23 minutes to fully switch tasks" ‚Üí Belief-shift: "Not a character
flaw‚Äîa cognitive mechanism. This is solvable."
What's the Point of Maximum Anxiety?
From Masterson: "The key is to show you 'feel their pain.' Not just that you know they have a
problem, but that you know the frustration, desperation, or even fear and anger it causes."
This is the worst moment. The 3 AM thought. The thing they feel but won't say out loud.
Finding it unlocks empathy that opens doors.
What I'm going to do:
1.‚Äã Extract your inputs (avatar, desire, angle, point of maximum anxiety)
2.‚Äã Generate 10-15 headline options
3.‚Äã You pick your favorite headline
4.‚Äã I write complete ad copy (2,500-3,000 words) using empathy-first gradualization
The copy follows the Problem-Solution framework: Emotional recognition ‚Üí Problem validation
‚Üí Anxiety acknowledgment ‚Üí Mechanism revelation ‚Üí Hope introduction ‚Üí Proof ‚Üí Offer.

ANYTHING BELOW THIS LINE.

Step 3: Map The Belief-Shift To Gradualization
The angle's belief-shift must be gradualized within Step 4 (Mechanism Revelation).
Break it into 5-7 micro-steps:
Micro-Step 1: Introduce Small Doubt
‚óè‚Äã What tiny anomaly challenges their current belief?
‚óè‚Äã Frame as confusion, not revelation
Micro-Step 2: Show Pattern
‚óè‚Äã The anomaly isn't isolated‚Äîhappens to others too
‚óè‚Äã Build from "weird coincidence" to "consistent pattern"
Micro-Step 3: Create Question
‚óè‚Äã What question does this pattern raise?
‚óè‚Äã Show you investigating, not knowing answer yet
Micro-Step 4: Introduce New Information
‚óè‚Äã Present the new information from the angle
‚óè‚Äã Can they observe/verify? Use authority?
‚óè‚Äã Make it undeniable
Micro-Step 5: Connect to Their Situation
‚óè‚Äã How does new information apply to their specific case?
‚óè‚Äã Show the verification moment
Micro-Step 6: Show Gap
‚óè‚Äã What's the difference between what they thought and what's true?
‚óè‚Äã Quantify if possible
Micro-Step 7: State New Belief as Discovery
‚óè‚Äã Frame as realization, not claim
‚óè‚Äã "That's when I realized..."

Each micro-step = 2-3 paragraphs in actual copy.

HEADLINE: Name The Need With Empathy
From Schwartz: "Start by naming the need"
Use the headline selected from Part 2.
CRITICAL: The headline should create recognition ("that's my problem") with an empathetic
tone.

OPENING: Emotional Recognition
Structure:
Paragraphs 1-3: NAME THE FEELING
Open by naming the FEELING they're experiencing, not just the problem.
BAD Opening: "Are you struggling with [problem]?"
GOOD Opening: "If you've been feeling like you've tried everything and nothing works... if
you're starting to wonder if this is just how life is going to be from now on... I need you to know
something."
Opening structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"If you've been feeling..."
Name the emotional experience
Validate the struggle
Offer immediate relief ("It's not your fault" OR "I need you to know something")

Examples across niches:
‚óè‚Äã Pet: "If you've been watching your cat lose weight, meal after meal... if you've tried every
food brand, every flavor, every trick... and nothing changes... I need you to know: It's not
your fault."
‚óè‚Äã Parenting: "If you've been watching your kid struggle with homework for hours... if
you've felt that helpless frustration when nothing you try helps... I need you to know: It's
not a discipline problem."

‚óè‚Äã Business: "If you've been lying awake at night, calculating whether you can make
payroll... if you've turned down opportunities because you can't predict your cash flow... I
need you to know: There's a reason this keeps happening."

‚ùå

‚ùå

DON'T:
Start with the surface problem
Use clinical language
validation
Start with your story (this is about THEM)

‚ùå Skip the emotional

DO: ‚úì Name the feeling first ‚úì Use "you" language (second person) ‚úì Include the
struggle/attempt ‚úì Offer immediate emotional relief

Paragraphs 4-6: PROBLEM VALIDATION
Describe what they're experiencing in THEIR words. Show you've "been there" emotionally.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Use language from avatar doc
Describe specific moments they recognize
Include the hidden thoughts (what they think but don't say)
Validate they're doing everything "right"

Example:
"Maybe you've stood there, watching your cat sniff her bowl, take one bite, and walk away.
Again.
And you feel that knot in your stomach. That helpless feeling.
Because you're doing everything right. You bought premium food. You followed all the vet's
advice. You've tried fifteen different brands.
And she's still losing weight.
Maybe you've had this thought: 'What if there's something I'm missing? What if she gets
seriously sick because I couldn't figure out something as simple as getting her to eat?'
You haven't told anyone that thought. But it's there. At 2 AM when you can't sleep."

DON'T:
judged

‚ùå Stay generic ‚ùå Skip the private thoughts ‚ùå Use third person ‚ùå Make them feel

DO: ‚úì Use specific moments they recognize ‚úì Include the 2 AM thoughts ‚úì Pull from
language doc ‚úì Validate their efforts

Paragraphs 7-10: ANXIETY ACKNOWLEDGMENT
Touch the point of maximum anxiety. Name the hidden fear. Then move forward.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Name the secret fear (from user input)
Validate it's real (don't dismiss it)
Remove blame ("you're not missing anything")
Transition to mechanism ("the problem isn't what you think")

Example:
"That fear‚Äîthat you're missing something obvious, that you're failing at something every other
cat owner can do‚Äîis real.
I'm not going to tell you it's silly or that you're overreacting.
But I am going to tell you this:
You're not missing anything.
Your cat isn't being difficult.
And this isn't something 'simple' that you failed to figure out.
Because the problem isn't what you think it is."

CRITICAL: Don't linger here. Touch it, validate it, then move to mechanism.
DON'T:
entirely

‚ùå Wallow in the pain ‚ùå Amplify the anxiety ‚ùå Dismiss their fear as silly ‚ùå Skip this

DO: ‚úì Name the specific fear ‚úì Validate it's real ‚úì Remove self-blame ‚úì Transition to "here's
why"

THE MECHANISM REVELATION SECTION (Paragraphs 11-20 - CRITICAL)

This is where you gradualize the belief-shift from the angle.
You mapped the 5-7 micro-steps in Step 3. Now execute them in the copy.
PACING DISCIPLINE:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

DO NOT state the new belief before paragraph 18
Each micro-step = 2-3 paragraphs
Use confusion/curiosity language, not claims
Include physical details, specific moments
Frame as investigation/discovery

‚ùå

‚ùå
‚ùå

‚ùå

FORBIDDEN patterns:
"I was wrong about [old belief]"
"The truth is [new belief]"
Jumping from doubt ‚Üí new belief in under 8 paragraphs
Stating mechanism before building
sufficient doubt
REQUIRED patterns: ‚úì "But I noticed something weird..." (doubt) ‚úì "That didn't make
sense..." (confusion) ‚úì "So I started investigating..." (curiosity) ‚úì "Found a study/expert who
explained..." (new information) ‚úì "I checked my own [situation]..." (personal application) ‚úì
"That's when it clicked..." (realization)

MICRO-STEP EXECUTION GUIDE:
Micro-Step 1 (Introduce Small Doubt) - Paragraphs 11-12:
Share a specific anomaly that challenges their current belief.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Pet: "But then I noticed something weird. Even when I changed to the food she used to
love, she still wouldn't eat."
‚óè‚Äã Parenting: "But then I noticed something. On weekends, when there was no homework
pressure, my kid could focus for hours on video games."
‚óè‚Äã Business: "But then I looked at my numbers differently. The months I had the most
sales were also the months I had the worst cash flow."
Make it concrete. Reader should think: "Huh, that IS weird"
Micro-Step 2 (Show Pattern) - Paragraphs 13-14:
Show this isn't isolated‚Äîhappened to others too.
Examples:

‚óè‚Äã Pet: "Started asking around. Other cat owners had the same thing. 'My cat used to eat
this food, now she won't touch it.'"
‚óè‚Äã Parenting: "Talked to other parents. Same thing. Kids who 'can't focus' on homework
can hyperfocus on games, books, building things."
‚óè‚Äã Business: "Checked with other business owners. Same pattern. Their best sales
months created the worst cash crunches."
Reader should think: "Why does this keep happening?"
Micro-Step 3 (Create Question) - Paragraphs 15-16:
Articulate the question this pattern raises.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Pet: "If it's about the food, why does changing food not fix it? Why do cats who used to
eat normally suddenly become 'picky'?"
‚óè‚Äã Parenting: "If it's a focus problem, why can they focus perfectly in some contexts but not
others? What's different?"
‚óè‚Äã Business: "If more sales means more money, why does it create cash flow problems?
What's the gap?"
Reader should think: "Yeah, what DOES explain this?"
Micro-Step 4 (Introduce New Information) - Paragraphs 17-19:
Present the new information from the angle.
This is the CRITICAL moment. Handle it carefully.
If the new information is observable: Guide them to check something themselves.
If the new information is mechanism-based: Use authority dialogue or research to explain.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Pet (Observable): "Watch your cat next time she approaches her bowl. Her whiskers.
See what happens when they touch the bowl sides? She pulls back slightly."
‚óè‚Äã Parenting (Mechanism): "Found a cognitive researcher who explained 'attention
residue.' When your brain switches tasks, it takes 23 minutes to fully transition.
Homework requires constant task-switching."
‚óè‚Äã Business (Mechanism): "Accountant explained the '45-day gap.' You spend money on
ads today. Customer buys today. But payment processing + product fulfillment + actual
deposit = 45 days before cash hits your account."
Reader should think: "That would explain it..." OR "I can see that right now"

Micro-Step 5 (Connect to Their Situation) - Paragraph 19:
Apply new information to their specific case.
Include concrete details (checking, observing, calculating).
Micro-Step 6 (Show Gap) - Paragraph 20:
Reveal the difference between assumption and reality.
Explain why old belief seemed right but wasn't complete.
Micro-Step 7 (State New Belief as Discovery) - Paragraph 20:
"That's when I realized [resulting belief-shift from angle]"
Frame as shared discovery, not lecture.
SCHWARTZ'S TEST at each micro-step: "Could the reader reject this thought?"
‚óè‚Äã If YES ‚Üí add more setup, slow down
‚óè‚Äã If NO ‚Üí continue to next step
By paragraph 20, the new belief should feel like the reader's conclusion.

HOPE INTRODUCTION (Paragraphs 21-25)
Transition from "here's the problem" to "solutions exist."
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"Here's the good news..."
State the solution principle (not product yet)
Show it's achievable/simple
Offer timeline for relief
Create hope

Example:
"Here's the good news:
Once you know [the real problem from angle], the solution is simple.
[State solution principle]

That's it. That's the whole solution.
Most [people/pets/situations] [see results] within [timeline].
Because you've removed [the real cause]."

‚ùå

DON'T:
Introduce product yet
this transition

‚ùå Oversell or hype ‚ùå Make it sound complicated ‚ùå Skip

DO: ‚úì Offer genuine hope ‚úì Keep it simple ‚úì Provide timeline ‚úì Connect to mechanism

PROOF & CREDIBILITY (Paragraphs 26-40)
Build credibility through authority, research, and results.
Paragraphs 26-30: Authority & Research (if available)
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Name authority/researcher
Explain what they studied
Present findings
Cite institution if possible

Paragraphs 31-35: Show It Works
Results/testimonials from people like them.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Specific person
Their situation (similar to reader)
What happened
Timeline
Emotional payoff

Paragraphs 36-40: Present Your Product
Now introduce YOUR solution as the BEST way to solve this.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã Name the product
‚óè‚Äã Explain how it addresses the REAL problem (from angle)

‚óè‚Äã Show what makes it better than DIY
‚óè‚Äã Provide specific design features
‚óè‚Äã Give results/stats

Paragraphs 51-55: Push-Pull Close
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"I can't make you try this..."
Acknowledge they could keep trying other things
"But now you know: [restate the real problem]"
Show cost of continuing
Make the choice clear

Example:
"I can't make you try this.
If you want to keep [old approach], you can do that.
But now you know: The problem isn't [what they thought]. It's [real problem from angle].
And every [time unit] you wait is another [time unit] [consequence continues].
The question is: Do you want to keep trying things that can't work?
Or do you want to fix the actual problem?"

MASTERSON PRINCIPLES (Apply Throughout)
Principle 1: Feel Their Pain
From Masterson: "Show you 'feel their pain.' Not just that you know they have a problem, but
that you know the frustration, desperation, or even fear and anger it causes."
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Use their language
Name the hidden emotions
Touch the worst moments
Validate their struggle

Principle 2: Target Core Emotions
From Masterson: "You'll consistently get more bang for your buck if you can tap these
subconscious feelings first."
‚óè‚Äã Fear

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Frustration
Guilt
Shame
Self-doubt
Inadequacy

Principle 3: Don't Linger Too Long
From Masterson: "Don't linger too long before offering hope"
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Touch the pain
Validate it
Move to mechanism by paragraph 11
Offer hope by paragraph 21

Principle 4: External Blame
Remove guilt and shame:
‚óè‚Äã "It's not your fault"
‚óè‚Äã "You couldn't have known"
‚óè‚Äã "The mechanism is to blame"
Principle 5: Hope Quickly
After acknowledging pain, offer light at end of tunnel:
‚óè‚Äã Solutions exist
‚óè‚Äã This is fixable
‚óè‚Äã Here's what's possible

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Problem-Solution Lead voice is DIFFERENT from:
‚óè‚Äã Story Lead: Educational revelation
‚óè‚Äã Secret Lead: Mysterious exclusive

‚óè‚Äã Proclamation Lead: Bold truth-telling
‚óè‚Äã Problem-Solution Lead: Empathetic hope-offering
Sentence structure: Natural, conversational, emotionally connected

8.1 - Story Lead

PROMPT #8.1: SOLUTION-AWARE AD
COPY GENERATOR (STORY LEAD)
1. UPLOAD YOUR RESEARCH DOCUMENTS:
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Document: [ATTACH FILE]
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Language Document: [ATTACH FILE]
‚óè‚Äã Mass Desire Research Document: [ATTACH FILE]

2. SPECIFY YOUR TARGET:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Mass Desire You're Targeting: [INSERT MASS DESIRE]
Macro-Avatar You‚Äôre Targeting (Optional): [INSERT AVATAR # OR NAME]
Product Name: [YOUR PRODUCT]
What's Included: [Main product + included items]
Guarantee: [Terms - days, conditions, refund policy]
Bonuses (if any): [List with values]
Price + Offer: [Listing price + Discount]

3. YOUR ANGLE:
Current Belief: [What they currently believe that keeps them stuck]
New Information: [The specific insight/fact that contradicts their belief]
Resulting Belief-Shift: [What they now realize/conclude]

4. SOLUTIONS THEY'VE TRIED (CRITICAL FOR SOLUTION-AWARE):
List the actual products/methods they purchased and tried:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

[Brand/product name - Example: "PetSafe fountain"]
[Brand/product name - Example: "Catit fountain"]
[Brand/product name - Example: "Switched to 100% wet food"]
[etc.]

Note: These must be FORMAL SOLUTIONS (products purchased, methods researched), not
casual attempts.
‚óè‚Äã

‚úÖ "Tried 3 different cat fountains from Amazon" = Solution-Aware

‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå "Moved water bowl to different spots" = Problem-Aware (casual attempt)

I'm going to give you everything you need to write Solution-Aware ad copy using the Story Lead
approach.
Here's what's happening:
You've done your research. You know your avatar, their problem, and what desire you're
targeting. Most importantly, you know they've TRIED multiple solutions that all failed to deliver
the result they want. Now you need ad copy that acknowledges their frustration, explains why
those solutions failed, and shows what actually works.
This prompt is flexible - you can use it for:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Testing angles - Write multiple versions with different belief-shifts
Testing desires - Same angle, different underlying desires
Testing story approaches - Same angle, different narrative entry points
Scaling winners - Create variations of what's already working

What's Solution-Aware?
Solution-Aware prospects know they have a problem, know the result they want, and know
solutions exist. They've actively tried multiple products/methods. None delivered the result.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Cat owner tried 3 fountains, wet food, supplements - cat still won't drink, kidneys
declining
‚óè‚Äã Woman tried Venus, Billie, electric razors - still gets razor burn with all of them
‚óè‚Äã Insomniac tried melatonin, sleep apps, white noise machines - still can't sleep
‚óè‚Äã Person tried keto, paleo, Whole30 - still can't lose weight
They're frustrated, exhausted, and skeptical. They've heard "this one's different" before.
What's the Story Lead approach?
Story Lead = "I tried everything you tried. Nothing worked. Here's why they all failed. Then I
discovered what actually delivers the result. Here's my story."
It's personal narrative and mechanism revelation through lived experience.
When to use Solution-Aware vs. Problem-Aware:
Use SOLUTION-AWARE when:

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

They know the result they want
They know solutions exist
They've tried multiple ACTUAL PRODUCTS (not just casual attempts)
Those solutions didn't deliver the result
They're frustrated and skeptical

Use PROBLEM-AWARE when:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

They only tried casual things (moved the bowl, tried different techniques)
They haven't bought actual solutions yet
They're not sure if solutions exist
They're resigned or hopeful, not frustrated/skeptical

What's an angle?
Your angle is the new information that shifts their belief about why solutions failed and what
actually works.
It's made of three parts:
1.‚Äã Current Belief - What they think now that keeps them trying the same category
2.‚Äã New Information - The specific insight that contradicts that belief
3.‚Äã Resulting Belief-Shift - What they realize after learning the new info
For Solution-Aware, the angle often explains why the ENTIRE CATEGORY they tried can't work.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Pet: Current belief: "I just need to find the right fountain" ‚Üí New info: "Cats have mineral
receptors that plain water (moving or not) doesn't trigger" ‚Üí Belief-shift: "It's not about
delivery‚Äîit's about composition. Fountains can't fix this."
‚óè‚Äã Beauty: Current belief: "I need better technique or the right razor" ‚Üí New info: "ALL
razors traumatize living skin tissue when blade scrapes across‚Äîit's physics" ‚Üí
Belief-shift: "It's not my technique‚Äîthe method itself causes damage. No razor solves
this."
What I'm going to do:
1.‚Äã Extract your inputs (avatar, desire, angle, solutions tried)
2.‚Äã Determine market sophistication (based on # of solutions tried)
3.‚Äã Generate 10-15 headline options using Schwartz sophistication frameworks
4.‚Äã You pick your favorite headline
5.‚Äã I write complete ad copy (2,400-2,800 words) using skepticism-reduction gradualization
The copy follows Solution-Aware Story Lead framework: "I tried everything too" ‚Üí "Here's why
they all failed" ‚Üí "What makes this different" ‚Üí "The result I finally got."

ANYTHING BELOW THIS LINE.

CRITICAL - SCHWARTZ SOLUTION-AWARE HEADLINE PRINCIPLES
From Breakthrough Advertising:
Solution-Aware headlines must:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Crystallize the EMOTIONAL DESIRE (Level 2), not logical want (Level 1)
State the END STATE/RESULT, not the journey
NO mechanism explanation (that's for body copy)
NO story/journey ("I tried X then Y" is body copy)
NO comparison to competitors ("when fountains fail" is body copy)

From Schwartz: "Sometimes the simplest statement of the desire is the best."
Examples of correct Solution-Aware headlines:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"Who else wants a whiter wash‚Äîwith no hard work?" (desire + ease)

SCHWARTZ'S HEADLINE STRENGTHENING TECHNIQUES
Based on sophistication level, apply these techniques:
Available Techniques:
1.‚Äã VERBALIZATION (Make Desire Specific)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Add numbers, timeframes, specific outcomes
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Hands that look lovelier in 24 hours"
2.‚Äã INTENSIFICATION (Dramatize The Desire)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Heighten emotional stakes
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Feel confident in your skin again"

3.‚Äã PERSONALIZATION (Identify WHO)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Specific identification with emotional state
‚óã‚Äã Example: "To people who want to write‚Äîbut can't get started"
4.‚Äã DRAMATIZATION (Add Scenario/Moment)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Frame as pivotal moment with setup + payoff
‚óã‚Äã Example: "They laughed when I sat down‚Äîbut when I began to play!"
5.‚Äã PROOF ELEMENTS (Add Credibility)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Add guarantee, authority, statistics, timeframe
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Or your money back"
6.‚Äã WONDERMENT (Impossible-Seeming Result)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Present surprising/counterintuitive combination
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Who ever heard of a woman losing weight‚Äîand enjoying 3 delicious
meals?"
7.‚Äã IDENTIFICATION + EMOTIONAL STATE‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Name them + their painful emotional state
‚óã‚Äã Example: "To men who want to quit work someday"
8.‚Äã FUTURE STATE PROJECTION‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Paint the future they desire (often uses "again" = restoration)
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Feel confident again"
9.‚Äã QUESTION FORM (Identifying Pain)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Question that identifies specific painful situation
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Do you make these mistakes in English?"
10.‚ÄãCOMMAND FORM (Direct Action)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Direct command + transformation
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Stop hiding and start feeling confident"
11.‚ÄãHOW-TO FORMAT (Classic Schwartz)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Simple, direct format crystallizing desire
‚óã‚Äã Example: "How to win friends and influence people"

MAP SOPHISTICATION TO TECHNIQUES
STAGE 3 SOPHISTICATION (1-2 products tried):
‚óè‚Äã Lower skepticism = simpler headlines work

‚óè‚Äã Use: Verbalization + Simple desire statement
STAGE 4 SOPHISTICATION (3-4 products tried):
‚óè‚Äã Higher skepticism = need proof + drama
‚óè‚Äã Use: Verbalization + Proof + Dramatization
STAGE 5 SOPHISTICATION (5+ products tried):
‚óè‚Äã Highest skepticism = need identification + emotional drama
‚óè‚Äã Use: Identification + Dramatization + Emotional State

WINNING HEADLINE FRAMEWORKS
Framework 1: Simple How-To + Emotional Desire
‚óè‚Äã Template: "How To [EMOTIONAL RESULT]"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 3-4
Framework 2: Identification + Future State
‚óè‚Äã Template: "To [WHO] Who Want [EMOTIONAL END STATE]"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 4-5
Framework 3: Verbalized Result + Proof
‚óè‚Äã Template: "[SPECIFIC RESULT] In [TIME]‚ÄîOr Your Money Back"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 4
Framework 4: Who Else Wants + Emotional Desire
‚óè‚Äã Template: "Who Else Wants [EMOTIONAL RESULT]?"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 3-4
Framework 5: Dramatized Moment
‚óè‚Äã Template: "They [EMBARRASSMENT]‚ÄîBut [TRIUMPH]!"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 4-5
Framework 6: Identification + Stuck Point
‚óè‚Äã Template: "To [WHO] Who [SPECIFIC PROBLEM/BEHAVIOR]"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 5
Framework 7: When Authority + Solution

‚óè‚Äã Template: "When [AUTHORITY] [PROBLEM]‚ÄîThis Is What They Do"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 4-5
Framework 8: Command + Transformation
‚óè‚Äã Template: "Stop [PAINFUL BEHAVIOR] And Start [DESIRED STATE]"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 4-5
Framework 9: Personal Testimony + Result + Time
‚óè‚Äã Template: "How I [GOT RESULT] In [TIMEFRAME]"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 4
Framework 10: Question Identifying Pain
‚óè‚Äã Template: "Do You [PAINFUL BEHAVIOR] Because [EMOTIONAL REASON]?"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 5
Framework 11: The Moment + Emotional Restoration
‚óè‚Äã Template: "The Moment [EMOTIONAL BREAKTHROUGH]"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 5
Framework 12: Wonderment + Impossibility
‚óè‚Äã Template: "Who Ever Heard Of [IMPOSSIBILITY]?"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 4

CLAUDE INSTRUCTIONS FOR HEADLINE GENERATION:

4.‚Äã Apply strengthening techniques for that sophistication level
5.‚Äã Generate headlines that crystallize EMOTIONAL desire (Level 2)
For each headline, provide:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Framework name/number
Strengthening techniques applied
Why it works (1-2 sentences explaining how it crystallizes Level 2 emotional desire)

FINAL CHECK BEFORE GENERATING: ‚úì Does each headline crystallize Level 2 emotional

removed? ‚úì Are em dashes used sparingly (only for dramatic contrast)? ‚úì Are strengthening
techniques applied appropriately for sophistication level?
