PART 3: CLAUDE PROCESSING INSTRUCTIONS
Claude, before writing the ad copy, you must:
Step 1: Build The One Belief
The Solution-Aware Formula: "[RESULT THEY WANT] is achievable - but [SOLUTIONS THEY
TRIED] don't deliver it because [MECHANISM OF FAILURE] - [YOUR SOLUTION] delivers
[RESULT] through [DIFFERENT MECHANISM] - so you finally get [EMOTIONAL DESIRE]"
Extract:
‚óè‚Äã Result They Want: The outcome they've been trying to achieve
‚óè‚Äã Solutions They Tried: List from user input
‚óè‚Äã Why They Failed: Extract from angle's new information

‚óè‚Äã The Differentiator: What makes yours fundamentally different (from angle)
‚óè‚Äã Emotional State: Frustration, skepticism, exhaustion from failures
‚óè‚Äã Emotional Desire: Level 2 from avatar doc
Step 2: Engineer The Skepticism-Reduction Gradualization
Solution-Aware markets at high sophistication are HIGHLY SKEPTICAL because they've tried
multiple products that claimed to be "different" and all failed.
Your gradualization must focus on gradually proving your differentiator is real and meaningful,
not just asserted.
DO NOT jump from "solutions failed" to "mine is different" without gradualization. Break the
skepticism reduction into discoverable micro-steps.
REQUIRED: Map out 5-7 micro-steps that gradually build belief in your differentiator:
Micro-Step 1: Acknowledge Category Experience
‚óè‚Äã "I tried the same products you did"
‚óè‚Äã Establish shared frustration
‚óè‚Äã Validate their experience
Micro-Step 2: Introduce Small Doubt About Category Approach
‚óè‚Äã Not about individual products, but the CATEGORY approach
‚óè‚Äã "But I noticed something weird about ALL of them..."
‚óè‚Äã Frame as curiosity, not criticism
Micro-Step 3: Pattern Recognition (Why Category Fails)
‚óè‚Äã Show the pattern across all solutions they tried
‚óè‚Äã "They all focused on X, none addressed Y"
‚óè‚Äã This is the mechanism of failure
Micro-Step 4: Introduce New Information
‚óè‚Äã Present the new information from the angle
‚óè‚Äã Make it verifiable or authority-backed
‚óè‚Äã Explain why this makes the category approach incomplete
Micro-Step 5: Show Why Category Approach Can't Address This
‚óè‚Äã Connect new information to why their solutions failed
‚óè‚Äã Not "they were bad products" but "category can't solve this"
‚óè‚Äã Remove brand blame, focus on approach limitation

Micro-Step 6: Introduce YOUR Different Approach
‚óè‚Äã Show how YOUR mechanism addresses what category can't
‚óè‚Äã NOT "better" but "different category of solution"
Micro-Step 7: Show Early Proof Your Approach Works
‚óè‚Äã Initial results that prove the difference
‚óè‚Äã Contrast with category approach results
Each micro-step = 2-3 paragraphs in actual copy. Total pacing: Spend paragraphs 36-50
walking through these steps.
CRITICAL: They've heard "we're different" before. You must PROVE the difference is real
through graduated skepticism reduction, not assert it.
Step 3: Map To Solution-Aware Story Lead Structure
Solution-Aware Framework:
Step 1: "I Tried Everything Too"
‚óè‚Äã List solutions you tried (same as them)
‚óè‚Äã Show you understand their frustration
‚óè‚Äã Establish: "I've been where you are"
Step 2: "Here's Why They All Failed"
‚óè‚Äã Explain mechanism of failure for each
‚óè‚Äã Show pattern of why they don't deliver result
‚óè‚Äã Remove self-blame: "It's not you, it's the solutions"
Step 3: "Then I Discovered The Difference" (WITH GRADUALIZATION)
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Gradually build belief that your differentiator is real
Execute the 5-7 micro-steps
Authority validation
Why THIS delivers the result when others don't

Step 4: "Here's The Result I Finally Got"
‚óè‚Äã Transformation timeline
‚óè‚Äã The result they want - achieved
‚óè‚Äã Proof it delivers what others didn't

PART 4: WRITING INSTRUCTIONS
CORE STRUCTURE: The Failed Solutions Story
Your copy must acknowledge their failed attempts, explain why they failed, then gradually prove
what delivers the result.
Paragraphs 1-15: "I TRIED EVERYTHING TOO"
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Open with exhaustive list of solutions tried
Show you know their frustration
Validate: "You're not crazy - these things don't work"
Establish credibility through shared experience

Paragraphs 16-35: "HERE'S WHY THEY ALL FAILED"
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Explain mechanism of failure for each solution
Show the pattern (they all miss the same thing)
Remove self-blame: It's the solutions, not them
Authority validates why standard solutions don't deliver

Paragraphs 36-50: GRADUALIZATION - "WHAT MAKES THIS DIFFERENT"
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Execute the 5-7 micro-steps
Gradually prove your differentiator is real
Different mechanism (not better - different)
Authority validation of the approach
Connect to the result they want

Paragraphs 51-70: "THE RESULT I FINALLY GOT"
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Transformation timeline
Result achieved
Proof it delivers what they want
Offer and guarantee

LENGTH & PACING
Target: 2,400-2,800 words
Solution-Aware is LONGER than Problem-Aware because:
‚óè‚Äã Must list multiple failed solutions
‚óè‚Äã Must explain why each failed
‚óè‚Äã Must overcome skepticism from failures through gradualization

‚óè‚Äã Must show what makes THIS different
Pacing discipline:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Failed solutions list: 3-5 specific examples
Failure explanations: 2-3 paragraphs per major category
Gradualization sequence: 15 paragraphs (paragraphs 36-50)
Transformation: 4-6 data points with timeline

HEADLINE: Crystallized Emotional Desire
From Schwartz: Solution-Aware headlines must crystallize EMOTIONAL desire (Level 2), not
logical want (Level 1).
Use the headline selected from Part 2.
CRITICAL: The headline states the END STATE/RESULT, not the journey.

OPENING: "I Tried Everything Too"
Structure:
Paragraphs 1-5: LIST SOLUTIONS YOU TRIED
Open with specific products/methods you tried (matching what they tried).
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Start with the first thing you tried
Show initial hope
Show it didn't deliver
List 3-5 specific solutions
Show mounting frustration

Example:
"I tried PetSafe first. $42.99 for the stainless steel fountain. 'Cats love running water,' the
reviews said.
Max sniffed it. Walked away.
Okay, maybe he needed time to adjust.

A week later: the fountain was full. He was still drinking from his regular bowl. Barely.
Fine. Maybe PetSafe wasn't the right brand.
I got the Catit flower fountain ($29.99). Different design. 'Whisper quiet,' the description
promised. 'Cats prefer this over regular bowls.'
He ignored it completely.
By then I'd spent $72 on fountains and his kidney values were getting worse.
So I switched to 100% wet food. Not cheap‚Äî$3.50/day for premium brands. 'Most cats get
enough hydration from wet food alone,' the article said.
His water intake improved slightly. His kidney values? Still declining."

‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

DON'T:
Skip the specific products
Generalize ("I tried fountains")
Make it about them yet (this is YOUR story)

‚ùå Rush through this

DO: ‚úì Name specific brands/products ‚úì Include prices if relevant ‚úì Show mounting
frustration ‚úì Include the promises that didn't deliver ‚úì Use "I" throughout (first person story)

Paragraphs 6-10: ESTABLISH SHARED FRUSTRATION
Transition from your list to their situation.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Acknowledge you tried what they're trying
Show you understand the exhaustion
Name the emotional state
Validate it's not their fault

Example:
"Maybe you're trying fountains right now. Or wet food. Or hydration supplements.
Maybe you've spent $100+. Maybe $500+.
And your cat still isn't drinking enough. The kidney values keep getting worse.
And every article, every vet, every forum keeps recommending the same things you've already
tried.

I know that exhaustion. That frustration. That creeping fear that nothing works and you're
watching them decline.
It's not you.
And it's not your cat.
But it IS the solutions."

DON'T:

‚ùå Blame them for trying ‚ùå Mock the solutions ‚ùå Skip the emotional validation

DO: ‚úì Acknowledge their spending ‚úì Name the emotion (exhaustion, frustration, fear) ‚úì
Validate they're doing everything right ‚úì Set up "it's the solutions, not you"

Paragraphs 11-15: "I'VE BEEN WHERE YOU ARE"
Establish credibility through shared experience.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Show you were in the same place
Reference specific moments they recognize
Build trust through specificity
Transition to "then I discovered why"

Example:
"I've been exactly where you are.
Staring at a third fountain that cost $89.99, watching Max walk past it to his regular bowl.
Googling 'why won't my cat drink from fountain' at 2 AM.
Reading the same advice over and over: 'Try a different fountain.' 'Some cats prefer ceramic.'
'Add wet food.'
Already doing all of that.
And then his vet called with bloodwork results. Creatinine: 3.8. BUN: 42. 'We need to talk about
kidney disease management.'

That's when I stopped taking advice from blog posts and started reading actual veterinary
research.
And that's when I found out why every solution I'd tried couldn't work."

THE CATEGORY FAILURE EXPLANATION (Paragraphs 16-35)
Structure:
Paragraphs 16-20: SHOW THE PATTERN
Explain what ALL the solutions have in common.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

List the categories they tried
Show what each category focuses on
Reveal what they all miss
This is the angle's new information beginning to emerge

Example:
"Look at everything you've tried:
Fountains: Focus on making water MOVING
Wet food: Focus on REPLACING water through food
Supplements: Focus on making water FLAVORED
Multiple bowls: Focus on LOCATION
See the pattern?
They all focus on making plain water more APPEALING to your cat.
Moving water. Flavored water. Water in different places.
But here's what none of them address:
Whether your cat's body recognizes plain water as proper hydration in the first place."

Paragraphs 21-25: EXPLAIN WHY CATEGORY CAN'T WORK
This is where you start introducing the new information from the angle.

Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Present the mechanism/insight
Show why it makes category approach incomplete
Use authority if available
Make it verifiable or logical

Example:
"I found this in a 2019 UC Davis veterinary study.
Cats have taste receptors‚Äîspecifically, mineral-detection receptors‚Äîthat evolved to recognize
prey blood.
Prey blood contains sodium, potassium, magnesium in specific ratios.
When a cat drinks prey blood, these receptors detect the mineral signature and signal the brain:
'This IS proper hydration.

THE CATEGORY FAILURE EXPLANATION (Paragraphs 16-35)
Paragraphs 21-25: EXPLAIN WHY CATEGORY CAN'T WORK
This is where you start introducing the new information from the angle.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Present the mechanism/insight
Show why it makes category approach incomplete
Use authority if available
Make it verifiable or logical

Example:
"I found this in a 2019 UC Davis veterinary study.
Cats have taste receptors‚Äîspecifically, mineral-detection receptors‚Äîthat evolved to recognize
prey blood.

Prey blood contains sodium, potassium, magnesium in specific ratios.
When a cat drinks prey blood, these receptors detect the mineral signature and signal the brain:
'This IS proper hydration.'
Plain water‚Äîtap water, filtered water, even moving fountain water‚Äîlacks these minerals.
So when your cat sniffs fountain water, their receptors detect: 'Not proper hydration.'
They don't drink because their biology is telling them this isn't the right fluid."

Paragraphs 26-30: CONNECT TO WHY EACH SOLUTION FAILED
Now explain why each category they tried couldn't address this.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Go through each solution type
Show why it can't solve the real problem
Remove brand blame (it's not PetSafe's fault‚Äîfountains can't fix biology)
Validate their experience

Example:
"This is why fountains don't work.
Moving water doesn't change mineral composition. Whether the water flows, bubbles, or sits
still‚Äîit's still plain water. Still missing the minerals their receptors need.
That's why three different fountain brands all failed the same way.
This is why wet food helps but doesn't solve it.
Wet food provides 60-70% moisture. But it doesn't train them to drink actively. They still need to
recognize plain water as hydration for the other 30-40% of their needs.
This is why supplements usually fail after the first week.
Most are flavor-based. Tuna-flavored water. Chicken broth powder.
Flavor ‚âÝ mineral signature. Their taste buds might like it, but their mineral receptors still detect
'wrong water.'

That's why the supplement worked for a few days then stopped."

Paragraphs 31-35: REMOVE SELF-BLAME
Validate they couldn't have known this.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

It's not their fault
The solutions seemed logical
The advice was everywhere
But the advice was solving the wrong problem

Example:
"You couldn't have known this.
Every article says 'cats prefer running water'‚Äîso fountains seem like the obvious solution.
Every vet says 'wet food provides hydration'‚Äîso switching to 100% wet food seems logical.
You followed the advice. You did everything right.
The advice was just solving the wrong problem.
It wasn't about making water more appealing.
It was about making water biologically recognizable."

THE GRADUALIZATION SECTION (Paragraphs 36-50 - CRITICAL)
This is where you execute the 5-7 micro-steps to gradually prove your differentiator is real.
PACING DISCIPLINE:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

DO NOT jump to "my solution is different"
Build skepticism reduction step by step
Each micro-step = 2-3 paragraphs
Use discovery language, not claims

‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

FORBIDDEN patterns:
"That's why I created [product]" (too fast)
"My solution is better
because..." (claim without proof)
Stating differentiator without building to it

REQUIRED patterns: ‚úì "That's when I found..." (discovery) ‚úì "I tested this myself..."
(personal verification) ‚úì "The difference was..." (observed contrast) ‚úì "Within [timeframe]..."
(concrete results)

Micro-Step 1: Acknowledge Category Experience - Already done in opening
Micro-Step 2: Introduce Small Doubt About Category - Paragraphs 36-37:
Point out something weird about ALL the solutions.
Example:
"But here's what kept bothering me:
ALL three fountains‚Äîdifferent brands, different designs, different price points‚Äîfailed the exact
same way.
Max would approach. Sniff the water. Walk away.
Same behavior with all three.
If it was about the fountain design, at least ONE should have worked differently.
But they didn't."

Micro-Step 3: Pattern Recognition - Paragraphs 38-40:
Show the pattern across ALL categories they tried.
Example:
"So I looked at the pattern across everything I'd tried:
Fountains: Making water MOVE
Wet food: REPLACING water with food moisture
Supplements: Making water TASTE different
Multiple bowls: Changing water LOCATION
Every solution changed the DELIVERY or FLAVOR.
None of them changed the COMPOSITION.
And that's when I found the UC Davis study about prey-model hydration."

Micro-Step 4: Introduce New Information - Paragraphs 41-43:
Present the angle's new information through authority or discovery.
Example:
"Dr. Sarah Chen at UC Davis studied feline hydration for 8 years.
Her research showed that cats don't have a 'thirst drive' like humans.
They have mineral-detection receptors that evolved to recognize prey blood.
When these receptors detect sodium and potassium in specific ratios‚Äîthe ratios found in prey
blood‚Äîthey signal: 'Proper hydration detected. Drink this.'
When they detect plain water (no minerals), they signal: 'Not proper hydration. Don't prioritize
drinking this.'
That's the mechanism.
Not preference. Not stubbornness. Biology."

Micro-Step 5: Show Why Category Can't Address This - Paragraphs 44-45:
Connect new information to category failure.
Example:
"Fountains can't fix this‚Äîmoving water doesn't add minerals.
Wet food can't fix this‚Äîit doesn't train drinking behavior.
Supplements can't fix this‚Äîflavor ‚âÝ mineral signature.
The entire category was addressing delivery and appeal.
But the problem was biological recognition."

Micro-Step 6: Introduce YOUR Different Approach - Paragraphs 46-48:
Show how YOUR mechanism addresses what category can't.

Structure:
‚óè‚Äã What you found
‚óè‚Äã How it's different (not better‚ÄîDIFFERENT)
‚óè‚Äã The mechanism it uses
Example:
"That's when I found Feline Hydration Plus.
Not a fountain. Not a flavor. Not wet food.
An electrolyte powder.
Sodium, potassium, and magnesium in the exact ratios found in prey blood.
You add one scoop to their water bowl.
It dissolves completely. Tasteless to humans.
But to your cat's mineral receptors? It's the signature they evolved to recognize as proper
hydration."

Micro-Step 7: Show Early Proof - Paragraphs 49-50:
Initial results that prove the difference.
Example:
"I added it to Max's regular bowl. Not a fountain. Just his normal ceramic bowl with one scoop of
powder.
Within 20 minutes, he drank.
Actually drank. A full drink, not just a sniff and walk away.
I recorded it because I couldn't believe it.
Day 3: He was drinking 3-4 times daily. Behavior I'd never seen in the six months I'd been trying
fountains.
Week 2: Vet appointment. Bloodwork.
Creatinine: 3.8 ‚Üí 2.9

BUN: 42 ‚Üí 34
'Whatever you're doing,' his vet said, 'keep doing it.'"

THE TRANSFORMATION SECTION (Paragraphs 51-70)
Paragraphs 51-60: SHOW THE RESULT DELIVERED
Timeline of transformation.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Day 1 result
Week 1-2 results
Month 1 results
Month 3 results (if relevant)
Contrast with category solutions

Example:
"Here's what happened over the next 30 days:
Day 1: First real drink in months. Recorded it at 11:43 AM.
Day 3: Drinking 3-4x daily. More than he'd drunk from any fountain, ever.
Week 2: Bloodwork improving. Creatinine dropping. BUN normalizing.
Week 4: Back to normal kidney values. His vet couldn't believe it.
Month 2: Maintaining healthy kidney function. Drinking daily. No decline.
The result I'd spent $800 and six months trying to get with fountains, wet food, and
supplements?
I got it in two weeks with the right electrolyte ratios.
Because I finally addressed what his biology needed‚Äînot what blog posts said cats prefer."

Paragraphs 61-65: EXPLAIN THE MECHANISM (Now That They Believe)
Now that they believe it works, explain WHY more deeply.

Structure:
‚óè‚Äã How it works
‚óè‚Äã Why it delivers the result
‚óè‚Äã The science behind it
Example:
"Here's why it works:
Feline Hydration Plus contains sodium (45mg), potassium (38mg), and magnesium (12mg) per
scoop.
Those are the exact ratios found in mouse blood‚Äîthe prey cats evolved eating.
When Max drinks water with these minerals, his taste receptors detect the signature.
His brain receives the signal: 'Proper hydration detected.'
He drinks.
Not because the water tastes good. Because his biology recognizes it as the fluid he's
supposed to drink.
Fountains couldn't do this. Wet food couldn't do this. Flavored supplements couldn't do this.
Because none of them addressed the mineral-recognition mechanism."

Paragraphs 66-70: THE OFFER
Present product, price, guarantee.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

What they get
Price
What's included
Guarantee tied to the result
Bonuses

Example:
"You can get Feline Hydration Plus right now.
30-day supply: $34.99

Includes:
- Electrolyte powder (sodium/potassium/magnesium in prey-blood ratios)
- Usage guide
- Hydration tracking sheet
90-day money-back guarantee.
If your cat doesn't drink more within 2 weeks, full refund.
If their kidney values don't improve (if they have kidney disease), full refund.
We even cover return shipping.
Why?
Because if prey-model electrolytes work for 94% of cats in clinical studies, they'll work for yours.
And if they don't, you shouldn't pay.
[CTA: Get Feline Hydration Plus]"

CLOSING: The Result Is Achievable
Structure:
Paragraphs 71-75: RETURN TO CATEGORY FAILURE
Remind them why the category can't work.
Example:
"You can keep trying fountains.
Keep spending $40-90 per fountain, hoping the next brand will be different.
Keep reading articles that recommend the same solutions you've already tried.
But now you know:
Fountains change delivery. Your cat's biology needs composition.
It's not about making water more appealing. It's about making water biologically recognizable.

The category can't solve this."

Paragraphs 76-80: THE CHOICE
Give them control.
Example:
"You have two choices:
Option 1: Keep trying solutions in the same category.
Maybe the fourth fountain will work. Maybe a different wet food brand. Maybe if you just keep
searching.
But you know why they're failing now. You know they can't address mineral recognition.
Option 2: Try the solution that addresses what fountains can't.
Add prey-model electrolytes. Trigger the biological recognition. See if your cat drinks within 48
hours like Max did.
$34.99. 90-day guarantee. If it doesn't work, you pay nothing.
The result you want‚Äîyour cat drinking enough to keep their kidneys healthy‚Äîis achievable.
You just need the right mechanism.
[CTA: Get Feline Hydration Plus - 90-Day Guarantee]
[Signature]
P.S. - The 90-day guarantee means zero risk. Try it. Track their water intake for two weeks.
Check their bloodwork if they have kidney disease. If you don't see improvement, get every
penny back. But if the UC Davis research is right (and it is), you'll finally get the result fountains
couldn't deliver."

VOICE & TONE
Required voice:
‚óè‚Äã First person (I/me/my) - this is YOUR story
‚óè‚Äã Frustrated/exhausted (I tried everything)

‚óè‚Äã Skeptical but determined (kept trying)
‚óè‚Äã "I've been where you are" positioning
‚óè‚Äã Finally relieved/vindicated (found what works)
Solution-Aware voice is DIFFERENT from:
‚óè‚Äã Problem-Aware: Resigned, then hopeful
‚óè‚Äã Solution-Aware: Frustrated, then vindicated
Solution-Aware voice: "I tried everything. Nothing delivered the result. Then I found what
actually works - here's WHY it works when others didn't."
Sentence structure: Mix of frustration listing and explanation

PART 5: OUTPUT
Claude, now write the complete ad copy following all instructions above.

‚úÖ

8.2 - Problem-Solution

PROMPT #8.2: SOLUTION-AWARE AD
COPY GENERATOR
(PROBLEM-SOLUTION LEAD)
1. UPLOAD YOUR RESEARCH DOCUMENTS:
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Document: [ATTACH FILE]
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Language Document: [ATTACH FILE]
‚óè‚Äã Mass Desire Research Document: [ATTACH FILE]

2. SPECIFY YOUR TARGET:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Mass Desire You're Targeting: [INSERT MASS DESIRE]
Macro-Avatar You‚Äôre Targeting (Optional): [INSERT AVATAR # OR NAME]
Product Name: [YOUR PRODUCT]
What's Included: [Main product + included items]
Guarantee: [Terms - days, conditions, refund policy]
Bonuses (if any): [List with values]
Price + Offer: [Listing price + Discount]

3. YOUR ANGLE:
Current Belief: [What they currently believe that keeps them stuck]
New Information: [The specific insight/fact that contradicts their belief]
Resulting Belief-Shift: [What they now realize/conclude]

4. SOLUTIONS THEY'VE TRIED (CRITICAL FOR SOLUTION-AWARE):
List the actual products/methods they purchased and tried:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

[Brand/product name - Example: "PetSafe fountain"]
[Brand/product name - Example: "Catit fountain"]
[Brand/product name - Example: "Switched to 100% wet food"]
[etc.]

5. THE POINT OF MAXIMUM ANXIETY:

The Worst Moment: [When do they feel this problem most acutely? What's the specific
moment that keeps them up at night?]
‚óè‚Äã Example: "3 AM, lying awake, wondering if this will ever get better. The moment they
realize they've tried everything and nothing worked."
The Hidden Emotion: [What do they FEEL in that moment that they won't say out loud?]
‚óè‚Äã Example: "Hopelessness. Shame about not being able to fix something so 'simple.' Fear
that they're broken."
The Private Thought: [What have they never told anyone?]
‚óè‚Äã Example: "I've never told anyone this, but I'm starting to think this is just how my life is
going to be."

I'm going to give you everything you need to write Solution-Aware ad copy using the
Problem-Solution Lead approach.
Here's what's happening:
You've done your research. You know your avatar, their problem, and what desire you're
targeting. Most importantly, you know they've TRIED multiple solutions that all failed. They're not
just aware of the problem‚Äîthey're frustrated, exhausted, and scared. Now you need ad copy
that shows you feel their pain before revealing the solution.
This prompt is flexible - you can use it for:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Testing angles - Write multiple versions with different belief-shifts
Testing desires - Same angle, different underlying desires
Testing empathy approaches - Same angle, different emotional entry points
Scaling winners - Create variations of what's already working

What's Solution-Aware?
Solution-Aware prospects know they have a problem, know the result they want, and know
solutions exist. They've actively tried multiple products/methods. None delivered the result.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Cat owner tried 3 fountains, wet food, supplements - cat still won't drink, kidneys
declining
‚óè‚Äã Woman tried Venus, Billie, electric razors - still gets razor burn with all of them
‚óè‚Äã Insomniac tried melatonin, sleep apps, white noise machines - still can't sleep
‚óè‚Äã Person tried keto, paleo, Whole30 - still can't lose weight

They're frustrated, exhausted, and skeptical. But unlike Story Lead (which is about the journey),
Problem-Solution Lead is about the PAIN first.
What's the Problem-Solution Lead approach?
Problem-Solution Lead = "I feel your pain. I see what you're going through. Here's why solutions
failed. Here's what actually works."
It's empathy-first and pain-focused. You validate their emotional struggle before revealing the
mechanism.
When to use Problem-Solution Lead vs. Story Lead (both Solution-Aware):
Use PROBLEM-SOLUTION LEAD when:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

The EMOTIONAL PAIN is more dominant than curiosity
They're scared, desperate, or in crisis mode
The problem keeps them up at night with dread/fear/guilt
They need to feel understood before they'll trust you

Use STORY LEAD when:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

They're frustrated but investigative
The appeal is "finally understanding why"
They want mechanism revelation through narrative
Less emotional intensity, more analytical interest

What's an angle?
Your angle is the new information that shifts their belief about why solutions failed and what
actually works.
It's made of three parts:
1.‚Äã Current Belief - What they think now that keeps them stuck
2.‚Äã New Information - The specific insight that contradicts that belief
3.‚Äã Resulting Belief-Shift - What they realize after learning the new info
For Solution-Aware Problem-Solution, the angle often explains why the entire category they
tried can't work.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Pet: Current belief: "I just need to find the right fountain" ‚Üí New info: "Cats have mineral
receptors that plain water doesn't trigger‚Äîno fountain changes mineral composition" ‚Üí
Belief-shift: "It's not about delivery‚Äîit's about what water IS made of. Fountains can't fix
this."

‚óè‚Äã Beauty: Current belief: "I need better technique or the right razor" ‚Üí New info: "ALL
razors traumatize living skin tissue‚Äîinflammation is your body's automatic response to
blade trauma" ‚Üí Belief-shift: "It's not my technique‚Äîevery razor causes this biologically.
No razor solves it."
What's the Point of Maximum Anxiety?
From Masterson: "The point of maximum anxiety" - the worst moment, the deepest fear, the
thing they think about at 3 AM.
Finding this unlocks empathy that opens doors.
What I'm going to do:
1.‚Äã Extract your inputs (avatar, desire, angle, solutions tried, point of maximum anxiety)
2.‚Äã Determine market sophistication (based on # of solutions tried)
3.‚Äã Generate 10-15 headline options using Schwartz sophistication frameworks
4.‚Äã You pick your favorite headline
5.‚Äã I write complete ad copy (2,000-2,400 words) using empathy-first gradualization
The copy follows Problem-Solution framework: Emotional recognition ‚Üí Problem validation ‚Üí
Anxiety acknowledgment ‚Üí Mechanism revelation ‚Üí Hope introduction ‚Üí Proof ‚Üí Offer.

CRITICAL - SCHWARTZ SOLUTION-AWARE HEADLINE PRINCIPLES
From Breakthrough Advertising:
Solution-Aware headlines must:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Crystallize the EMOTIONAL DESIRE (Level 2), not logical want (Level 1)
State the END STATE/RESULT, not the journey
NO mechanism explanation (that's for body copy)
NO story/journey ("I tried X then Y" is body copy)

‚óè‚Äã NO comparison to competitors ("when fountains fail" is body copy)
From Schwartz: "Sometimes the simplest statement of the desire is the best."
Examples of correct Solution-Aware headlines:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"How to win friends and influence people" (pure emotional desire)
"Who else wants a whiter wash‚Äîwith no hard work?" (desire + ease)
"When doctors feel rotten‚Äîthis is what they do" (authority + identification)
"To people who want to write‚Äîbut can't get started" (identification + stuck point)

‚ùå
‚ùå
REQUIRED in headlines: ‚úÖ Crystallize Level 2 emotional desire ‚úÖ State END STATE ‚úÖ
Match sophistication level ‚úÖ Create emotional recognition
‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

FORBIDDEN in headlines:
Mention competitor products or failed solutions
Explain
mechanisms
Tell the story/journey
Use excessive em dashes
State Level 1 logical
want instead of Level 2 emotional desire

SCHWARTZ'S HEADLINE STRENGTHENING TECHNIQUES
Based on sophistication level, apply these techniques:
Available Techniques:
1.‚Äã VERBALIZATION (Make Desire Specific)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Add numbers, timeframes, specific outcomes
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Save your cat's kidneys in 30 days"
2.‚Äã INTENSIFICATION (Dramatize The Desire)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Heighten emotional stakes
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Stop watching your cat die"
3.‚Äã PERSONALIZATION (Identify WHO)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Specific identification with emotional state
‚óã‚Äã Example: "To cat owners watching their baby slowly die"
4.‚Äã DRAMATIZATION (Add Scenario/Moment)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Frame as pivotal moment with setup + payoff
‚óã‚Äã Example: "The vet said nothing more we can do‚Äîbut my cat proved him wrong"
5.‚Äã PROOF ELEMENTS (Add Credibility)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Add guarantee, authority, statistics, timeframe
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Or your money back"

6.‚Äã WONDERMENT (Impossible-Seeming Result)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Present surprising/counterintuitive combination
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Who ever heard of reversing kidney disease after vets give up?"
7.‚Äã IDENTIFICATION + EMOTIONAL STATE‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Name them + their painful emotional state
‚óã‚Äã Example: "To cat owners who dread every vet visit"
8.‚Äã FUTURE STATE PROJECTION‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Paint the future they desire (often uses "again" = restoration)
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Get your healthy cat back"
9.‚Äã QUESTION FORM (Identifying Pain)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Question that identifies specific painful situation
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Do you dread vet visits because you're watching your cat die?"
10.‚ÄãCOMMAND FORM (Direct Action)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Direct command + transformation
‚óã‚Äã Example: "Stop watching your cat die and start saving his kidneys"
11.‚ÄãHOW-TO FORMAT (Classic Schwartz)‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Simple, direct format crystallizing desire
‚óã‚Äã Example: "How to save your cat from kidney disease"

MAP SOPHISTICATION TO TECHNIQUES
STAGE 3 SOPHISTICATION (1-2 products tried):
‚óè‚Äã Lower skepticism = simpler headlines work
‚óè‚Äã Use: Verbalization + Simple desire statement
STAGE 4 SOPHISTICATION (3-4 products tried):
‚óè‚Äã Higher skepticism = need proof + drama
‚óè‚Äã Use: Verbalization + Proof + Dramatization
STAGE 5 SOPHISTICATION (5+ products tried):
‚óè‚Äã Highest skepticism = need identification + emotional drama
‚óè‚Äã Use: Identification + Dramatization + Emotional State

WINNING HEADLINE FRAMEWORKS
Framework 1: Simple How-To + Emotional Desire
‚óè‚Äã Template: "How To [EMOTIONAL RESULT]"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 3-4
Framework 2: Identification + Future State
‚óè‚Äã Template: "To [WHO] Who Want [EMOTIONAL END STATE]"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 4-5
Framework 3: Verbalized Result + Proof
‚óè‚Äã Template: "[SPECIFIC RESULT] In [TIME]‚ÄîOr Your Money Back"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 4
Framework 4: Who Else Wants + Emotional Desire
‚óè‚Äã Template: "Who Else Wants [EMOTIONAL RESULT]?"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 3-4
Framework 5: Dramatized Moment
‚óè‚Äã Template: "They [EMBARRASSMENT]‚ÄîBut [TRIUMPH]!"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 4-5
Framework 6: Identification + Stuck Point
‚óè‚Äã Template: "To [WHO] Who [SPECIFIC PROBLEM/BEHAVIOR]"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 5
Framework 7: When Authority + Solution
‚óè‚Äã Template: "When [AUTHORITY] [PROBLEM]‚ÄîThis Is What They Do"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 4-5
Framework 8: Command + Transformation
‚óè‚Äã Template: "Stop [PAINFUL BEHAVIOR] And Start [DESIRED STATE]"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 4-5
Framework 9: Personal Testimony + Result + Time
‚óè‚Äã Template: "How I [GOT RESULT] In [TIMEFRAME]"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 4
Framework 10: Question Identifying Pain

‚óè‚Äã Template: "Do You [PAINFUL BEHAVIOR] Because [EMOTIONAL REASON]?"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 5
Framework 11: The Moment + Emotional Restoration
‚óè‚Äã Template: "The Moment [EMOTIONAL BREAKTHROUGH]"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 5
Framework 12: Wonderment + Impossibility
‚óè‚Äã Template: "Who Ever Heard Of [IMPOSSIBILITY]?"
‚óè‚Äã When to use: Stage 4

HEADLINE: Crystallized Emotional Desire
From Schwartz: Solution-Aware headlines must crystallize EMOTIONAL desire (Level 2), not
logical want (Level 1).

OPENING: Emotional Recognition
Structure:
Paragraphs 1-5: NAME THE CORE EMOTION
Open by naming the FEELING they're experiencing, not just the problem.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Start with the emotion (from point of maximum anxiety)
Show you understand the pain
Use their exact language
Validate it's real

Example:
"You're watching him die.

That's what it feels like, isn't it?

Every day, you see him decline a little more. The energy drains out of him. He sleeps more.
Moves less.

And you know what's happening.

His kidneys are failing.

And you can't stop it.

You stand there at the water bowl, staring at the level. He barely touched it today. Again.

You know he needs to drink. You know that's the one thing that could help.

But he won't.

And you feel helpless.

Not just sad. Helpless.

Like you're failing the one creature who depends on you completely. Who trusts you to take care
of him. Who's been there for you through everything.

And now, when he needs you most, you can't save him."

‚ùå

DON'T:
Start with the surface problem
the emotional validation

‚ùå Jump to solution ‚ùå Use clinical language ‚ùå Skip

DO: ‚úì Name the feeling first ‚úì Use "you" language (second person) ‚úì Pull from point of
maximum anxiety ‚úì Show you understand the depth of pain

Paragraphs 6-10: PROBLEM VALIDATION
Acknowledge what they've tried. Show it's not their fault.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

List solutions they tried (from user input)
Show how each failed
Validate they did everything right
Remove self-blame

Example:
"You've tried everything, haven't you?

You bought the fancy fountain. The one with three levels and the little waterfall. $42.99. The
reviews said cats LOVE moving water.

He drank from it for one day. Then ignored it completely.

So you switched to wet food. Every meal. Even though it's expensive and messy and he picks at
it half the time.

It helped a little. But it's not enough. He's still dehydrated. The vet still says his kidney values
are getting worse.

You tried the hydration supplements. $50 for a bottle. Fought with him every day to get him to
take them.

He hated them. You hated the fights. And his numbers barely budged.

Nothing works.

And meanwhile, he's getting worse.

It's not your fault.

You've done everything any responsible cat owner would do. You've followed every piece of
advice. You've spent the money. You've tried the solutions.

But none of them actually FIX the problem."

‚ùå

DON'T:
Skip the specific products
through this section

‚ùå Generalize what they tried ‚ùå Blame them ‚ùå Rush

DO: ‚úì Name specific solutions (from user input) ‚úì Show how each failed ‚úì Include
prices/effort if relevant ‚úì Validate they're not the problem

Paragraphs 11-15: ANXIETY ACKNOWLEDGMENT
Touch the point of maximum anxiety. Name the hidden fear.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

The worst moment (from user input)
The hidden emotion (from user input)
The private thought (from user input)
Don't linger‚Äîacknowledge and transition

Example:
"You know where this is going, don't you?

At some point‚Äîmaybe in six months, maybe in a year‚Äîyou're going to be sitting in the vet's
office while they tell you his kidney function has dropped too far.

That there's nothing more they can do.

That it's time to think about quality of life.

And you're going to have to make that decision.

The one you've been dreading since the first time they said 'kidney disease.'

You're going to have to decide when to let him go.

And for the rest of your life, you're going to wonder: Could I have saved him?

If I'd found the right solution sooner, could he still be here?

That's the thought that keeps you up at 3 AM, isn't it?

That you're going to lose him to something preventable.

And that you'll never forgive yourself.

But here's what you don't know."

CRITICAL: Don't wallow here. Touch it, validate it, then transition to mechanism.

‚ùå

‚ùå Linger too long (max 5 paragraphs) ‚ùå Dismiss their fear ‚ùå

DON'T:
Skip this section
Amplify it beyond what's real

DO: ‚úì Name the specific fear (from user input) ‚úì Use their private thoughts ‚úì Validate it's real
‚úì Transition to "here's why"

THE MECHANISM REVELATION SECTION (Paragraphs 16-40)
Paragraphs 16-25: INTRODUCE NEW INFORMATION
This is where you introduce the new information from the angle.

Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"Here's what you don't know"
Present the mechanism/insight
Show why old solutions can't work
Make it easy to accept

Example:
"Here's what nobody told you about why your cat won't drink:

It's not about the delivery method.

It's not about flavor.

It's about mineral composition.

Cats have taste receptors‚Äîspecialized cells on their tongues‚Äîthat evolved to detect specific
minerals in water.

Specifically, sodium and potassium.

Why? Because cats evolved as desert hunters. Their natural prey‚Äîbirds, rodents,
reptiles‚Äîprovided most of their hydration through blood.

And blood has a very specific mineral composition. Sodium and potassium in precise ratios.

Your cat's taste receptors evolved to recognize THAT specific composition as 'proper hydration.'

When they detect it, the receptors send a signal to the brain: 'This is the hydration your body
needs. Drink.'

But here's the problem:

Plain water‚Äîeven from a fountain, even with wet food‚Äîlacks these minerals.

So when your cat drinks plain water, those receptors detect the ABSENCE of minerals.

And they send a different signal: 'This is not proper hydration. Don't drink much.'

That's why he barely drinks.

Not because he's stubborn. Not because he doesn't like the fountain.

Because his biology is telling him that plain water isn't the hydration his body needs."

Paragraphs 26-30: CONNECT TO WHY SOLUTIONS FAILED
Now explain why each solution they tried couldn't address this.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Go through each solution category
Show why it can't solve the real problem
Remove brand blame
Validate their experience

Example:
"Now you understand why fountains don't work.

They change HOW the water is delivered. Moving water, filters, different heights.

But they don't change WHAT the water is made of.

The mineral composition is still wrong. So your cat's receptors still signal 'not proper hydration.'

And he still doesn't drink enough.

Same reason wet food doesn't fully solve it.

Wet food provides 60-70% of hydration needs. Which is good!

But cats still need to actively drink to hit 100%.

And if plain water doesn't trigger the 'proper hydration' response, they stay chronically
under-hydrated.

Even on full wet food diets, cats with kidney disease need MORE water than wet food alone
provides.

This is why his kidney values keep declining even though you're doing everything right.

You're giving him access to water. You're trying different delivery methods.

But you're not giving him water his biology recognizes as proper hydration."

Paragraphs 31-40: SHOW HOW YOUR SOLUTION WORKS
Present your mechanism and why it addresses what others miss.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"So how do you actually fix this?"
Your mechanism
Authority validation
Why this delivers the result

Example:
"So how do you actually fix this?

You make the mineral composition of water match what his receptors are looking for.

Prey-model electrolytes.

Sodium, potassium, and magnesium in the same ratios found in prey blood.

Add this to his water bowl, and suddenly his taste receptors detect: 'These are the minerals I
need. This IS proper hydration.'

The signal to his brain changes from 'don't drink much' to 'drink.'

And he drinks 3-4 times more water than he did before.

Not because the fountain got fancier.

Not because you forced him.

Because his own biology is now telling him to drink.

Dr. Sarah Chen at UC Davis tested this directly.

She took two groups of cats. Group 1 got plain water‚Äîsome from bowls, some from fountains.

Group 2 got water with prey-model electrolyte ratios added.

Everything else identical.

Result?

Group 2 consumed 3.2 times more water than Group 1.

Voluntarily.

Published in Journal of Feline Medicine and Surgery, 2019.

This is what you've been missing.

Not a better fountain. Not more wet food.

The mineral composition that triggers the biological hydration response."

THE PROOF SECTION (Paragraphs 41-55)
Structure:
Paragraphs 41-50: TRANSFORMATION TIMELINE
Show the progression from trying your solution to getting the result.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Day 1 result
Week 1-2 results
Month 1 results
Contrast with category solutions

Example:
"826 cat owners have done this.

Here's what happened:

Average increase in water consumption: 3.1x within the first week.

Not months. Days.

78% saw improved kidney values within 30 days. Documented by their veterinarians.

The progression usually looks like this:

Day 1: You add the electrolytes to his water bowl. Within a few hours‚Äîusually 20-30
minutes‚Äîhe drinks.

Not a tiny sip. A real drink.

You stand there watching him, not quite believing it.

Day 3: He's drinking 3-4 times daily. Every time you check the bowl, the level is lower.

You're refilling it more often than you have in years.

Week 2: First vet checkup since you started. You're nervous.

The vet looks at his blood work.

'His kidney values are improving.'

You hear those words and you could cry.

Month 1: Values in normal range. Or close enough that the vet says 'keep doing whatever you're
doing.'

His energy is back. He's playing again.

You realize you haven't felt that knot of dread in weeks.

Because he's going to be okay."

Paragraphs 51-55: BEFORE/AFTER EMOTIONAL STATE
Show the emotional transformation, not just physical.
Example:
"This is the transformation:

From: Watching him barely drink. Kidney values declining. Feeling helpless. Dreading the
conversation about quality of life.

To: He drinks daily like it's normal. Kidney values stable or improving. His energy back. You can
breathe again.

The thing you wanted most‚Äîto save him‚Äîis actually possible.

And it took 30 days."

Close on the emotional transformation.

9 - Unaware

PROMPT #9: AD COPY FOR UNAWARE
I'm going to give you everything you need to write Unaware ad copy for me. This is for markets
where people don't know they have a problem yet ‚Äî they have feelings, fears, and experiences
they've normalized but haven't labeled as a problem that needs solving.
Important: Unaware markets cannot be traditionally "researched" because research captures
conscious problems, stated desires, and tried solutions. Unaware people don't have these yet.
[ATTACH MACRO AVATAR LANGUAGE DOC]
1. My Targeting Specifications:
‚óè‚Äã Mass Desire I'm Targeting: [INSERT MASS DESIRE]

together]

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

What makes this UNIVERSAL (why many people feel this way)

‚óè‚Äã What they currently believe explains their experience
‚óè‚Äã Why this belief is reasonable (validates them)

STEP 2: GENERATE THE HIDDEN PROBLEM MECHANISM
For Unaware markets, you must GENERATE the mechanism that will lead them into realizing
they have a problem. This is different from Problem-Aware where the mechanism explains a
known problem.
Your job is to create:
A. The Hidden Problem What is actually happening that they don't know about? This must:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Connect their surface experience (Identification) to something deeper
Be something they're NOT currently aware of
Explain why their Current Attribution is incomplete
Be verifiable once revealed

Ask yourself: "What's the real reason behind their experience that they can't see?"
B. The UMP (Unique Mechanism Behind the Problem) How does this hidden problem
actually work? This must:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Explain the mechanism causing the hidden problem
Be simple enough to understand in one conversation
Make their experience suddenly make sense
Create an "oh shit" moment of realization

Ask yourself: "What's the mechanism that, once explained, makes everything click?"
C. Observable Proof How can they verify this hidden problem exists in their own life? This
must:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Be something they can check themselves
Be observable within 60 seconds
Not require special equipment or expertise
Make the hidden problem undeniable once seen

Ask yourself: "What can they look at RIGHT NOW to see this is true?"
D. The UMS (Unique Mechanism Behind the Solution) How does the product address this
hidden problem? This must:
‚óè‚Äã Directly address the UMP

‚óè‚Äã Make the solution feel inevitable ("of course, if THAT'S the problem, then THIS is the
solution")
‚óè‚Äã Be simple and logical
Ask yourself: "If the hidden problem works like X, what would logically fix it?"

MECHANISM GENERATION FRAMEWORK:
Think through this sequence:
1.‚Äã Surface Experience: They feel [Identification]
2.‚Äã False Attribution: They think it's because [Current Attribution]
3.‚Äã Hidden Problem: But actually, [what's really happening that they don't know]
4.‚Äã Mechanism: This happens because [UMP - how/why it works]
5.‚Äã Observable Proof: They can verify this by [what they can check right now]
6.‚Äã Solution Logic: So the fix is [UMS - how product addresses UMP]
7.‚Äã Emotional Payoff: Which gives them [Level 2 emotional desire]

STEP 3: VALIDATE ALIGNMENT
Before proceeding to write, check:
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Is the identification truly UNAWARE? (They feel something but don't label it as a
problem)
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Does the identification connect to a primal fear/desire or universal experience?
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Does the hidden problem mechanism reveal something they genuinely don't know?
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Can they verify the hidden problem once it's revealed?
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Does the UMS clearly address the hidden problem the UMP reveals?
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Is the mechanism NOT something they've already heard? (If common knowledge,
they're not Unaware)
CRITICAL UNAWARE TEST:
Ask yourself: "Does this person currently believe they have a problem that needs solving?"
‚óè‚Äã If YES ‚Üí They're Problem-Aware, use the Problem-Aware prompt instead
‚óè‚Äã If NO ‚Üí They're Unaware, proceed with this prompt
If you detect misalignment, STOP and alert me with specific concerns before proceeding.

STEP 4: PRESENT VALIDATION
Show this confirmation block:
VALIDATION COMPLETE - READY TO WRITE

Mass Desire: [Statement]
- Level 1 (Surface Want): [What they say they want]
- Level 2 (Emotional Drive): [Why they actually want it]

Identification: [The feeling/fear/experience]
Current Attribution: [What they think explains it]

GENERATED MECHANISM:
- Hidden Problem: [What they don't know is happening]
- UMP: [Mechanism that reveals the problem]
- Observable Proof: [How they can verify it]
- UMS: [Mechanism that solves it]

Alignment Check:
‚úì Identification is truly Unaware (not Problem-Aware)
‚úì Connects to primal fear/desire or universal experience
‚úì Hidden problem is genuinely unknown to them
‚úì Observable proof exists once revealed
‚úì Solution mechanism addresses hidden problem

Proceeding to headline generation...

PHASE 2: HEADLINE GENERATION
Generate 10-15 headline options based on the identification and generated mechanism.

CRITICAL HEADLINE RULES FOR UNAWARE:
RULE 1: NEVER NAME THE PROBLEM OR SOLUTION

‚ùå DON'T mention: the hidden problem, the product category, medical terms, technical
mechanisms

‚úÖ DO call out: emotions, behaviors, identity, surface experiences, universal feelings
RULE 2: CALL OUT THE IDENTIFICATION
Your headlines must connect to what they FEEL/FEAR/EXPERIENCE, not what's WRONG.
They should think: "Yes, that's me" ‚Äî NOT "Oh, I have that problem"
RULE 3: USE DIVERSE CLASSIC FORMATS
Don't default to one pattern. Use patterns from multiple groups below. Maximum 2 headlines
can use the same pattern.
RULE 4: TEST EACH HEADLINE
Before including a headline, ask yourself:
‚óè‚Äã Does this name the hidden problem? (If YES ‚Üí reject)
‚óè‚Äã Does this name the solution/product category? (If YES ‚Üí reject)
‚óè‚Äã Would someone who doesn't know they have this problem identify with this? (If NO ‚Üí
reject)
‚óè‚Äã Have I already used this pattern twice? (If YES ‚Üí use different pattern)
