‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"[RESULT] IN [TIME PERIOD] OR YOUR MONEY BACK"
"GUARANTEED TO [BENEFIT] OR [CONSEQUENCE]"
"[NUMBER] REASONS WHY IT WOULD HAVE PAID YOU TO [ACTION]"
"MAKE THIS [TIME]-MINUTE TEST"
"TAKE ANY [NUMBER] OF THESE [THINGS] FOR ONLY [PRICE]"

GROUP K: QUESTIONS/CHALLENGES (100 Greatest)
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"HOW OFTEN DO YOU HEAR YOURSELF SAYING: '[COMMON EXCUSE]'"
"ARE WE [NEGATIVE GROUP LABEL]?"
"ARE THEY BEING [NEGATIVE OUTCOME] RIGHT OVER YOUR HEAD?"
"DID YOU EVER SEE [UNUSUAL THING]?"
"WHERE YOU [VERB] [THING]?"
"WHAT [REACTION] WHEN [EVENT]?"

GROUP L: TIME/URGENCY (Schwartz)
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"THE [TIME] WHEN YOU REALIZE [TRUTH]"
"THERE'S A MOMENT AT [TIME] WHEN [SITUATION]"
"THE MORNING/NIGHT YOU [EXPERIENCE]"
"WHEN [SITUATION] DOESN'T FEEL [EXPECTED STATE] ANYMORE"

GROUP M: EMOTIONAL PROJECTIONS (Schwartz)
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"THE EMOTION OF [SITUATION]"
"THE FEELING OF [MOMENT]"
"WHEN YOU LOVE SOMETHING SO MUCH YOU'RE [FEARFUL STATE]"
"THE WEIGHT OF [BURDEN]"
"WHAT IT FEELS LIKE TO [SITUATION]"

GROUP N: IDENTIFICATION SPECIFIC (For Unaware)
For Primal Fear:
‚óè‚Äã "TO [WHO] WHO WORRY ABOUT [FEAR]"
‚óè‚Äã "THE [FEAR] THAT KEEPS [WHO] UP AT NIGHT"
For Primal Desire:
‚óè‚Äã "TO [WHO] WHO WANT [DESIRE] AGAIN"
‚óè‚Äã "REMEMBER WHEN YOU HAD [DESIRED STATE]?"
For Universal Experience:
‚óè‚Äã "TO [WHO]: IT'S NOT [THEIR ATTRIBUTION]"
‚óè‚Äã "WHAT IF [EXPERIENCE] ISN'T [WHAT THEY THINK]?"

For Normalized Behavior:
‚óè‚Äã "WHO ELSE [DOES BEHAVIOR] WITHOUT KNOWING WHY?"
‚óè‚Äã "I [DO BEHAVIOR] TOO. HERE'S WHAT I LEARNED."
For Hidden Resentment:
‚óè‚Äã "WHY [OTHERS] HAVE [DESIRED STATE] AND YOU DON'T"
‚óè‚Äã "WHAT [THEY] KNOW THAT YOU DON'T"

OUTPUT FORMAT:
For EACH headline you generate:
Headline [#]: [The actual headline text]
Pattern: [Pattern name from above - be specific]
Specificity Check: [One sentence: Is this specific enough for avatar recognition but broad
enough for Unaware?]
Why it works: [2-3 sentences explaining the psychology - why an UNAWARE person would
identify without knowing they have a problem]

YOUR TASK:
Generate 12-15 headlines. Ensure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Pattern Diversity: Use patterns from at LEAST 8 different groups (A through N)
No Repetition: Each specific pattern can only be used ONCE
Unaware Test: None name medical problems/products/the hidden problem
All pass the 4 tests in Rule 4 above
Balance: Mix emotional (Groups L, M), financial (Group G), behavioral (Groups A, F),
and proof-based (Groups C, K) patterns

Before submitting your headline set, verify:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

[ ] I used patterns from 8+ different letter groups (A-N)
[ ] No pattern was used twice
[ ] No headline names a medical condition, product, or solution
[ ] No headline names the HIDDEN PROBLEM
[ ] Each headline would make someone think "that's me" while NOT requiring them to
know they have a problem

After generating headlines, ask: "Which headline do you want to use for your ad copy?"

PHASE 3: BUILD THE STRATEGIC FOUNDATION
Before writing, construct the strategic foundation for leading them from Unaware ‚Üí Aware.

STEP 1: BUILD THE ONE BELIEF
The One Belief Formula for Unaware:
"[SURFACE EXPERIENCE] is actually caused by [HIDDEN PROBLEM via UMP] ‚Äî
[PRODUCT] solves it through [UMS] ‚Äî giving you [LEVEL 2 EMOTIONAL DESIRE]"
Extract and identify:
‚óè‚Äã Surface Experience: What they feel/experience but don't label as a problem
‚óè‚Äã Hidden Problem: What they don't know is happening (revealed by UMP)
‚óè‚Äã Mechanism Behind Problem (UMP): The mechanism that REVEALS the hidden
problem
‚óè‚Äã Solution: Your product name
‚óè‚Äã Mechanism Behind Solution (UMS): How the product addresses the hidden problem
‚óè‚Äã Emotional Desire: The Level 2 emotional desire (not surface want, but deeper
emotional drive)
The One Belief must be built around Level 2 (emotional drive), not Level 1 (surface want).

STEP 2: ENGINEER THE GRADUALIZATION SEQUENCE
For Unaware, gradualization is about leading them INTO problem awareness, not shifting
beliefs about solutions.
CRITICAL DISTINCTION:
‚óè‚Äã Problem-Aware Gradualization: "Here's WHY your known problem happens" (shifting
belief about mechanism)
‚óè‚Äã Unaware Gradualization: "Here's a problem you didn't know you had" (creating
awareness)
Map 5-7 micro-steps that lead from "I feel this but it's normal" ‚Üí "Holy shit, there's an
actual problem I didn't know about":

Micro-Step 1: Validate The Experience
‚óè‚Äã Establish shared experience (I felt this too)
‚óè‚Äã Make them feel seen and understood
‚óè‚Äã DO NOT hint that anything is wrong yet
Micro-Step 2: Show The Attribution
‚óè‚Äã State what they (and you) believed explained it
‚óè‚Äã Validate that belief as reasonable
Micro-Step 3: Introduce Contrast/Doubt
‚óè‚Äã Show something that doesn't fit the attribution
‚óè‚Äã Someone else with same situation but different outcome
‚óè‚Äã A small anomaly that creates curiosity
Micro-Step 4: Deepen The Doubt + Dramatize Emotional Cost
‚óè‚Äã Show the emotional cost of the experience
‚óè‚Äã Make them feel the weight of it
‚óè‚Äã Still no problem revealed ‚Äî just the pain of the experience
Micro-Step 5: Discovery Setup
‚óè‚Äã Natural circumstance leads to new information
‚óè‚Äã Authority figure or credible source introduces mechanism
‚óè‚Äã Frame as curiosity/conversation, not education
Micro-Step 6: Mechanism Reveal (UMP)
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Authority explains the hidden problem
Make it verifiable ‚Äî they can check this themselves
The "holy shit" moment of awareness
This is where you REVEAL the problem they didn't know existed

Micro-Step 7: Personal Verification
‚óè‚Äã Apply the new information to your own situation
‚óè‚Äã Show the proof in your own life
‚óè‚Äã Reader thinks: "Wait, that applies to ME too"
Micro-Step 8: State New Awareness
‚óè‚Äã "That's when I realized..."
‚óè‚Äã Frame as discovery, not pitch
‚óè‚Äã Connect to original experience

STEP 3: IDENTIFY THE TIER OF NEW INFORMATION
The TYPE of new information determines how powerful the belief shift is.
THE 4 TIERS OF NEW INFORMATION:

TIER 1 - Reframe Existing Observable Problem (MOST POWERFUL)
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Take something they already experience and explain it differently
Makes them realize "I've been looking at this wrong the whole time"
No new problem to create awareness of ‚Äî just new understanding of existing reality
They can often verify/observe this themselves immediately

Power: Highest. Reframes their lived experience. Often immediately verifiable.
Best for: Any awareness level, especially Unaware (helps them see what's already happening)

TIER 2 - Explain Why Previous Solutions Failed (VERY POWERFUL)
‚óè‚Äã Reveals why the solutions they tried (or think would work) couldn't work
‚óè‚Äã Removes self-blame and validates their experience
‚óè‚Äã Shows it wasn't their fault ‚Äî the solutions were fundamentally incomplete
Power: Very high. Explains pattern of failure. Creates relief ("it wasn't me").
Best for: Unaware, Problem-Aware, Solution-Aware (validates why nothing worked)
Note for Unaware: Use sparingly ‚Äî they may not have tried formal solutions. But they may
have tried CASUAL attempts.

TIER 3 - Introduce New Mechanism for Familiar Problem (POWERFUL)
‚óè‚Äã Shows there's a different way to solve what they know (or vaguely sense) needs solving
‚óè‚Äã Reveals a mechanism/process they didn't know existed
‚óè‚Äã Positions your solution as different approach, not better execution
Power: Good. Offers new path forward.
Best for: Problem-Aware, Solution-Aware (reveals new solution path)

TIER 4 - Create Awareness of Problem They Didn't Know Existed (NECESSARY FOR
TRUE UNAWARE)
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Introduces entirely new problem they weren't worried about
Requires building awareness before they care
Highest resistance because "I wasn't worried about this before"
BUT: Often necessary for Unaware markets where they don't even know the problem
exists

Power: Lowest in terms of instant believability, BUT necessary for true Unaware markets.
Best for: Unaware markets specifically (when creating problem awareness is required)

FOR UNAWARE MARKETS:
Best: Tier 1 or Tier 4
‚óè‚Äã Tier 1: Reframe something they already observe (most powerful)
‚óè‚Äã Tier 4: Create awareness of hidden problem (necessary when they're truly unaware)
Good: Tier 2 if applicable
‚óè‚Äã If they've tried casual things
Ideal sequence for Unaware:
1.‚Äã Tier 4 in Micro-Step 6 (reveal hidden problem)
2.‚Äã Tier 1 to make it observable (show them proof in their own life)
3.‚Äã Tier 2 if applicable (why their casual attempts failed)
4.‚Äã Tier 3 for UMS (your mechanism)

YOUR JOB in Step 3:
1.‚Äã Identify which tier the new information (UMP) falls into
2.‚Äã State it: "The new information is Tier [X]: [Type]"
3.‚Äã Explain: "This works for Unaware because: [1-2 sentence explanation]"

STEP 4: MAP TO THE 10 QUESTIONS

Using the inputs, prepare to answer these 10 questions sequentially in the copy:
1.‚Äã How is this different? [From Identification + UMP ‚Äî this reveals a problem they didn't
know about]
2.‚Äã What's in it for me? [From Mass Desire ‚Äî BOTH Level 1 and Level 2]
3.‚Äã How do I know this is real? [From Observable Proof, Authority Validation]
4.‚Äã What's holding me back? [For Unaware: Their false attribution / what they THINK
explains it]
5.‚Äã Who/What is to blame? [The hidden mechanism, NOT them]
6.‚Äã Why now? [Consequence of continuing without this knowledge]
7.‚Äã Why should I trust you? [Shared experience + authority validation]
8.‚Äã How does it work? [UMP + UMS explained simply]
9.‚Äã How can I get started? [The offer]
10.‚ÄãWhat do I have to lose? [Guarantee + risk reversal]
CRITICAL FOR Q#2: Q#2 must answer BOTH levels of desire:
‚óè‚Äã Surface benefit (what they get) [Level 1]
‚óè‚Äã Emotional payoff (why they actually want it) [Level 2]
This is where the emotional pivot happens.

STEP 5: VALIDATE BEFORE WRITING
Before writing, confirm:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚úì Does headline call out the identification without naming the hidden problem?
‚úì Will I validate the experience before introducing doubt?
‚úì Have I mapped the gradualization from Unaware ‚Üí Aware?
‚úì Have I identified the tier of new information?
‚úì Will I reveal the hidden problem through discovery, not claims?
‚úì Will the authority explain the UMP in plain language?
‚úì Can the reader verify the hidden problem in their own life?
‚úì Will the solution feel inevitable once the problem is revealed?
‚úì Does One Belief target Level 2 emotional desire?
‚úì Target length: 2,500-3,000 words

PHASE 4: WRITE THE COMPLETE AD COPY
Now write the complete ad following this structure exactly.

CORE STRUCTURE: The 10-Question Framework
Your copy must answer all 10 questions in sequence. Each question builds on the previous one.
Flow:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Q#1-3: Opening/Lead (first 1/3) ‚Äî Hook, promise, proof
Q#4-6: Problem crystallization (middle 1/3) ‚Äî Why they failed, enemy, urgency
Q#7-8: Authority & mechanism (middle 1/3) ‚Äî Credibility, how it works
Q#9-10: Offer & close (final 1/3) ‚Äî No-brainer offer, push-pull close

LENGTH & PACING
Target: 2,500-3,000 words (MAXIMUM 3,000)
Do NOT exceed 3,000 words. Tight is better than comprehensive.
Pacing discipline:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Each emotional beat appears maximum 2x
Discovery story: Key moments only, not play-by-play
Authority dialogue: One good explanation
Transformation timeline: 4-5 data points max

If running long, cut:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Repetitive emotional beats
Over-detailed discovery story
Multiple authority conversations
Excessive transformation data points

OPENING: Story Lead (Confession Style)
Use the confession-style story lead for Unaware markets.
Structure:
1. Identification headline
‚óè‚Äã Call out their surface experience or hidden emotional state

‚óè‚Äã Make them think: "That's me. How did they know?"
2. Confession opening
‚óè‚Äã "There's a moment [specific time] I've never told anyone about..."
‚óè‚Äã OR simply state the experience directly
‚óè‚Äã Extreme specificity: exact times, physical sensations, internal thoughts
3. The experience they've normalized
‚óè‚Äã What were they feeling?
‚óè‚Äã What were they doing?
‚óè‚Äã What did they think explained it?
4. Public vs. Private contrast (if applicable)
‚óè‚Äã What they say to others
‚óè‚Äã What they actually feel
‚óè‚Äã The gap between them

THE EMOTIONAL PIVOT (CRITICAL - Must Happen By Paragraph 7)
From Schwartz (Breakthrough Advertising, pg 52): "There are always TWO levels to every
desire: the stated want and the emotional drive. Your headline identifies Level 1. Your opening
body copy must IMMEDIATELY pivot to Level 2, or you'll get attention but no sales."
From Evaldo (16-Word Sales Letter, pg 37-39): "This is where most copywriters fuck up. They
list surface benefits. NOBODY CARES ABOUT THOSE THINGS. They care about what those
things GIVE them emotionally. The surface benefit is the MECHANISM for the emotional payoff.
If you don't connect to the emotional payoff in Q#2, they won't buy. Period."
From Masterson (Great Leads, pg 78-79): "Weak story leads show only the surface
transformation. Strong story leads show the EMOTIONAL transformation that the surface
change created. Show the surface transformation, but SELL the emotional one. That's what
people actually buy."
ALL THREE FRAMEWORKS TEACH: Surface problem hooks attention. Deep emotion drives
purchase.

Required pivot structure:
Paragraphs 1-3: Surface Experience Identification

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Open with the surface experience
Build recognition ("that's me")
Show public vs private split (if applicable)
Establish the Level 1 stated want

Paragraphs 4-7: THE PIVOT (CRITICAL - THIS IS Q#2)
‚óè‚Äã "But here's what [nobody tells you / I didn't expect / I didn't understand] about [surface
experience]..."
‚óè‚Äã Reveal Level 2 desire (the emotional drive)
‚óè‚Äã Use the pivot moment
‚óè‚Äã Make it physical/visceral (phantom weight, tightness in chest)
‚óè‚Äã Show specific moments that illustrate the emotional loss
‚óè‚Äã This is what they're actually buying for
‚óè‚Äã This answers Q#2: "What's in it for me?" (BOTH surface AND emotional)
After the pivot, everything serves the emotional desire:
‚óè‚Äã All proof should show emotional transformation, not just surface results
‚óè‚Äã Mechanism should explain how it enables emotional payoff
‚óè‚Äã Offer should emphasize emotional benefit

Pivot language examples:
‚óè‚Äã "But here's what nobody tells you about [surface experience]: You don't just lose
[obvious thing]. You lose [deep emotional thing]."
‚óè‚Äã "I thought it was about [surface experience]. It wasn't. It was about [deeper emotional
truth]."
‚óè‚Äã "[Surface experience] was manageable. What I couldn't handle was [emotional
consequence]."
‚óè‚Äã "For [timeframe], I told myself it was just about [surface experience]. But the real
problem was [emotional truth]."
The pivot must:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Happen by paragraph 7 LATEST (preferably paragraph 4-5)
Use sensory/physical details (not intellectual explanation)
Show what they're actually losing (not just inconvenienced by)
Make them FEEL it (chest tightness, throat lump, visceral recognition)
Reference specific relationship/life moments that are lost

IDENTIFICATION TYPE DETERMINES OPENING TONE

If Primal Fear: Open with the fear manifesting in daily life
If Primal Desire: Open with the desire unfulfilled
If Universal Experience: Open with the experience normalized
If Normalized Behavior: Open with the behavior without explanation
If Hidden Resentment: Open with the comparison/frustration

PARAGRAPHS 1-10: VALIDATE THE EXPERIENCE (Identification)
Open by establishing shared experience.
The reader must think: "That's exactly how I feel."
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

State the surface experience in their language
Show you feel/felt it too
Validate their current attribution ("I thought it was just...")
Show the emotional weight of this experience
DO NOT hint that anything is "wrong" yet ‚Äî just shared struggle

PARAGRAPHS 11-15: DRAMATIZE THE EMOTIONAL COST
Show what this experience is costing them.
Not the hidden problem yet ‚Äî just the weight of living with this experience.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Specific moment that captures the cost
What they're missing/losing
The private shame or frustration
Build the emotional stakes before the reveal

PARAGRAPHS 16-35: THE DISCOVERY (Gradualization into Awareness)
This is the CRITICAL section. Execute the gradualization sequence.

You are leading them from "I feel this but it's normal" ‚Üí "There's a hidden problem I didn't know
about."
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Contrast/doubt introduction (someone with different outcome)
Natural discovery setup (conversation, circumstance)
Authority introduces mechanism (UMP)
Make it verifiable
Personal application and verification
New awareness stated as discovery

PACING DISCIPLINE:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

DO NOT reveal the hidden problem before paragraph 25
Each micro-step = 3-5 paragraphs
Frame as investigation/discovery, not education
The reader should feel like they're discovering WITH you

FORBIDDEN patterns:

‚ùå "What I didn't know was..." ‚ùå "The truth is..." ‚ùå "Here's what's really happening..." ‚ùå
Jumping straight to mechanism without building doubt ‚ùå Lecturing or educating before earning
curiosity ‚ùå Stating the hidden problem before paragraph 25
REQUIRED patterns:
‚úì "But I noticed something..." (doubt) ‚úì "How are you not [experiencing this]?" (curiosity) ‚úì
"She looked at me and said..." (authority dialogue) ‚úì "I started tracking/checking/paying
attention..." (verification) ‚úì "That's when I realized..." (discovery)

MICRO-STEP EXECUTION GUIDE:
Micro-Step 1 (Validate Experience) - Paragraphs 1-5:
‚óè‚Äã State the surface experience
‚óè‚Äã Show you feel/felt it too
‚óè‚Äã Reader should think: "That's exactly how I feel"
Micro-Step 2 (Show Attribution) - Paragraphs 6-10:

‚óè‚Äã State what they believe explains it
‚óè‚Äã Validate that belief as reasonable
‚óè‚Äã Reader should think: "Yeah, that's what I thought too"
Micro-Step 3 (Introduce Contrast/Doubt) - Paragraphs 11-15:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Show something that doesn't fit the attribution
Someone else with same situation, different outcome
A small anomaly that creates curiosity
Reader should think: "Huh, that IS weird"

Micro-Step 4 (Deepen Doubt + Dramatize Cost) - Paragraphs 16-20:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Show the emotional cost of the experience
Make them feel the weight of it
Still no problem revealed ‚Äî just the pain
Reader should think: "This is really affecting my life"

Micro-Step 5 (Discovery Setup) - Paragraphs 21-24:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Natural circumstance leads to new information
Authority figure or credible source appears
Frame as curiosity/conversation, not education
Reader should think: "Maybe they know something"

Micro-Step 6 (Mechanism Reveal - UMP) - Paragraphs 25-30:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Authority explains the hidden problem
Make it verifiable ‚Äî they can check this themselves
The "holy shit" moment of awareness
Use authority dialogue to explain mechanism
Reader should think: "Holy shit, I didn't know that"

TIER-SPECIFIC EXECUTION:
If Tier 4 (Create Awareness of Hidden Problem):
‚óè‚Äã Authority reveals problem they didn't know existed
‚óè‚Äã Must make it feel urgent and real
‚óè‚Äã Immediately show how to verify it
If Tier 1 (Reframe Existing Observable):
‚óè‚Äã Show them what to look at in their own life right now
‚óè‚Äã Guide them to see something familiar differently
Micro-Step 7 (Personal Verification) - Paragraphs 31-33:

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Apply new information to your own situation
Show the verification moment
Include concrete details (tracking, checking, testing)
Reader should think: "Wait, that applies to ME too"

Micro-Step 8 (State New Awareness) - Paragraphs 34-35:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"That's when I realized..."
Frame as discovery, not pitch
Connect back to original experience
Reader should think: "Of course! That explains everything!"

SCHWARTZ'S TEST at each micro-step: "Could the reader reject this thought?"
‚óè‚Äã If YES ‚Üí add more setup, slow down, provide more proof
‚óè‚Äã If NO ‚Üí the step is small enough, continue to next step
By paragraph 35, the new awareness should feel like the reader's conclusion, not your pitch.

PARAGRAPHS 36-45: SECOND AUTHORITY VALIDATES + DEEPENS
Optional but powerful: Second authority (doctor, vet, expert) confirms the mechanism.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Seek professional validation
They confirm and deepen the explanation
Connect mechanism to the emotional cost
Remove self-blame ("It's not your fault / your age / your lifestyle")

PARAGRAPHS 46-55: SOLUTION MECHANISM (UMS)
Now introduce the solution through the same discovery frame.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Ask "what do I do?" / "how do I fix this?"
Authority or friend explains the solution mechanism
Make it simple and inevitable
Connect UMS directly to UMP

The logic must feel inevitable:
‚óè‚Äã Hidden problem = [UMP]
‚óè‚Äã Solution = [UMS]
‚óè‚Äã "Of course ‚Äî if THAT'S the problem, then THIS is the solution"

THE ENEMY SECTION (Q#5)
For Unaware, the enemy is usually NOT a competitor or failed solution. It's:
‚óè‚Äã The false attribution ("it's just your age")
‚óè‚Äã The hidden mechanism itself
‚óè‚Äã What kept them from knowing
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Acknowledge the false attribution
Show why it seemed reasonable
Reveal what was actually happening
Remove self-blame

URGENCY SECTION (Q#6)
Create urgency through CONSEQUENCE, not fake scarcity.
For Unaware, urgency comes from:
‚óè‚Äã The hidden damage that's accumulating
‚óè‚Äã The time/moments being lost
‚óè‚Äã The progressive nature of the problem
Pattern:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"I'm not trying to scare you, but..."
State the progressive nature of the hidden problem
Show window of opportunity
Emphasize time lost can't be recovered

PARAGRAPHS 56-65: TRANSFORMATION TIMELINE

Show the results with specific metrics and emotional transformation.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

First night / Day 1: Initial experience
Week 1: Observable pattern
Week 2-4: Consistent results
Month 1-3: Full transformation
Emotional milestone (the moment that captures the transformation)

Required:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Specific timeframes (not "soon" but "Day 3")
Observable changes (not "felt better" but specific result)
External validation (what others noticed)
Behaviors that stopped/started
Emotional shifts through actions

PARAGRAPHS 66-70: REFLECTION + BRIDGE TO READER
Reflect on what was lost, then bridge to the reader.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Acknowledge time/moments lost
Shift to direct address
Connect their experience to your discovery
Restate the hidden problem in simple terms

OFFER SECTION (Q#9)
Structure the offer simply for Unaware.
For Unaware, you've just revealed a problem they didn't know they had. The offer should feel
like natural next step, not hard sell.
Pattern:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Acknowledge the mechanism made sense
State the offer (price, included, guarantee)
Make it accessible
Remove risk

For higher-priced offers, use value stack:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

[Main Product] - $[Value]
[Bonus 1] - $[Value]
[Bonus 2] - $[Value]
Total Value: $[Total]
Your Price Today: $[Price]
Backed by [X]-Day Guarantee

CLOSING: Push-Pull Technique (Q#10)
CRITICAL: Your closing must be ORIGINAL.
FORBIDDEN patterns:

‚ùå Using identical phrases from reference ads ‚ùå "X months ago... Today..." (overused time
contrast structure) ‚ùå Future predictions about what they'll do ‚ùå Redemption clich√©s ‚ùå
Template feeling ‚Äî if you could swap 5 words for another product, you failed
ACCEPTABLE elements:
‚úì Direct address (vary the phrasing) ‚úì Present moment description (without formulaic
predictions) ‚úì Reflection on past self ‚úì Insight about what was really happening ‚úì Soft
product mention with trial/guarantee ‚úì Brief timeframe contrast (just don't make it the entire
structure)
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Give them control ("I can't force you...")
Present options (do nothing, try something else, get yours)
Be honest about what each option means
No fake scarcity, but real consequence
Soft invitation, not hard push

SCHWARTZ PRINCIPLES (Apply Throughout)
Principle 1: Extreme Specificity
Generic = sounds fake
Schwartz-level = sounds real

Required details:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Specific times (but natural ‚Äî see timestamp authenticity below)
Exact numbers ("fourth time," "three months")
Physical sensations ("tightness in chest," "heart racing")
Concrete actions ("staring at phone," "checking if breathing")
Direct internal dialogue

CRITICAL - TIMESTAMP AUTHENTICITY:

‚ùå Too specific (feels calculated):

‚óè‚Äã "11:34pm" (exact minute)
‚óè‚Äã "forty-seven minutes" (hyper-precise)
‚óè‚Äã "3:17am" (oddly specific)
‚úì Specific but natural:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"Late at night" or "After 11pm"
"Almost an hour" or "close to an hour"
"Middle of the night" or "early morning"
"Tuesday evening" (day + general time)

Rule: Use round numbers ("fifteen minutes," "half an hour") or general time windows.
Down-to-the-minute precision feels performative.

Principle 2: ABT (And-But-Therefore) Structure
Use for proof sections and story beats:
‚óè‚Äã AND: Set up background, create momentum
‚óè‚Äã BUT: Introduce conflict/tension (the critical element)
‚óè‚Äã THEREFORE: Present solution

Principle 3: Intensification
Make them feel the experience MORE before offering solution.
Three methods (use all three):
Verbalization ‚Äî Make them SAY internally what they feel
Multiplication ‚Äî Show it's happening MORE than realized

Dramatization ‚Äî Show future pain if unsolved

Principle 4: Gradualization
Lead them step-by-step into awareness. Each step is small and undeniable.
DO NOT jump from: "I feel X" ‚Üí "You have a hidden problem"
DO lead through:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Step 1: "I feel this too" (identification)
Step 2: "I thought it was normal" (validate attribution)
Step 3: "But something didn't fit" (doubt)
Step 4: "I learned something" (discovery)
Step 5: "I verified it myself" (proof)
Step 6: "Now I understand" (awareness)
Step 7: "Here's what fixed it" (solution)

Principle 5: Authority Validation (NOT Testimonials)
Authority figure INSIDE the story explains mechanism in plain language.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Authority enters naturally (friend, doctor, expert)
They explain WHY it works (mechanism)
In plain language
Reframes from "character flaw" to "solvable problem"

Principle 6: The Accidental Discovery
Never: "I searched for a solution and found this" Always: "I stumbled on this by accident..."
Pattern:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Unexpected circumstance (friend mentions something, overheard conversation)
Surprising result
Confusion (what's different?)
Investigation (aha moment)
Verification test (proves causation)

Principle 7: Transformation Timeline with Metrics
Vague (weak): "Things got better and I felt great"
Schwartz-style (strong): Specific timeframes, observable changes, external validation,
emotional shifts through actions.
Required:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Specific timeframes (not "soon" but "Day 3")
Observable changes (not "felt better" but measurable result)
External validation (what others noticed)
Behaviors that stopped/started
Emotional shifts through actions

FRAMEWORK DISCIPLINE (CRITICAL)
Do NOT add extra emotional beats between framework sections.
FORBIDDEN patterns:

‚ùå Establishing experience ‚Üí Attribution ‚Üí Experience AGAIN ‚Üí Discovery ‚úì Establishing
experience ‚Üí Attribution ‚Üí IMMEDIATELY to contrast/doubt

‚ùå Discovery ‚Üí Extra dramatization ‚Üí THEN mechanism ‚úì Discovery ‚Üí IMMEDIATELY to
mechanism

‚ùå Research at discovery ‚Üí Return home ‚Üí Research AGAIN ‚úì Research ONCE (either at
discovery OR after returning, not both)

‚ùå Story ‚Üí Break into criticism/blame ‚Üí Back to story ‚úì Stay IN story, make it personal
("nobody told me"), not systemic ("they know but don't tell you")

Rule: Each beat appears ONCE, maybe twice if critical. Three times = you're circling.

BACKSTORY WARNING (CRITICAL)
Backstory = telling how you got here Present story = what's happening now

Backstory KILLS momentum. Avoid it.
When backstory is acceptable: ‚úì In the opening (before main crisis starts) ‚úì As a single
sentence callback mid-story ‚úì When it's literally the discovery moment

‚ùå Middle of emotional beat ‚ùå Right after emotional peak ‚ùå
‚ùå More than 50 words

When backstory kills your ad:
To justify why something matters

RULE: If subject is universally understood (baby, parent, spouse) ‚Üí NO backstory needed. If
subject needs justification (pet, hobby, job) ‚Üí ONE line maximum, or cut it.

VOICE & TONE
Required voice:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

First person ONLY (I/me/my)
Confessional ‚Äî like telling a secret to a close friend
Vulnerable ‚Äî admitting you didn't know
Detailed ‚Äî extreme specificity signals truth
Conversational but serious
NEVER "marketery" or sales-y

Unaware voice is DIFFERENT from Problem-Aware:
‚óè‚Äã Problem-Aware: Educational mechanism revelation ("here's WHY your known problem
happens")
‚óè‚Äã Unaware: Discovery journey ("I didn't know I had a problem until...")
Sentence structure: Mix short and long for rhythm.
‚óè‚Äã Short for impact: "I froze."
‚óè‚Äã Long for immersion: Extended sentences with specific details that pull them into the
moment.
Paragraph length: Vary dramatically.
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Single sentences for gut punches
3-5 sentences for story flow
Occasional longer paragraphs for immersion
Use white space for emphasis

COPYRIGHT COMPLIANCE (CRITICAL)
HARD LIMITS - NEVER VIOLATE:

Limit 1: Quote Length
‚óè‚Äã 15+ words from any single source = SEVERE VIOLATION
‚óè‚Äã If quote would be longer, extract key phrase (under 15 words) OR paraphrase entirely

Limit 2: Quotes Per Source
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

ONE quote per source MAXIMUM
After quoting once, that source is CLOSED
All additional content must be fully paraphrased
2+ quotes from one source = SEVERE VIOLATION

Limit 3: Complete Works
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

NEVER reproduce song lyrics (not even one line)
NEVER reproduce poems (not even one stanza)
NEVER reproduce haikus
NEVER reproduce article paragraphs verbatim

Default to paraphrasing. Quotes should be rare exceptions.
Self-check before responding:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Is this quote 15+ words? ‚Üí PARAPHRASE
Have I quoted this source already? ‚Üí PARAPHRASE
Is this a complete creative work? ‚Üí DON'T REPRODUCE
Am I mirroring original phrasing? ‚Üí REWRITE ENTIRELY

‚ùå

Revolutionary, game-changing, amazing, incredible, breakthrough,
miracle, limited time, act now

Unaware is LONGER than Problem-Aware because:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

CRITICAL CONVERSION REMINDER
By the end of this copy, the reader must:
1.‚Äã Recognize themselves in the opening experience
2.‚Äã Realize they have a problem they didn't know about
3.‚Äã Understand the mechanism causing it (UMP)
4.‚Äã See that the solution addresses that mechanism (UMS)
5.‚Äã Believe this is the only way to achieve their desire

The One Belief for Unaware:
"I didn't know I had this problem, but now that I understand the mechanism, this product is the
obvious solution."
If they don't convert, it's usually because:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

The identification didn't resonate (wrong feeling/fear/experience)
The doubt wasn't built gradually enough (jumped to mechanism too fast)
The mechanism wasn't believable or verifiable
The solution didn't feel inevitable from the mechanism
The emotional pivot didn't happen (stayed at surface level)

10 - Product Aware

PRODUCT-AWARE AD COPY GENERATOR
(PROMISE LEAD)
1. UPLOAD YOUR RESEARCH DOCUMENTS:
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Document: [ATTACH FILE]
‚óè‚Äã Macro Avatar Language Document: [ATTACH FILE]
‚óè‚Äã Mass Desire Research Document: [ATTACH FILE]

2. SPECIFY YOUR TARGET:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Mass Desire You're Targeting: [INSERT MASS DESIRE]
Macro-Avatar You‚Äôre Targeting (Optional): [INSERT AVATAR # OR NAME]
Product Name: [YOUR PRODUCT]
What's Included: [Main product + included items]
Guarantee: [Terms - days, conditions, refund policy]
Bonuses (if any): [List with values]
Price + Offer: [Listing price + Discount]

3. YOUR PROMISE:
The Biggest Benefit: [The ONE thing that makes the biggest difference - NOT what it
does, but what they GET/FEEL]
‚óè‚Äã Example:
get)

‚ùå "Whitens teeth" (what it does) ‚Üí ‚úÖ "Hollywood smile in 3 days" (what they

How It's Delivered:
‚óè‚Äã Speed: [How fast? In 3 days? 2 weeks? 30 minutes?]
‚óè‚Äã Size/Amount: [How much? 10 pounds? 50%? 3x more?]
‚óè‚Äã Quality: [What level? Hollywood-level? Professional-grade? Better than what?]

4. YOUR PROOF:
Testimonials/Results: [Specific results, names if possible, exact transformations]
Studies/Data: [Research, statistics, clinical results - be specific]

Before/After: [Specific transformations with numbers]
Expert Endorsements: [Who validates this and why they're credible]
Social Proof: [How many people, how long in market, awards, recognition]

I'm going to give you everything you need to write Product-Aware ad copy using the Promise
Lead approach.
Here's what's happening:
You've done your research. You know your avatar and what desire you're targeting. Most
importantly, they already KNOW your product exists. They're on the fence‚Äîalmost ready to buy
but comparing options. Now you need ad copy that proves you can deliver what you promise.
This prompt is flexible - you can use it for:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Testing promises - Write multiple versions emphasizing different benefits
Testing proof angles - Same promise, different proof emphasis
Testing offers - Same promise and proof, different offer structures
Scaling winners - Create variations of what's already working

What's Product-Aware?
Product-Aware prospects know your product exists. They've seen it, heard about it, maybe read
reviews. They're not sure if it's right for them.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Saw your teeth whitening ad, now reading reviews to see if it really works in 3 days
‚óè‚Äã Found your cat hydration product, comparing it to other options
‚óè‚Äã Knows about your hair removal tool, wondering if it actually prevents razor burn
They're fence-sitters. Almost ready to buy. Just need that extra nudge.
What's the Promise Lead approach?
Promise Lead = "Here's the biggest benefit. Here's proof I can deliver it. Here's the offer. Here's
the guarantee."
It's direct and proof-heavy. No long stories. No mechanism explanations. Just promise ‚Üí prove
‚Üí offer.
When to use Promise Lead:

Use PROMISE LEAD when:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

They KNOW your product exists
They're NOT sure it's right for them
They're "on the fence" - almost ready to buy
They're comparing options or reading reviews
They need PROOF more than education

Don't use if:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

They don't know your product ‚Üí Use Solution-Aware or lower
They're already sold ‚Üí Use Offer Lead (Most Aware)
They're highly skeptical ‚Üí Use Problem-Solution or Story
They don't know solutions exist ‚Üí Use lower awareness

What's a promise?
Your promise is the BIGGEST benefit your product delivers. Not what it does‚Äîwhat they GET
or FEEL.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå "Whitens teeth" (what it does)
‚úÖ "Hollywood smile in 3 days" (what they get)
‚úÖ "Confidence to smile without covering your mouth" (what they feel)

The Believability Challenge:
From Masterson: "Oversized promises can be so large that they become unbelievable."
The Sears Battery Example:
‚óè‚Äã Showed 10 cars starting from ONE battery ‚Üí Nobody believed it
‚óè‚Äã Showed 3-4 cars starting from ONE battery ‚Üí HUGE success
Lesson: Scale promise back just enough to be believable.
What I'm going to do:
1.‚Äã Extract your inputs (avatar, desire, promise, proof)
2.‚Äã Generate 10-15 headline options using Schwartz's 7 tasks
3.‚Äã You pick your favorite headline
4.‚Äã I write complete ad copy (1,000-1,500 words) that's PROOF-HEAVY
The copy follows Promise Lead framework: Big promise ‚Üí Prove it ‚Üí Offer ‚Üí Close with
guarantee.

Everything below this section is instructions for the LLM. DO NOT EDIT OR CHANGE
ANYTHING BELOW THIS LINE.

‚óè‚Äã Use the most common/universal pain points and language
‚óè‚Äã Focus on the problem and desire, not avatar-specific nuances

PART 2: HEADLINE GENERATION
Claude, now generate 10-15 headline options based on the promise and proof.
SCHWARTZ'S 7 TASKS FOR PRODUCT-AWARE HEADLINES
From Breakthrough Advertising Stage 2:

Note on Product Names: For new/unknown brands (most ecommerce/dropshipping), SKIP the
product name in headlines. The promise matters more than an unknown name. Focus on the
task instead.

TASK (a): Reinforce desire through ASSOCIATION
Link your product to something they already desire or admire.
For New Brands:
‚óè‚Äã "The [Category] Professionals Use"
‚óè‚Äã "The Same [Benefit] As [Luxury Brand] For [Fraction] The Price"
‚óè‚Äã "What [Aspirational Group] Use For [Benefit]"
Pattern: [Benefit/Result] + [Association with desired group/standard]

TASK (a): Reinforce desire through EXAMPLE
Show the result is real by providing proof through example.
For New Brands:
‚óè‚Äã "Can You Tell Which [Result] Cost $500 and Which Cost $49?"
‚óè‚Äã "Only Her [Expert] Knows It's Not [Expensive Alternative]"
‚óè‚Äã "Before and After: Same Person, 3 Days Apart"
Pattern: Question or comparison that proves result is indistinguishable from expensive
alternative

TASK (a): Reinforce desire through SENSORY SHARPENING
Help them imagine the exact sensation/experience.
For New Brands:
‚óè‚Äã "Tastes Like [Ideal Experience]"
‚óè‚Äã "Feels Like [Premium Sensation]"
‚óè‚Äã "Looks Like [Desired Standard]"
Pattern: Sensory verb + "like" + ideal comparison

TASK (b): Sharpen the image of HOW it satisfies the desire
Show the specific way your product delivers the benefit.
For New Brands:
‚óè‚Äã "So [Quality Attribute] That [Extreme Proof]"
‚óè‚Äã "The [Product] That [Extreme Test/Proof]"
‚óè‚Äã "Works So Well That [Unbelievable But True Story]"
Pattern: Extreme proof of the quality/mechanism

TASK (c): Extend the image of WHERE and WHEN it satisfies
Show it works in more situations than they realized.
For New Brands:
‚óè‚Äã "[Benefit] Anywhere, Anytime"
‚óè‚Äã "Works Even [Unexpected Situation]"
‚óè‚Äã "Not Just For [Expected Use] - Also For [Unexpected Use]"
Pattern: Expands use cases beyond what they assumed

TASK (d): Introduce NEW PROOF, details, documentation
Add credibility with specific numbers, endorsements, data.
For New Brands:
‚óè‚Äã "[Percentage/Number] of [Credible Group] Use This For [Benefit]"
‚óè‚Äã "Tested by [Authority/Number] - Here's What They Found"
‚óè‚Äã "[Specific Number/Result] in [Specific Timeframe]"
Pattern: Specific proof with numbers

TASK (e): Announce NEW MECHANISM that satisfies desire BETTER
Show there's a new way it works that improves the benefit.
For New Brands:

‚óè‚Äã "Now: [Benefit 1] AND [Benefit 2] In One [Product]"
‚óè‚Äã "The First [Product] That [Does Two Things At Once]"
‚óè‚Äã "Finally: [Benefit] Without [Usual Tradeoff]"
Pattern: Announces innovation or combination

TASK (f): Announce NEW MECHANISM that eliminates LIMITATIONS
Show you've removed a previous drawback.
For New Brands:
‚óè‚Äã "[Benefit] Without [Previous Limitation]"
‚óè‚Äã "Finally: [Desired Result] With No [Previous Drawback]"
‚óè‚Äã "All The [Benefit] - None Of The [Problem]"
Pattern: Benefit + removal of known limitation

CLAUDE INSTRUCTIONS FOR HEADLINE GENERATION:
1.‚Äã Extract the promise (biggest benefit)
2.‚Äã Identify which tasks are most relevant based on proof
3.‚Äã Generate 10-15 headlines testing different tasks:
‚óã‚Äã 2-3 headlines using Task (a) - Association/Example/Sensory
‚óã‚Äã 2-3 headlines using Task (b) - How it works
‚óã‚Äã 1-2 headlines using Task (c) - Extended use
‚óã‚Äã 2-3 headlines using Task (d) - New proof
‚óã‚Äã 2-3 headlines using Task (e) - New mechanism
‚óã‚Äã 1-2 headlines using Task (f) - Removes limitation
For each headline, provide:
‚óè‚Äã The actual headline
‚óè‚Äã Task (a, b, c, d, e, or f)
‚óè‚Äã Why it works (how this addresses Product-Aware fence-sitters)
Output Format:
Headlines:

Headline 1: [The actual headline]

Task: [(a), (b), (c), (d), (e), or (f)]
Why it works: [Explanation]

Headline 2: [The actual headline]
Task: [(a), (b), (c), (d), (e), or (f)]
Why it works: [Explanation]

[Continue for 10-15 headlines]

After generating headlines, ask user: "Which headline do you want to use for your ad
copy?"
Once user picks headline, proceed to Part 3.

PART 3: CLAUDE PROCESSING INSTRUCTIONS
Claude, before writing the ad copy, you must:
Step 1: Identify The Promise Type
Extract from user's promise input:
‚óè‚Äã The core promise: What they get
‚óè‚Äã The speed/size/quality: How it's delivered
‚óè‚Äã The emotional payoff: How they'll feel (from Level 2 desire in avatar doc)
Step 2: Map The Proof Sequence
Product-Aware needs HEAVY PROOF. Map out 4-6 proof elements from user's proof input:
Proof Element 1: [Type - testimonial/study/data] Proof Element 2: [Type] Proof Element 3:
[Type] Proof Element 4: [Type] Proof Element 5: [Type] Proof Element 6: [Type]
CRITICAL: Vary proof types. Don't use 6 testimonials in a row. Mix testimonials, studies, expert
endorsements, social proof, comparisons.

PART 4: WRITING INSTRUCTIONS
CORE STRUCTURE: The Big Promise + Proof
Your copy must lead with the promise, then PROVE you can deliver it.
Paragraphs 1-3: THE PROMISE
‚óè‚Äã Headline states promise
‚óè‚Äã Repeat/expand promise in opening
‚óè‚Äã Acknowledge their position (comparing options)
Paragraphs 4-15: THE PROOF (50-60% of copy)
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Execute 4-6 proof elements
Each element = 2-3 paragraphs
Vary proof types
Build momentum

Paragraphs 16-18: THE OFFER
‚óè‚Äã What they get
‚óè‚Äã Guarantee
‚óè‚Äã Call to action
Paragraphs 19-20: THE CLOSE
‚óè‚Äã Return to emotional promise
‚óè‚Äã Final nudge over fence
‚óè‚Äã P.S. with guarantee reminder

LENGTH & PACING
Target: 1,000-1,500 words MAXIMUM
Product-Aware is SHORTER than lower awareness because:
‚óè‚Äã They already know your product
‚óè‚Äã They don't need education or mechanism
‚óè‚Äã They just need proof you can deliver
Pacing discipline:
‚óè‚Äã Promise: 3 paragraphs
‚óè‚Äã Proof: 12 paragraphs (this is the bulk)

‚óè‚Äã Offer: 3 paragraphs
‚óè‚Äã Close: 2 paragraphs

HEADLINE: The Promise
Use the headline selected from Part 2.
CRITICAL: The headline leads with the BIGGEST BENEFIT, not features or mechanism.

OPENING: THE PROMISE (Paragraphs 1-3)
Structure:
Paragraph 1: Repeat/Expand The Promise
Headline stated the promise. Now expand it.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã State the benefit in different words
‚óè‚Äã Make it feel immediate and real
‚óè‚Äã Connect to what they want
Example:
A Hollywood smile in 3 days.

You've seen them on the red carpet‚Äîthe tuxes and gowns, the makeup and jewelry‚Äîbut what
do they all have in common?

Yep. Pearly, shining smiles full of straight, white teeth.

Now you can get the same flashy grin, and you don't need $50,000 worth of caps and a
high-ticket Hollywood dentist to make it happen.

This wasn't even possible, just a year ago. But it is now.

Paragraph 2: Acknowledge Their Position
Show you understand they're comparing options.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã "You've probably seen other [products]..."
‚óè‚Äã "You might be wondering if [this] really works..."
‚óè‚Äã Show you get their skepticism
Example:
You've probably seen other teeth whitening products. Maybe you've even tried a few.

And you're wondering: Does this one actually work in 3 days? Or is it just another overpromise?

Fair question.

Paragraph 3: Preview What's Coming
Set up the proof section.
Example:
Let me show you why this works when others don't.

And more importantly, let me prove it.

THE PROOF SECTION (Paragraphs 4-15 - CRITICAL)
This is where you win them over. Spend 50-60% of copy on PROOF.
Execute the proof elements you mapped in Step 2.

PROOF ELEMENT #1: Testimonial/Result (2-3 paragraphs)
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Specific person with specific result
Include numbers (days, amount, percentage)
Quote their exact words
Show the transformation

Example:
Sarah M. tried [product] after seeing her dentist.

Three days later, her husband asked if she'd gotten work done.

"I couldn't believe it," she said. "My teeth were actually THREE SHADES whiter. People noticed
immediately."

Her dentist asked what she'd done. When Sarah told her, the dentist ordered one herself.

PROOF ELEMENT #2: Study/Data (2-3 paragraphs)
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Research institution or authority
Specific findings with numbers
What they measured
What they concluded

Example:
Clinical study at UCLA dental school tested [product] on 200 patients.

After 72 hours:
- 94% showed measurable whitening
- Average improvement: 3.2 shades

- Zero sensitivity reported
- Results lasted 6+ months

The study concluded: "[Product] delivers professional-grade whitening without the professional
price tag."

PROOF ELEMENT #3: More Testimonials (2-3 paragraphs)
Different person, different situation.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã Reinforces the pattern
‚óè‚Äã Addresses different objections
‚óè‚Äã Shows it works for different types of people
Example:
James K. had tried strips, gels, even professional whitening.

Nothing lasted. Results faded within weeks.

With [product], 90 days later, his teeth are still 3 shades whiter than before he started.

"I finally stopped wasting money on treatments that don't last," he said.

PROOF ELEMENT #4: Expert Endorsement (2-3 paragraphs)
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã Who they are, why they're credible
‚óè‚Äã What they say about it
‚óè‚Äã Why they recommend it
Example:

Dr. Jennifer Chen, cosmetic dentist with 15 years experience:

"I've seen every whitening product on the market. Most either don't work or damage enamel.

[Product] is different. It uses [mechanism] that whitens without weakening enamel. I recommend
it to patients who want results without the cost of professional treatments."

PROOF ELEMENT #5: Social Proof (1-2 paragraphs)
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã How many people have used it
‚óè‚Äã How long it's been available
‚óè‚Äã Awards, recognition, popularity
Example:
Over 47,000 people have used [product] in the last 18 months.

Average rating: 4.8/5 stars.

Featured in [Publication 1], [Publication 2], and [Publication 3] as one of the top teeth whitening
products of 2024.

PROOF ELEMENT #6: Comparison (1-2 paragraphs)
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã How this compares to alternatives
‚óè‚Äã Why it's better/faster/easier
‚óè‚Äã What makes it different
Example:
Professional whitening: $500-800, requires dentist appointment, results last 3-6 months.

[Product]: $49, use at home, results last 6+ months.

Same result. Fraction of the cost. No appointment needed.

PACING DISCIPLINE:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Each proof element = 2-3 paragraphs
Vary proof types (don't use 6 testimonials)
Keep moving - don't belabor any single point
Build momentum toward the offer

THE OFFER (Paragraphs 16-18)
Paragraph 16: Present What They Get
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã Price
‚óè‚Äã What's included
‚óè‚Äã Bonuses
Example:
Get [product] today for $49.

Includes:
- [Main product] (60-day supply)
- [Bonus 1]
- [Bonus 2]

Paragraph 17: The Guarantee
Structure:

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

What's covered
How long
How easy to get refund
Tie back to the promise

Example:
60-day guarantee: If you don't see at least 2 shades of whitening within 3 days, send it back for
a full refund.

No questions asked. We even cover return shipping.

You risk nothing.

Paragraph 18: Call To Action
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã Tell them exactly what to do
‚óè‚Äã Make it simple
‚óè‚Äã Create appropriate urgency (if genuine)
Example:
[CTA Button: Get [Product] - Risk-Free]

Order now and get [bonus/discount if applicable].

[Delivery timeframe if relevant]

THE CLOSE (Paragraphs 19-20)
Paragraph 19: Return To The Emotional Promise

Structure:
‚óè‚Äã What they'll feel
‚óè‚Äã How life changes
‚óè‚Äã The confidence/relief/joy
Example:
Imagine smiling without covering your mouth.

Laughing without worrying.

Taking photos without editing your teeth.

That confidence is three days away.

Paragraph 20: Final Nudge
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã Remind them they're THIS CLOSE
‚óè‚Äã Small push over the fence
‚óè‚Äã Sign off
Example:
The choice is yours.

[Signature]

P.S. ‚Äî Remember: 60-day guarantee means you risk nothing. Try it. See the results. If you
don't love your new smile, you get your money back. But I think you're going to love it.

VOICE & TONE
Required voice:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Confident and direct (not pushy)
Proof-focused (show, don't just claim)
Acknowledges their skepticism
Specific numbers and results
Professional and credible

‚ùå
‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

Avoid:
Hype without proof
Vague claims ("amazing," "incredible" without data)
Explaining mechanism (they don't need education)
Problem dramatization (they know the
problem)
Long stories or empathy building

MASTERSON PRINCIPLES (Apply Throughout)
Principle 1: Start With The Product's Biggest Benefit
From Masterson: "Your safest opening is your prime benefit"
Lead with what matters most to them.
Principle 2: Hit The Targeted Promise Right Away
From Masterson: "Headlines that promise benefit sell more than those that don't"
No mystery. No buildup. Promise first.
Principle 3: Connect To Their Core Desire
The promise isn't what the product DOES‚Äîit's how they'll FEEL using it:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Confidence
Freedom from worry
How they'll be seen by others
Safety and security

Principle 4: Sound As New And Original As Possible
From Masterson: "The promises that work best are the promises that your prospect doesn't hear
anybody else making"
Differentiate your promise.
Principle 5: Be Bold But Still Believable

From Masterson: "Promise must be large enough to excite, small enough to believe"
Don't overpromise. Don't underpromise.
Principle 6: Follow With Even Bigger Proof
From Masterson: "Promise Leads especially demand airtight credibility"
Heavy proof. Multiple types. Specific numbers.
Principle 7: Often Focus On Speed, Size, Or Quality Of Results
‚óè‚Äã "In 3 days"
‚óè‚Äã "10 pounds"
‚óè‚Äã "Hollywood smile"
Make results concrete and measurable.

FORBIDDEN (Will Kill The Ad)

‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå

‚ùå
‚ùå

‚úÖ

‚úÖ Proof
‚úÖ

Never:
Hype without proof
Vague claims
Explain mechanism (they don't need it)
Long stories
Problem dramatization
Empathy building (they're comparing, not hurting)
Promise more than you can deliver

REQUIRED (Must Include)

‚úÖ

‚úÖ

‚úÖ

Language:
Promise is bold but believable
Headline leads with biggest benefit
is HEAVY (50-60% of copy)
Multiple proof types
Specific numbers everywhere
Guarantee is strong
Voice is confident, not desperate

‚úÖ

‚úÖ

‚úÖ

‚úÖ

‚úÖ

Structure:
Promise (paragraphs 1-3)
Proof (paragraphs 4-15) - 4-6 elements
(paragraphs 16-18)
Close (paragraphs 19-20)
P.S. with guarantee reminder

‚úÖ Offer

PART 5: OUTPUT

Before writing, internally confirm: ‚úì I extracted the promise (biggest benefit) ‚úì I mapped 4-6
proof elements ‚úì I will spend 50-60% of copy on proof ‚úì I will vary proof types (testimonials,

specific promise and proof

11 - Most Aware

MOST AWARE AD COPY GENERATOR
(OFFER LEAD)
1. SPECIFY YOUR TARGET:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Mass Desire You're Targeting: [INSERT MASS DESIRE]
Macro-Avatar You‚Äôre Targeting (Optional): [INSERT AVATAR # OR NAME]
Product Name: [YOUR PRODUCT]
What's Included: [Main product + included items]
Guarantee: [Terms - days, conditions, refund policy]
Bonuses (if any): [List with values]
Price + Offer: [Listing price + Discount]

2. YOUR OFFER:
The Deal:
‚óè‚Äã Regular Price: [What it normally costs]
‚óè‚Äã Sale Price: [What they pay today]
‚óè‚Äã Discount: [Percentage off OR dollar amount saved]
OR if not price-focused:
‚óè‚Äã What Makes It Valuable: [Exclusive access, limited quantity, rare item, etc.]
The "Reason Why": [Why are you offering this deal NOW? Limited time, clearing inventory,
anniversary, exclusive for members, etc.]

4. ENDORSEMENT (Optional):
Who's Signing: [Founder name, person they trust, or leave blank]
Why It Matters: [Your relationship with this audience - have they bought from you before? Do
they know you?]

I'm going to give you everything you need to write Most Aware ad copy using the Offer Lead
approach.
Here's what's happening:

You've done your research. You know your avatar and what desire you're targeting. Most
importantly, they already KNOW you, TRUST you, and WANT your product. They're repeat
buyers. Fans. Your house list. Now you just need to tell them THE DEAL.
This prompt is flexible - you can use it for:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Testing offers - Same product, different deal structures
Testing urgency angles - Different "reason why" approaches
Testing bonuses - Different bonus configurations
Scaling winners - Create variations of what's already working

What's Most Aware?
Most Aware prospects already bought from you before. They love your brand. They're
emotionally ready to buy again. They just need to know what the deal is.
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Previous customers receiving an exclusive discount email
House list getting early access to new product
Repeat buyers offered a bundle deal
Fans who've been waiting for your next launch

They don't need education. They don't need convincing. They just need THE DEAL.
What's the Offer Lead approach?
Offer Lead = "Here's the deal. Here's why now. Here's what you get. Click here."
It's direct and deal-focused. Shortest of all awareness levels. No fluff.
When to use Offer Lead:
Use OFFER LEAD when:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

They already BOUGHT from you before
They LOVE your brand
They're "multi-buyers" (house list)
They just need to know THE DEAL
They're emotionally ready to buy

Don't use if:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

They don't know your brand ‚Üí Use Product-Aware or lower
They're skeptical or jaded ‚Üí Use Problem-Solution or Story
First-time buyers ‚Üí Use lower awareness
Cold traffic ‚Üí Use Stage 3-5 prompts

The "Too Aware" Problem:
From Masterson: Sometimes high awareness is a NET NEGATIVE if they're:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Hit too many times by similar pitches
Tried products that let them down
Reached spending limit
Made up their mind

Solution: If your Most Aware audience is JADED/SKEPTICAL, use Problem-Solution or Story
Lead instead to rebuild trust.
What makes a good offer?
From Masterson:
‚óè‚Äã Valuable (high quality, desirable, scarce)
‚óè‚Äã A Steal (compared to normal price)
‚óè‚Äã Reason Why (answers "what's the catch?")
What I'm going to do:
1.‚Äã Extract your inputs (avatar, desire, offer, reason why)
2.‚Äã Generate 8-10 headline options testing different offer angles
3.‚Äã You pick your favorite headline
4.‚Äã I write complete ad copy (500-1,000 words) that's DEAL-FIRST
The copy follows Offer Lead framework: Deal ‚Üí Reason Why ‚Üí What You Get ‚Üí CTA.

ANYTHING BELOW THIS LINE.

OFFER LEAD HEADLINE PATTERNS
From Schwartz/Masterson examples:
Pattern 1: Product + Price (Pure Deal)
‚óè‚Äã "[Product Name] ‚Äî Was [Regular Price] ‚Äî Now [Sale Price]"
Pattern 2: Guarantee-First (Soft Offer)
‚óè‚Äã "Don't Pay One Penny Till [Product] [Delivers Result]!"

‚óè‚Äã "[Product] + [Bonus 1] + [Bonus 2] ‚Äî Only [Price]"
Pattern 10: Time/Quantity Limit
‚óè‚Äã "Last Chance: [Product] At [Price] ‚Äî Ends [Date]"
‚óè‚Äã "Only [Number] Left At This Price"

PART 4: WRITING INSTRUCTIONS

Your copy must lead with the offer, explain why now, show what they get, and close with CTA.
Paragraphs 1-3: THE OFFER
‚óè‚Äã State the deal immediately
‚óè‚Äã Underscore the value
‚óè‚Äã Add the most compelling detail

‚óè‚Äã State it clearly
‚óè‚Äã Create appropriate urgency

‚óè‚Äã Break down the savings OR
‚óè‚Äã Detail each bonus OR

LENGTH & PACING
Target: 500-1,000 words MAXIMUM
Most Aware is SHORTEST because:
‚óè‚Äã

Pacing discipline:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

The Offer: 3 paragraphs
Reason Why: 3 paragraphs
Elaborate on Deal: 6 paragraphs
What They Get: 4 paragraphs
CTA: 4 paragraphs

HEADLINE: The Offer
Use the headline selected from Part 2.
CRITICAL: The headline leads with THE DEAL, not education or mechanism.

OPENING: THE OFFER (Paragraphs 1-3)
Structure:

Paragraph 1: State The Deal Immediately
What they get, the price/discount/value.
Make it concrete and specific.
Example:
Don't Pay A Penny For This Book Till It Doubles Your Power to Learn!
Here at last is your chance to make such an overwhelming difference in your child's
performance in school‚Äîin as little as five short minutes of your time everyday‚Äîthat the teacher
may actually call you up to see what happened!

Paragraph 2: Underscore The Value
Why this is a great deal. What they're saving. Do the math if possible.
Example:
The Complete XYZ System normally sells for $297.
Today, you pay just $147.
That's 50% off‚Äîa full $150 in savings.
And you risk nothing with our 60-day guarantee.

Paragraph 3: Add The Most Compelling Detail
Best bonus, strongest guarantee element, exclusivity factor.
Example:
Plus, when you order today, you'll also receive our Premium Template Pack (a $67 value)
absolutely free.

THE "REASON WHY" (Paragraphs 4-6 - CRITICAL)
This is CRITICAL for Offer Leads. Answer: "Why are you giving me this deal?"
Paragraph 4: Set Up The Reason

Context for why now. What's changed or happening.
Example:
Here's why I'm making this offer now:

Paragraph 5: State The Reason Clearly
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Limited time

Example:

Rather than let these sit in storage, I'd rather get them to customers like you who've supported
us for years.

Paragraph 6: Create Appropriate Urgency
Deadline (if genuine), limited quantity (if true), why they should act now.
Example:
But I can only hold this price until [DATE].
After that, these units go to our outlet stores at the regular price.

Now expand on what makes this valuable.
For Price-Focused Offers:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Show what else costs this much
Make them feel smart for getting it

For Bonus-Focused Offers:

‚óè‚Äã Detail each bonus

‚óè‚Äã "Dissolving bonus" technique (bonus so good it melts resistance)
For Guarantee-Focused Offers:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

MASTERSON PRINCIPLES (Apply Throughout)
Principle #1: The Deal IS The Hook
From Masterson: "It's pretty tough to screw up an Offer Lead when you have a good deal going
to fans"
Lead with the deal. Everything else supports it.
Principle #2: Value + Steal
The prospect must feel the benefit is:
‚óè‚Äã Valuable (high quality, desirable, scarce)
‚óè‚Äã A steal (compared to normal price)
Principle #3: Include "Reason Why"
Answer the inevitable question: "What's the catch?"
Real reasons work best:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

New version launching
Clearing inventory
Anniversary sale
Exclusive for members

Principle #4: Blend Offer + Promise
Unlike other lead types, Offer Leads can blend offer details with promises:
‚óè‚Äã "Don't Pay A Penny Till This Book Doubles Your Power to Learn!"
‚óè‚Äã "Guaranteed To Improve Your Child's School Marks ‚Äî or you pay nothing!"

WARNING: THE "FREE" TRAP
From Masterson:
The problem:
‚óè‚Äã If bonus feels as valuable as product ‚Üí People sign up for bonus, try product (GOOD)
‚óè‚Äã If product has much higher value ‚Üí Too many freebies make product look worthless
(BAD)
The rule:
‚óè‚Äã Low-priced product + big bonuses = OK
‚óè‚Äã High-priced product + too many freebies = Devalues main product
Use FREE strategically, not automatically.

‚ùå

Tell long stories (waste their time)
Treat them like strangers (they're fans)

scarcity (they'll see through it)
Make them work to understand the deal

12 - Advertorials

Prompt 12(a) - Creating the Nightmare Personal Story
Advertorial
You are an expert advertorial copywriter who creates compelling, conversion-focused
advertorials following proven frameworks.
We are going to be creating an advertorial around [INSERT YOUR ANGLE HERE]
Use the provided examples and frameworks to create a complete advertorial based on the
user's product/angle.
REQUIRED READING MATERIALS
Before writing, carefully study these provided documents:
- **1Tac Roadside Safety Advertorial** (structure and length reference)
- **Cat Dehydration Advertorial** (tone and style reference)
Writing Effective Copy
Writing Effective Advertorials
TARGET SPECIFICATIONS
**Length:** 900-1,200 words (match 1Tac length, not longer)
**Reading Level:** 5th grade maximum
**Structure:** Customer testimonial story format (NOT company selling)
**Tone:** Conversational, emotional, urgent but helpful
CRITICAL FRAMEWORK REQUIREMENTS
OPENING HOOK
- Start with ONE short, punchy sentence that cuts to core pain/fear
- Example: "Max was dying, and it was my fault."
- Should be 6-12 words maximum
- Must create immediate emotional impact
RMBC STRUCTURE (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
**Lead:**
- Call out problem with statistics/urgency
- Promise solution + tease discovery story
- Tease unique mechanism (contrarian/surprising)

- Brief credibility builders
- Qualify audience
**Background Story:**
- Customer narrator (NOT company owner)
- "I was just like you" connection
- Traditional solutions failing
- Trigger event/crisis moment
- Search for truth/answers
**Unique Mechanism of Problem:**
- Surprising/counterintuitive explanation
- "99% know this, but the missing 1% is..."
- Scientific backing/credibility
**Unique Mechanism of Solution:**
- Logical connection to problem
- Specific methodology/technology
- Why this works when others fail
**Product Buildup + Reveal:**
- Customer discovered product through research
- Testing/results story
- Proof it works
- Others asking for it
**Close:**
- Product details and differentiators
- Social proof/testimonials
- Urgency/scarcity
- Guarantee
- Price justification
- Multiple CTAs
- Two-choice close
### 3. HEATH WILCOCK PAS FLOW
- **Pain:** Identify and agitate the core problem
- **Agitate:** Make the problem feel urgent and costly
- **Solution:** Present the discovery and results

### 4. WRITING STYLE REQUIREMENTS
**Sentences:**
- Keep most sentences under 15 words
- Use simple words (5th grade level)
- Break up complex ideas into multiple short sentences
- Vary sentence length for rhythm
**Vocabulary:**
- Replace complex terms with simple ones
- If technical terms needed, immediately explain in simple language
- Use emotional, conversational language
**Paragraphs:**
- 1-3 sentences maximum
- Lots of white space
- Easy to scan
**Headlines:**
- Create curiosity and promise benefits
- Avoid starting with "The"
- Make them compelling, not just topic labels
- Examples: "How A $3,200 Emergency Saved My Cat's Life"
### 5. BOLDING FOR SCANNABILITY
Bold these elements:
- Key statistics and numbers
- Shocking revelations
- Solution benefits and results
- Urgency/scarcity elements
- Guarantee terms
- Call-to-action phrases
### 6. CUSTOMER STORY FORMAT
- Narrator is a CUSTOMER who discovered the product
- NOT the company owner or seller
- Share their discovery story to help others
- Product is made by separate company
- CTAs direct to "check availability" not "we're offering"

## MASS DESIRES TO TARGET
Focus on the 3 core desires:
1. **Prevention of Crisis:** Avoid disaster/emergency/loss
2. **Peace of Mind:** Eliminate worry and uncertainty
3. **Understanding:** Know the real reason behind problems
## INPUT REQUIREMENTS
Provide:
- Product name and description
- Target audience
- Main problem it solves
- Unique mechanism/angle
- Key benefits
- Price point
- Any specific claims/studies
## OUTPUT FORMAT
Deliver a complete advertorial following the exact structure and style of the provided
examples, ensuring:
- Proper customer testimonial format
- All RMBC elements included
- 5th grade reading level
- Strategic bolding applied
- Strong emotional hook opening
- Compelling headlines throughout
- Multiple strong CTAs
Remember: The goal is to create an advertorial that reads like a genuine customer sharing
their discovery story to help others, following proven conversion frameworks while
maintaining authentic emotional connection.

Prompt 12b - Authority Figure Based Advertorial
AUTHORITY REVELATION ADVERTORIAL FRAMEWORK

You're writing a 1,200-1,500 word advertorial using the Authority Revelation framework. This
framework features a credible expert who discovers a hidden truth that contradicts
conventional wisdom and shares it to help their audience.
CORE NARRATIVE STRUCTURE:
Opening Hook Sequence
‚óè‚Äã Start with stark contrast: "[Target] should have [succeeded]. They [failed] instead."
‚óè‚Äã Build recognition through 3-4 "If you..." statements (escalating specificity)
‚óè‚Äã Promise of discovery that could save/transform
‚óè‚Äã Reveal epidemic/widespread problem with specific percentage
‚óè‚Äã Contrast obvious vs. hidden problem using "But this isn't..." structure
Authority Establishment & Breaking Point
‚óè‚Äã Introduce expert with specific credentials and years of experience
‚óè‚Äã Present "perfect" case that inexplicably fails
‚óè‚Äã Show emotional impact of failure
‚óè‚Äã Expert's realization that conventional wisdom is wrong
‚óè‚Äã Personal mission to find real answers
The Investigation & Discovery (REVEALS UMP)
‚óè‚Äã Deep dive into research/data with specific numbers
‚óè‚Äã Shocking disconnect between "official" standards and reality
‚óè‚Äã UNIQUE MECHANISM OF PROBLEM (UMP): The REAL hidden cause nobody knows
about
‚óã‚Äã Must be counterintuitive but immediately make sense
‚óã‚Äã Scientific/biological explanation
‚óã‚Äã Shows why all traditional approaches fail
‚óã‚Äã "We've been thinking about this backwards"
‚óè‚Äã Reveal why target's instincts/behaviors are actually correct
‚óè‚Äã Build "you're not crazy, you're right" validation
Systematic Debunking
‚óè‚Äã Test each common solution with consistent structure
‚óè‚Äã Show how each fails because it doesn't address the UMP
‚óè‚Äã Format: "Solution? Specific flaw. Doesn't address [UMP]."
‚óè‚Äã Reveal what professionals use privately
‚óè‚Äã Question why public doesn't have access

The Professional Secret (REVEALS UMS)
‚óè‚Äã UNIQUE MECHANISM OF SOLUTION (UMS): How the solution specifically solves the
UMP
‚óã‚Äã Direct connection to the problem mechanism
‚óã‚Äã Explains WHY this works when others don't
‚óã‚Äã Scientific/technical explanation made simple
‚óã‚Äã "Because it addresses [UMP], it can actually [result]"
‚óè‚Äã Introduce professional-grade solution
‚óè‚Äã Explain it's not new, just hidden
‚óè‚Äã Single company making it available
Proof Sequence
‚óè‚Äã Immediate observable changes (hours/days)
‚óè‚Äã Formal trial/testing with specific numbers
‚óè‚Äã "X out of Y showed improvement" format
‚óè‚Äã Results that prove the UMS works
‚óè‚Äã Expert's personal/family experience
Paradigm Shift
‚óè‚Äã Reveal what "normal" should actually be
‚óè‚Äã Quantify the gap (years, performance, savings)
‚óè‚Äã Emotional weight of unnecessary loss
‚óè‚Äã Challenge accepted limitations
Urgency & Close
‚óè‚Äã Professional community discovering solution
‚óè‚Äã Supply/demand imbalance
‚óè‚Äã Risk reversal guarantee (90-day typical)
‚óè‚Äã Clear call to action with discount percentage
UNIQUE MECHANISM REQUIREMENTS:
UMP (Unique Mechanism Problem):
‚óè‚Äã The HIDDEN reason behind the visible problem
‚óè‚Äã Must contradict what people assume
‚óè‚Äã Explains why traditional solutions fail
‚óè‚Äã Creates "aha moment" - everything suddenly makes sense

‚óè‚Äã Examples: "It's not dehydration, it's chemical detection" or "It's not learning disability,
it's wrong neural activation"
UMS (Unique Mechanism Solution):
‚óè‚Äã DIRECTLY addresses the UMP
‚óè‚Äã Explains HOW it solves the real problem
‚óè‚Äã Not just features - the mechanism of action
‚óè‚Äã Creates confidence it will work
‚óè‚Äã Examples: "Triple filtration removes chemicals cats detect" or "Spatial manipulation
activates proper neural pathways"
KEY PSYCHOLOGICAL ELEMENTS:
1.‚Äã Authority as Whistleblower - Expert breaking ranks to help
2.‚Äã Validation Pattern - "Your instincts were right all along"
3.‚Äã David vs Goliath - Individual truth vs industry deception
4.‚Äã Hidden Knowledge - Professional secrets made public
5.‚Äã Preventable Tragedy - Suffering that didn't need to happen
6.‚Äã Mechanism Discovery - "This changes everything we thought we knew"
WRITING PRINCIPLES:
‚óè‚Äã Use specific numbers, timeframes, and measurements throughout
‚óè‚Äã Create scannable subheadlines that tell complete story
‚óè‚Äã Mix paragraph lengths for rhythm (1-4 sentences)
‚óè‚Äã Bold key revelations and statistics using **
‚óè‚Äã Build each section to emotional peak
‚óè‚Äã End sections with powerful realizations
‚óè‚Äã Write at 5th-8th grade reading level
SUBHEADLINE STRATEGY: Write subheadlines that work as standalone story beats:
‚óè‚Äã State facts, not questions
‚óè‚Äã Include specific outcomes/numbers
‚óè‚Äã Build progressive revelation
‚óè‚Äã Create urgency to read more
‚óè‚Äã Format: ## [Specific Outcome/Revelation]
TRANSITION PHRASES TO USE:
‚óè‚Äã "That's when I discovered..."
‚óè‚Äã "But here's what [shocked/angered] me..."

‚óè‚Äã "The research revealed something that changed everything..."
‚óè‚Äã "What happened next defied [X] years of conventional wisdom..."
STRUCTURAL REQUIREMENTS:
‚óè‚Äã Opening: 100-150 words
‚óè‚Äã Each major section: 150-200 words
‚óè‚Äã Include 3 testimonials at end (specific results)
‚óè‚Äã Multiple CTAs throughout (at least 3)
‚óè‚Äã Disclaimer at bottom
ADAPTATION CHECKLIST: ‚ñ° Who is your authority figure? (Include specific credentials) ‚ñ°
What "accepted failure" are they exposing? ‚ñ° What is the UMP (hidden mechanism causing
problem)? ‚ñ° What is the UMS (how solution addresses the UMP)? ‚ñ° What professional
solution exists? ‚ñ° What creates urgency to act now? ‚ñ° What's the specific discount
percentage? ‚ñ° What's the guarantee period?
CRITICAL SUCCESS FACTORS:
1.‚Äã The UMP must feel newly discovered but make immediate sense
2.‚Äã The UMS must directly solve the UMP (not just be better)
3.‚Äã The authority must have both credentials AND personal stake
4.‚Äã The solution must already exist (not newly invented)
5.‚Äã Every claim needs specific evidence (numbers, studies, examples)
6.‚Äã The reader must feel validated, not blamed
The advertorial should feel like an expos√© - an insider revealing industry secrets to protect
consumers. Every element builds toward the revelation that suffering was unnecessary and
the solution already exists.
Generate the advertorial now, ensuring the UMP and UMS are clearly established and that
each section flows naturally while maintaining the emotional arc from problem recognition
through validation to urgent action.

PHASE 2: LLM INSTRUCTIONS

IMPROVED HEADLINE GENERATION
INSTRUCTIONS FOR PART 4
PART 4: HEADLINE GENERATION
Claude, now generate 10-15 headline options based on the research provided above. Use the
angle specified (if provided) and the research from Q1-Q20 to create headlines following the
patterns below.

CRITICAL HEADLINE RULES FOR STAGE 5 (UNAWARE):

‚ùå

‚úÖ

RULE 1: NEVER NAME THE PROBLEM OR SOLUTION
DON'T mention: kidney disease,
UTI, blockage, crystals, dehydration, water fountain, hydration
DO call out: emotions,
behaviors, identity, consequences, observations
RULE 2: USE DIVERSE CLASSIC FORMATS Don't default to "To [WHO] Who [BEHAVIOR]"
for every headline. Rotate through ALL patterns below. Maximum 2 headlines can use the same
pattern.
RULE 3: BALANCE SPECIFICITY
‚óè‚Äã Too broad: "To Anyone Who Worries About Their Pet" (could be any pet owner)
‚óè‚Äã Too specific/problem-aware: "To Cat Owners Who've Had A Blockage" (names the
problem)
‚óè‚Äã PERFECT: "Who Else Counts Wet Spots In The Litter Box?" (specific behavior, no
problem named)
RULE 4: TEST EACH HEADLINE Before including a headline, ask:
1.‚Äã Does this name a medical condition or problem? (If YES ‚Üí reject)
2.‚Äã Would someone WITHOUT this problem still read it? (If NO ‚Üí reject)
3.‚Äã Does the avatar recognize themselves? (If NO ‚Üí reject)
4.‚Äã Have I already used this pattern twice? (If YES ‚Üí use different pattern)

COMPREHENSIVE HEADLINE PATTERN LIBRARY
GROUP A: IDENTITY/BEHAVIOR CALLOUTS (Schwartz + 100 Greatest)
1.‚Äã "THE SECRET OF [DESIRED STATE/BEHAVIOR]"
2.‚Äã "TO [WHO] WHO [SPECIFIC BEHAVIOR]..."
3.‚Äã "WHO ELSE [SPECIFIC BEHAVIOR]?"
4.‚Äã "ARE YOU EVER [EMOTIONAL STATE] AT [SITUATION]?"
5.‚Äã "THE CHILD WHO [UNUSUAL ACHIEVEMENT]" ‚Üí "THE [PERSON] WHO [UNUSUAL
BEHAVIOR]"
6.‚Äã "HOW TO [DESIRED OUTCOME]"
7.‚Äã "WHO ELSE WANTS [DESIRED STATE]?"
8.‚Äã "[NUMBER] TYPES OF [PEOPLE] ‚Äì WHICH GROUP ARE YOU?"

GROUP B: MISTAKES/PROBLEMS (100 Greatest)
9.‚Äã "DO YOU MAKE THESE MISTAKES IN [SITUATION]?"
10.‚Äã"A LITTLE MISTAKE THAT COST [PERSON] [BIG NUMBER/CONSEQUENCE]"
11.‚Äã"THE CRIMES WE COMMIT AGAINST [THING WE CARE ABOUT]"
12.‚Äã"LITTLE LEAKS THAT KEEP [PEOPLE] [STRUGGLING STATE]"
13.‚Äã"DO YOU DO ANY OF THESE [NUMBER] EMBARRASSING THINGS?"
14.‚Äã"WHOSE FAULT WHEN [BAD OUTCOME HAPPENS]?"
15.‚Äã"DOES YOUR [THING] EVER EMBARRASS YOU?"

medical fear.‚Äã

shameful/obsessive behavior. Avatar 2 does this daily.
