UPDATED STEP 2B FOR UNAWARE
STORY LEAD
Replace your current Step 2b "Engineer The Gradualization Sequence" with this:

Step 2b: Engineer The Gradualization Sequence
Using the belief shift from Q1 (old belief) ‚Üí Q3 (new belief), YOU must engineer the
gradualization path. DO NOT allow the copy to state the new belief directly. Break the belief shift
into discoverable micro-steps.
REQUIRED: Map out 5-7 micro-steps that lead from old belief to new belief:
Micro-Step 1: Introduce Small Doubt What tiny anomaly challenges the old belief? Frame as
confusion, not revelation Example: "But I noticed Sarah's cat was on wet food and STILL got
diagnosed..."
Micro-Step 2: Show Pattern The anomaly isn't isolated‚Äîit happens to others Example:
"Started asking around. Lot of cats on wet food still get kidney disease."
Micro-Step 3: Create Question What question does this pattern raise? Example: "If wet food
prevents it, why are so many cats on wet food still getting sick?"
Micro-Step 4: Introduce New Information (CRITICAL - Choose the Right Tier)
The TYPE of new information determines how powerful the belief shift is.
THE 4 TIERS OF NEW INFORMATION (Universal Framework):

TIER 1 - Reframe Existing Observable Problem (MOST POWERFUL)

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Take something they already experience and explain it differently
Makes them realize "I've been looking at this wrong the whole time"
No new problem to create awareness of - just new understanding of existing reality
They can often verify/observe this themselves immediately

Examples for Unaware:
‚óè‚Äã "Why cats on wet food diets still get kidney disease - it's not the food quality, it's the
moisture gap"
‚óè‚Äã "Why sleeping separately becomes permanent - bodies forget each other's warmth,
oxytocin production stops"
‚óè‚Äã "Why you're exhausted by 3pm even when you 'didn't do much' - attention residue from
40+ task switches"
Power: Highest. Reframes their lived experience. Often immediately verifiable. Best for: Any
awareness level, especially Unaware (helps them see what's already happening)

TIER 2 - Explain Why Previous Solutions Failed (VERY POWERFUL)
‚óè‚Äã Reveals why the solutions they tried (or think would work) couldn't work
‚óè‚Äã Removes self-blame and validates their experience
‚óè‚Äã Shows it wasn't their fault - the solutions were fundamentally incomplete
Examples for Unaware:
‚óè‚Äã "Water fountains can't work because they only change delivery, not what cats' biology
recognizes as hydration"
‚óè‚Äã "Date nights don't fix it because the problem isn't time together - it's that your bodies
have forgotten each other"
‚óè‚Äã "To-do lists don't address attention residue - your brain is still processing the last task"
Power: Very high. Explains pattern of failure. Creates relief ("it wasn't me"). Best for: Unaware,
Problem-Aware, Solution-Aware (validates why nothing worked)

TIER 3 - Introduce New Mechanism for Familiar Problem (POWERFUL)
‚óè‚Äã Shows there's a different way to solve what they know (or vaguely sense) needs solving
‚óè‚Äã Reveals a mechanism/process they didn't know existed
‚óè‚Äã Positions your solution as different approach, not better execution
Examples for Unaware:
‚óè‚Äã "Prey-model electrolytes trigger cats' biological hydration recognition at cellular level"

‚óè‚Äã "Tactile reactivation - 15 minutes of intentional touch retrains nervous systems to
recognize each other"
‚óè‚Äã "23-minute transition buffers between tasks eliminate attention residue, restore deep
focus"
Power: Good. Offers new path forward. Best for: Problem-Aware, Solution-Aware (reveals new
solution path)

TIER 4 - Create Awareness of Problem They Didn't Know Existed (LEAST POWERFUL BUT SOMETIMES NECESSARY FOR UNAWARE)
‚óè‚Äã Introduces entirely new problem they weren't worried about
‚óè‚Äã Requires building awareness before they care
‚óè‚Äã Highest resistance because "I wasn't worried about this before"
Examples for Unaware:
‚óè‚Äã "Chronic dehydration causes kidney damage years before symptoms appear - by the
time cats show signs, 75% of kidney function is gone"
‚óè‚Äã "Sleeping separately for 6+ months rewires your nervous systems to sleep alone - 73%
of couples never return to same bed"
‚óè‚Äã "Task-switching creates cognitive residue that accumulates - by 3pm you're processing
40+ incomplete tasks simultaneously"
Power: Lowest for most markets. Requires most education and proof. BUT: Often necessary for
Unaware markets where they don't even know the problem exists. Best for: Unaware markets
specifically (when creating problem awareness is required)

FOR UNAWARE MARKETS:
Best: Tier 1 or Tier 4
‚óè‚Äã Tier 1: Reframe something they already observe (most powerful)
‚óè‚Äã Tier 4: Create awareness of hidden problem (necessary when they're truly unaware)
Good: Tier 2 or Tier 3
‚óè‚Äã Tier 2: If they've tried things (validates failure)
‚óè‚Äã Tier 3: If they vaguely sense something's wrong (offers mechanism)
Ideal sequence for Unaware:
1.‚Äã Tier 1 or 4 in Micro-Step 4 (reframe reality OR create awareness)

2.‚Äã Tier 2 if applicable (why their attempts failed)
3.‚Äã Tier 3 in Micro-Step 5 (your mechanism)

YOUR JOB in Step 2b:
Identify which tier the new information [Q2 from angle, or Q3 from research] falls into
State it: "The new information is Tier [X]: [Type]"
Explain: "This works for Unaware because: [1-2 sentence explanation]"
Example: "The new information is Tier 1: Reframe Existing Observable Problem. This works for
Unaware because cat owners can immediately check their wet food label, see it shows 42%
moisture, realize cats need 70%, and understand the gap exists in their home right now. They
can verify it in 60 seconds without trusting me first."

Now continue mapping Micro-Steps 4-7:
Micro-Step 4 continued: How to present this new information How will you present this
information based on its tier? What can they observe/verify themselves? What authority
validates it? [Q9] What makes it undeniable? Example: "Found UC Davis study: Cats need 70%
moisture for kidney function. Checked my cat's wet food label: 42% moisture."
Micro-Step 5: Connect to Their Situation How does new information apply to their specific
case? Example: "Checked my cat's wet food label: 42% moisture. Even premium brands top out
at 45%."
Micro-Step 6: Show Gap What's the difference between what they thought and what's true?
Example: "Wet food is BETTER than dry, but still leaves a 25-30% moisture gap."
Micro-Step 7: State New Belief as Discovery Frame as realization, not claim Example: "That's
when I realized: wet food alone isn't enough. The gap is still there."
Each micro-step will become 3-5 paragraphs in the actual copy. Total pacing: Spend paragraphs
11-30 walking through these steps.
CRITICAL: The reader should feel like they're discovering this WITH you, not being sold TO.

[Q3]
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Can they observe/verify the new information in their own life? (Tier 1 is strongest)

3.‚Äã How do I know this is real? [From Q8, Q9, Q12]

PART 6: WRITING INSTRUCTIONS
Now write the ad copy following these principles:
CORE STRUCTURE: The 10-Question Framework
Your copy must answer all 10 questions in sequence. Each question builds on the previous one.
Flow:

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Q#1-3: Opening/Lead (first 1/3) - Hook, promise, proof
Q#4-6: Problem crystallization (middle 1/3) - Why they failed, enemy, urgency
Q#7-8: Authority & mechanism (middle 1/3) - Credibility, how it works
Q#9-10: Offer & close (final 1/3) - No-brainer offer, push-pull close

LENGTH & PACING
Target: 2,500-3,000 words (MAXIMUM 3,000)
Do NOT exceed 3,000 words. Tight is better than comprehensive.
Pacing discipline:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Each emotional beat appears maximum 2x
Failed solutions: 2-3 examples max
Discovery story: Key moments only, not play-by-play
Authority dialogue: One good explanation
Transformation timeline: 4-5 data points max

If running long, cut:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Repetitive emotional beats
Extra failed solution examples
Over-detailed discovery story
Multiple authority conversations
Excessive transformation data points

OPENING: Story Lead (Confession Style)
Use the confession-style story lead for Stage 5 (Unaware) markets.
Structure:
1. Identification headline [From Q1, Q2, Q15 Level 1]
‚óè‚Äã Call out their surface problem or hidden emotional state
‚óè‚Äã Make them think: "That's me. How did they know?"
2. Confession opening [From Q2]
‚óè‚Äã "There's a moment [specific time] I've never told anyone about..."
‚óè‚Äã Extreme specificity: exact times, physical sensations, internal thoughts
3. The shameful moment [From Q10]
‚óè‚Äã What were they doing?
‚óè‚Äã What were they feeling?

‚óè‚Äã What were they thinking?
4. Public vs. Private contrast [From Q3]
‚óè‚Äã What they say to others
‚óè‚Äã What they actually feel
‚óè‚Äã The gap between them
THE EMOTIONAL PIVOT (CRITICAL - Must Happen By Paragraph 7)
From Schwartz (Breakthrough Advertising, pg 52): "There are always TWO levels to every
desire: the stated want and the emotional drive. Your headline identifies Level 1. Your opening
body copy must IMMEDIATELY pivot to Level 2, or you'll get attention but no sales."
From Evaldo (16-Word Sales Letter, pg 37-39): "This is where most copywriters fuck up. They
list surface benefits. NOBODY CARES ABOUT THOSE THINGS. They care about what those
things GIVE them emotionally. The surface benefit is the MECHANISM for the emotional payoff.
If you don't connect to the emotional payoff in Q#2, they won't buy. Period."
From Masterson (Great Leads, pg 78-79): "Weak story leads show only the surface
transformation. Strong story leads show the EMOTIONAL transformation that the surface
change created. Show the surface transformation, but SELL the emotional one. That's what
people actually buy."
ALL THREE FRAMEWORKS TEACH: Surface problem hooks attention. Deep emotion drives
purchase.
Required pivot structure:
Paragraphs 1-3: Surface Problem Identification
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Open with confession about surface issue
Build recognition ("that's me")
Show public vs private split
Establish the Level 1 stated want

Paragraphs 4-7: THE PIVOT (CRITICAL - THIS IS Q#2)
‚óè‚Äã "But here's what [nobody tells you / I didn't expect / I didn't understand] about [surface
problem]..."
‚óè‚Äã Reveal Level 2 desire (the emotional drive) [From Q15 Level 2]
‚óè‚Äã Use the pivot moment [From Q15 - The Pivot Moment]
‚óè‚Äã Make it physical/visceral (phantom weight, flinching at touch, tightness in chest)
‚óè‚Äã Show specific moments that illustrate the emotional loss
‚óè‚Äã This is what they're actually buying for
‚óè‚Äã This answers Q#2: "What's in it for me?" (BOTH surface AND emotional)

After the pivot, everything serves the emotional desire:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

All proof should show emotional transformation, not just surface results
Mechanism should explain how it enables emotional payoff
Failed solutions failed because they didn't address emotional need
Offer should emphasize emotional benefit

Pivot language examples:
‚óè‚Äã "But here's what nobody tells you about [surface problem]: You don't just lose [obvious
thing]. You lose [deep emotional thing]."
‚óè‚Äã "I thought it was about [surface problem]. It wasn't. It was about [deeper emotional
truth]."
‚óè‚Äã "[Surface problem] was manageable. What I couldn't handle was [emotional
consequence]."
‚óè‚Äã "For [timeframe], I told myself it was just about [surface problem]. But the real problem
was [emotional truth]."
The pivot must:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Happen by paragraph 7 LATEST (preferably paragraph 4-5)
Use sensory/physical details (not intellectual explanation)
Show what they're actually losing (not just inconvenienced by)
Make them FEEL it (chest tightness, throat lump, visceral recognition)
Reference specific relationship/life moments that are lost

SCHWARTZ PRINCIPLES (Apply Throughout)
Principle 1: Extreme Specificity

‚ùå

Generic = sounds fake:
"I was stressed and it affected my relationship" Schwartz-level =
sounds real: ‚úì "At 11pm on a Tuesday, I sat in my driveway unwrapping a Big Mac‚Äîmy fourth
drive-through stop that week‚Äîwatching my family through the window"
Required details:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Specific times (but natural - see timestamp authenticity below)
Exact numbers ("fourth time," "three months")
Physical sensations ("tightness in chest," "heart racing")
Concrete actions ("staring at phone," "checking if breathing")
Direct internal dialogue

CRITICAL - TIMESTAMP AUTHENTICITY:

‚ùå Too specific (feels calculated):

‚óè‚Äã "11:34pm" (exact minute)
‚óè‚Äã "forty-seven minutes" (hyper-precise)
‚óè‚Äã "3:17am" (oddly specific)
‚úì Specific but natural:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"Late at night" or "After 11pm"
"Almost an hour" or "close to an hour"
"Middle of the night" or "early morning"
"Tuesday evening" (day + general time)

Rule: Use round numbers ("fifteen minutes," "half an hour") or general time windows.
Down-to-the-minute precision feels performative.
Principle 2: ABT (And-But-Therefore) Structure
Use for proof sections and story beats:
‚óè‚Äã AND: Set up background, create momentum
‚óè‚Äã BUT: Introduce conflict/tension (the critical element)
‚óè‚Äã THEREFORE: Present solution
Example: "I tried everything AND spent thousands on solutions BUT nothing worked until I
discovered the real problem THEREFORE I finally lost 34 pounds"
Principle 3: Intensification
Make them feel the pain MORE before offering solution.
Three methods (use all three):
1.‚Äã Verbalization - Make them SAY internally what they feel: [Use exact quotes from Q10,
Q16]‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã "Every morning I check if she's breathing"
‚óã‚Äã "Every time she sleeps too still, I panic"
2.‚Äã Multiplication - Show it's happening MORE than realized: [Use frequency from Q10]‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã "Not just once, but 3-4 times per night"
‚óã‚Äã "Not just checking once, but every single hour"
3.‚Äã Dramatization - Show future pain if unsolved: [Use consequences from Q18]‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã "By the time symptoms show, 75% of kidney function is gone"
‚óã‚Äã "She won't be this young forever"
Principle 4: Gradualization

Lead them step-by-step. Each step is small.
DO NOT jump from: "Random stranger" ‚Üí "Buy my product"
DO lead through:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Step 1: "You're not alone in this fear" (identification)
Step 2: "It's not your fault" (remove guilt)
Step 3: "There's a reason it's happening" (awareness)
Step 4: "There's a solution" (hope)
Step 5: "Here's how it works" (mechanism)
Step 6: "Here's how to get it" (offer)

Principle 5: Failed Solutions Stack
Pattern:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

List 3-5 things [From Q6]
Include costs
Say why each failed [From Q7]
Build to desperation

Example from Evaldo (16-Word Sales Letter): "I'd tried everything: hired three different coaches
at $2,000 each, bought every course on Clickbank, attended two $5,000 seminars, tried to DIY it
with YouTube videos.
Nothing worked because none of them addressed the actual problem‚ÄîI didn't have a traffic
problem. I had a conversion problem."
Principle 6: Authority Validation (NOT Testimonials)
Authority figure INSIDE the story explains mechanism.
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Authority enters naturally [From Q9]
They explain WHY it works (mechanism)
In plain language
Reframes from "character flaw" to "solvable problem"

Example from Great Leads (Masterson): "My doctor leaned forward.
'What you're experiencing isn't willpower failure. When you restrict calories too severely, your
body interprets it as starvation. Your metabolism slows down to conserve energy. That's why
you're exhausted and why the weight comes back.'
She paused.

'If we fix your metabolism first, the weight comes off naturally. You're not broken‚Äîyour approach
has been backwards.'"
Principle 7: The Accidental Discovery
Never: "I searched for a solution and found this" Always: "I stumbled on this by accident..."
Pattern:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Unexpected circumstance [From Q8]
Surprising result
Confusion (what's different?)
Investigation (aha moment)
Verification test (proves causation)

Example from 16-Word Sales Letter (Evaldo): "I wasn't even trying to fix my business. I was just
helping my friend debug his sales funnel because he'd bought me dinner.
While I was in his account, I noticed something weird. His conversion rate was 8%. Mine was
2%.
Same traffic source. Same niche. Same offer structure.
'What are you doing differently?' I asked.
He showed me one small change in his checkout sequence.
I tested it in my funnel that night. Conversion rate jumped to 7% within 48 hours."
Principle 8: Transformation Timeline with Metrics
Vague (weak): "Things got better and I felt great"
Schwartz-style (strong): [Use timeline from Q12]
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"First night: [specific result]
Day 3: [observable change]
Week 1: [measurable improvement]
Week 2: [external validation - what others noticed]
Week 3: [behavior that stopped]
Month 1: [emotional shift through action]
Month 3: [current state]"

Example from Breakthrough Advertising (Schwartz): "Day 1: Lost 2 pounds (water weight, but
motivating) Day 3: Stopped feeling hungry between meals Week 1: Down 4 pounds, energy
returning Week 2: Wife commented 'you seem happier' Week 3: Ordered smaller pants Month 1:

Down 15 pounds, stopped avoiding mirrors Month 3: Lost 32 pounds total, feel like myself
again"
Required:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Specific timeframes (not "soon" but "Day 3")
Observable changes (not "felt better" but "lost 4 pounds")
External validation (what others noticed)
Behaviors that stopped
Emotional shifts through actions

FRAMEWORK DISCIPLINE (CRITICAL)
Do NOT add extra emotional beats between framework sections.
FORBIDDEN patterns:
‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå Establishing panic ‚Üí Public/private split ‚Üí Panic AGAIN ‚Üí Failed solutions‚Äã

‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Establishing panic ‚Üí Public/private split ‚Üí IMMEDIATELY to failed solutions‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå Failed solutions ‚Üí Extra desperation beat ‚Üí THEN discovery‚Äã

‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Failed solutions ‚Üí IMMEDIATELY to discovery‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå Research at discovery ‚Üí Return home ‚Üí Research AGAIN‚Äã

‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Research ONCE (either at discovery OR after returning, not both)‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå Story ‚Üí Break into criticism/blame ‚Üí Back to story‚Äã

‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Stay IN story, make it personal ("nobody told me"), not systemic ("they know but don't
tell you")‚Äã

Rule: Each beat appears ONCE, maybe twice if critical. Three times = you're circling.
BACKSTORY WARNING (CRITICAL)
Backstory = telling how you got here Present story = what's happening now
Backstory KILLS momentum. Avoid it.
When backstory is acceptable:
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì In the opening (before main crisis starts)

‚óè‚Äã ‚úì As a single sentence callback mid-story
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì When it's literally the discovery moment
When backstory kills your ad:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå Middle of panic spiral
‚ùå Right after emotional peak
‚ùå To justify why something matters
‚ùå More than 50 words

RULE: If subject is universally understood (baby, parent, spouse) ‚Üí NO backstory needed If
subject needs justification (pet, hobby, job) ‚Üí ONE line maximum, or cut it
GRADUALIZATION EXECUTION (Paragraphs 11-30 - CRITICAL)
You mapped the gradualization sequence in Step 2b. Now execute it in the copy.
Paragraphs 11-30: Walk through your 5-7 micro-steps
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Each micro-step = 3-5 paragraphs
Use ABT structure within each step (And-But-Therefore)
Frame as investigation/discovery, not claims
Include physical details, dialogue, specific moments
Use their actual language [Q16]

PACING DISCIPLINE:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

DO NOT state the new belief before paragraph 28
If you're explaining mechanism before paragraph 25, you're going too fast
Each step must feel like natural progression of thought
The doubt must build gradually across 15-20 paragraphs

FORBIDDEN patterns in paragraphs 11-30:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå "I was wrong about [old belief]"
‚ùå "The truth is [new belief]"
‚ùå "Here's what I discovered: [new belief]"
‚ùå Jumping from doubt ‚Üí new belief in under 10 paragraphs
‚ùå Stating the mechanism before building sufficient doubt

REQUIRED patterns:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚úì "But I noticed something weird..." (doubt)
‚úì "That didn't make sense..." (confusion)
‚úì "So I started investigating..." (curiosity)
‚úì "Found a study that stopped me cold..." (new information)

‚óè‚Äã ‚úì "I checked my own [situation]..." (personal application)
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì "That's when it clicked..." (realization)
MICRO-STEP EXECUTION GUIDE:
Micro-Step 1 (Introduce Small Doubt) - Paragraphs 11-13:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Share the specific anomaly that didn't fit
Frame as confusion, not criticism of old belief
Make it concrete: names, situations, specifics
Reader should think: "Huh, that IS weird"

Micro-Step 2 (Show Pattern) - Paragraphs 14-16:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Show this isn't isolated‚Äîhappened to others too
Use actual examples or research [Q8, Q16]
Build from "weird coincidence" to "consistent pattern"
Reader should think: "Why does this keep happening?"

Micro-Step 3 (Create Question) - Paragraphs 17-19:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Articulate the question the pattern raises
Show you investigating, not knowing the answer yet
Include failed attempts to explain it with old belief
Reader should think: "Yeah, what DOES explain this?"

Micro-Step 4 (Introduce New Information) - Paragraphs 20-23:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Present the new information using the tier you identified in Step 2b
If Tier 1 (Observable Proof): Show them what to look at in their own life right now
If Tier 2 (Reframe Problem): Explain their pain differently than they've heard before
If Tier 3 (Why Solutions Failed): Reveal the mechanism that makes past solutions
incomplete
Use authority figure dialogue if available [Q9]
Make it verifiable‚Äîthey should be able to test/observe this
Frame as discovery, not argument
Keep it simple, one key insight per paragraph
Reader should think: "That would explain it..." OR "Holy shit, I can see that right now"

TIER 1 EXECUTION (Observable Proof): Guide them to look at something in their immediate
environment:
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì "Check your cat's water bowl right now. How much is gone?"
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì "Look at your wet food label. What's the moisture percentage?"
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì "Do the math: If your cat eats 6oz of wet food at 42% moisture, that's 2.5oz of
water..." Reader can verify in 60 seconds = belief shift is instant and sticky

TIER 2-3 EXECUTION (Reframe/Failed Solutions): Present research, authority insight, or
mechanism:
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Use authority dialogue [Q9] to explain mechanism
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Connect to their pattern of experience
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Show why this explains what they've been seeing Reader recognizes pattern = belief
shift through understanding
Micro-Step 5 (Connect to Their Situation) - Paragraphs 24-26:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Apply new information to your specific case
Show the verification moment [Q8]
Include concrete details (checking labels, doing math, testing)
Reader should think: "Wait, that applies to ME too"

Micro-Step 6 (Show Gap) - Paragraphs 27-29:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Reveal the difference between assumption and reality
Quantify the gap if possible
Explain why old belief seemed right but wasn't complete
Reader should think: "Oh my god, I didn't realize..."

Micro-Step 7 (State New Belief as Discovery) - Paragraph 30:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"That's when I realized [new belief]"
Frame as shared discovery
Connect back to their original problem/fear [Q1, Q10]
Reader should think: "Of course! That's why nothing worked!"

SCHWARTZ'S TEST at each micro-step: "Could the reader reject this thought?"
‚óè‚Äã If YES ‚Üí add more setup, slow down, provide more proof
‚óè‚Äã If NO ‚Üí the step is small enough, continue to next step
By paragraph 30, the new belief should feel like the reader's conclusion, not your pitch. The next
sections (mechanism, offer) simply provide the solution to the belief they now hold.
THE ENEMY SECTION (Q#5)
Attack who they already resent [From Q17]
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Describe failed solution in detail
Show why it failed
Reveal who's to blame
Make it "us vs. them"

Example from 16-Word Sales Letter (Evaldo): "And don't get me started on those $2,000
'business coaches.'
I hired three of them.
The first one gave me a 47-page PDF and never answered my emails again. Two grand for a
glorified eBook.
The second one spent six weeks teaching me 'mindset' when what I needed was a funnel that
converted.
The third one kept pushing me to 'invest in more coaching.' His entire business model was
selling more coaching.
I called him out on it.
You know what he said?
'If you're not willing to invest in yourself, you'll never succeed.'
Translation: 'Keep paying me even though nothing's working.'
These people don't care if your business grows.
They care about monthly recurring revenue from desperate entrepreneurs."
URGENCY SECTION (Q#6)
Create urgency through CONSEQUENCE, not fake scarcity [Use consequences from Q18]
Pattern:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"I'm not trying to scare you, but..."
State the progressive nature of problem
Show window of opportunity
Emphasize time lost can't be recovered

Example from Breakthrough Advertising (Schwartz): "Look, I'm not trying to scare you.
But here's what my accountant told me when I finally hired one:
'Every month you don't fix your bookkeeping, you're paying 30-40% more in taxes than you
should. That money is just... gone. You can't get it back.'
I'd been in business for three years.
I did the math. I'd overpaid roughly $47,000 in taxes because I didn't know what I was doing.

Forty-seven thousand dollars. Gone.
Every month you wait is more money you'll never get back."
MECHANISM SECTION (Q#8)
Explain how it actually works [From Q4]
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"So here's what makes product different..."
Explain mechanism in plain language
Contrast with how competitors work
Tie back to their biology/psychology
Make it believable through authority validation [From Q9]
Use existing beliefs from their world, not jargon.

Example from Great Leads (Masterson): "So here's what makes this approach different from
every diet you've tried.
Most diets focus on WHAT you eat. Count calories. Cut carbs. Eliminate sugar.
That's backwards.
Your body doesn't care about calories. It cares about WHEN you eat.
When you eat throughout the day, your insulin stays elevated. Elevated insulin tells your body:
'Store fat. Don't burn it.'
But when you compress your eating into a shorter window‚Äîsay, 8 hours instead of 16‚Äîyour
insulin drops for extended periods.
Low insulin tells your body: 'Burn fat. We need energy.'
That's why people lose weight even eating the same foods. It's not about what. It's about when.
My doctor explained it like this: 'Your body is either in fat-storage mode or fat-burning mode. You
can't be in both. When you eat constantly, you're in storage mode 16 hours a day. When you
fast, you flip the switch.'"
OFFER SECTION (Q#9)
Structure the no-brainer offer [From Q19]
Pattern:
‚óè‚Äã Acknowledge price concern

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Break down costs (manufacturing reality OR value comparison)
State regular price
Reveal promotional price
List what's included (value stack)
Add bonuses with values
Calculate total value
Show their price
Guarantee (risk reversal)

Example: "Look, I know what you're thinking.
'This sounds expensive.'
And honestly? It should be.
When I was building this course, I hired three different experts. Paid a developer $8,000 to build
the software. Spent six months testing it with beta users.
My all-in cost was over $30,000.
Most course creators would slap a 10x markup on that and call it a day. That'd be $300,000.
But I'm not trying to build a guru empire. I'm trying to help people who were where I was.
So here's what I'm doing:
The [Product Name] normally retails for $497.
That includes:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

The complete [framework/system]
[Software/tool]
[Template library]
Lifetime updates

Total value: $497
But right now, you're not paying $497.
For the first 100 people, I'm offering it for $197.
That's $300 off.
Why?
Because I know once you see results, you'll tell other people. Word of mouth is how I've grown.

But wait‚ÄîI'm also including:
Bonus #1: [Name] ($97 value) [Description]
Bonus #2: [Name] ($67 value) [Description]
Bonus #3: [Name] ($47 value) [Description]
Let's do the math:
Bonus #1: $97 Bonus #2: $67 Bonus #3: $47
Total Value: $708
Your Price Today: $197
And because I know buying something online feels risky, here's my guarantee:
The [Name] 60-Day Guarantee
Try product for 60 days. If you don't [specific result]‚Äîor if you're unhappy for ANY
reason‚Äîsend it back. I'll refund every penny. Including return shipping.
You literally cannot lose."
CLOSING: Push-Pull Technique (Q#10)
CRITICAL: Your closing must be ORIGINAL.
FORBIDDEN patterns:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå Using identical phrases from reference ads
‚ùå "X months ago... Today..." (overused time contrast structure)
‚ùå Future predictions: "She'll get up in a few minutes. Walk over. Drink."
‚ùå Redemption clich√©: "Because I didn't fail [them] after all"
‚ùå Template feeling - if you could swap 5 words for another product, you failed

ACCEPTABLE elements:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚úì Direct address (vary the phrasing - not "If you're reading this..." every time)
‚úì Present moment description (without formulaic predictions)
‚úì Reflection on past self
‚úì Insight about what was really happening
‚úì Soft product mention with trial/guarantee
‚úì Brief timeframe contrast (just don't make it the entire structure)

Structure:

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Give them control ("I can't force you...")
Present options (do nothing, try competitors, get yours)
Be honest about what each option means
No fake scarcity, but real consequence
Soft invitation, not hard push

Example from Evaldo (16-Word Sales Letter): "Here's the thing...
I can't force you to do anything.
You have three options right now:
Option 1: Keep doing what you're doing.
Keep trying to figure it out on your own. Maybe you'll crack the code eventually.
But how much time and money are you willing to lose while you're learning?
Option 2: Hire an expensive coach.
Go find someone who charges $3,000-5,000 for six weeks of their time.
Maybe they'll be good. Maybe they'll just give you another PDF and disappear.
Option 3: Get Product.
For $197, you get the exact system I used to go from broke to $80K/month in eight months.
The same system 2,400+ people have used to get results.
With a 60-day guarantee that means you risk absolutely nothing.
I'll be honest with you...
Some people buy this and never open it. Some people watch the first module and quit.
But the people who actually implement it‚Äîwho follow the steps, do the work, put in the
hours‚Äîthey get results.
I can't guarantee you'll make $80K/month. That'd be illegal.
But I can guarantee that if you follow this system, you'll have a funnel that converts better than
what you have now.
The choice is yours.
I'm not going to hit you with fake scarcity or countdown timers.

This offer is real. The $197 price is for first-time customers. The 60-day guarantee is real.
But I will tell you this:
Every day you wait is another day of losing money on a funnel that doesn't convert.
And I learned the hard way that you can't get that time back.
[CTA button/link]
You'll have a better funnel.
You'll stop bleeding money.
And you'll wish you'd done this sooner.
I know I do.
To your success,
[Signature] [Name] [Credentials from Q20]
P.S. - Remember, you're covered by the 60-day guarantee. If product doesn't help you [result],
send it back for a full refund. You risk nothing."
VOICE & TONE
Required voice:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

First person ONLY (I/me/my)
Confessional - like telling a secret to a close friend
Vulnerable - admitting shameful truths
Detailed - extreme specificity signals truth
Conversational but serious
NEVER "marketery" or sales-y

Use their language [From Q16]:
‚óè‚Äã Don't say "concerned" if they say "terrified"
‚óè‚Äã Don't say "issue" if they say "nightmare"
‚óè‚Äã Match their exact phrasing
Sentence structure: Mix short and long for rhythm.
‚óè‚Äã Short for impact: "I froze."
‚óè‚Äã Long for immersion: "I remember sitting in my car late at night, watching my family
through the window eating dessert, feeling my heart race as I unwrapped the Big Mac I'd

just bought, knowing this was my fourth drive-through stop that week, wondering what
was broken in me."
Paragraph length: Vary dramatically.
‚óè‚Äã Single sentences for gut punches
‚óè‚Äã 3-5 sentences for story flow
‚óè‚Äã Occasional longer paragraphs for immersion
Use white space for emphasis
COPYRIGHT COMPLIANCE (CRITICAL)
HARD LIMITS - NEVER VIOLATE:
Limit 1: Quote Length
‚óè‚Äã 15+ words from any single source = SEVERE VIOLATION
‚óè‚Äã If quote would be longer, extract key phrase (under 15 words) OR paraphrase entirely
Limit 2: Quotes Per Source
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

ONE quote per source MAXIMUM
After quoting once, that source is CLOSED
All additional content must be fully paraphrased
2+ quotes from one source = SEVERE VIOLATION

Limit 3: Complete Works
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

NEVER reproduce song lyrics (not even one line)
NEVER reproduce poems (not even one stanza)
NEVER reproduce haikus
NEVER reproduce article paragraphs verbatim

Default to paraphrasing. Quotes should be rare exceptions, not primary method.
Self-check before responding:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Is this quote 15+ words? ‚Üí PARAPHRASE
Have I quoted this source already? ‚Üí PARAPHRASE
Is this a complete creative work? ‚Üí DON'T REPRODUCE
Am I mirroring original phrasing? ‚Üí REWRITE ENTIRELY

FORBIDDEN (Will Kill The Ad)
Never use these words:

‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå Revolutionary, game-changing, amazing, incredible, breakthrough, miracle, limited
time, act now

Never do these things:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå Start selling in first 50% of copy
‚ùå Mention product name in headline
‚ùå Use generic marketing language
‚ùå Break story frame into "sales mode"
‚ùå Use bullet points or feature lists in body copy
‚ùå Include testimonials (authority validation is different)
‚ùå Create urgency through fake scarcity
‚ùå Make health claims without evidence
‚ùå Promise specific results as guarantees

‚úÖ Specific numbers everywhere

PART 7: OUTPUT

This is the complete UNAWARE prompt Claude instructions.

Want me to continue with the three Problem-Aware prompts (Story Lead, Secret Lead,
Proclamation Lead)?

Story Lead (Manual Research)

PROBLEM-AWARE AD COPY
GENERATOR (STORY LEAD)
Use this prompt to generate long-form ad copy for prospects who recognize their problem
immediately but don't yet know your product can solve it.

HOW TO USE THIS PROMPT
STEP 1: (Optional) Specify your angle OR skip to research
STEP 2: Provide research (documents OR answers to manual research questions)
STEP 3: Claude generates 10-15 headline options
STEP 4: Pick your favorite headline
STEP 5: Claude generates full ad copy

WHEN TO USE THIS PROMPT
Use Problem-Aware when your market:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚úÖ Recognizes the problem immediately when mentioned
‚úÖ Doesn't know your product/solution exists yet
‚úÖ May have tried casual things on their own (not formal solutions)
‚úÖ Doesn't understand the mechanism causing the problem

Examples:
‚óè‚Äã Woman whose husband snores - knows it's a problem, hasn't looked for solutions
‚óè‚Äã Cat owner whose cat doesn't drink water - knows it's not ideal, tried moving bowls
around
‚óè‚Äã Person with back pain - knows it hurts, tried different chairs/pillows
‚óè‚Äã Parent whose kid struggles with reading - knows it's an issue, tried reading more
together
DO NOT use Problem-Aware if:
‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå They don't recognize they have a problem ‚Üí Use Unaware instead

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå They've tried actual products/solutions ‚Üí Use Solution-Aware instead
‚ùå They're actively researching solutions ‚Üí Use Solution-Aware instead
‚ùå They know your specific product exists ‚Üí Use Product-Aware instead

THE KEY INSIGHT FOR PROBLEM-AWARE
From Schwartz (Breakthrough Advertising, Stage 4):
"The prospect has‚Äînot a desire‚Äîbut a need. He recognizes the need immediately.
But he doesn't yet realize the connection between the fulfillment of that need and
your product."
"Here you start by naming the need and/or its solution in your headline. Then
dramatize the need so vividly that the prospect realizes just how badly he needs the
solution. And then present your product as the inevitable solution."
The critical difference:
‚óè‚Äã Unaware: Don't know they have a problem
‚óè‚Äã Problem-Aware: Know the problem, DON'T know your solution exists ‚Üê THIS
PROMPT
‚óè‚Äã Solution-Aware: Know solutions exist, have tried them or are comparing
Your job:
1.‚Äã Name the problem in the headline
2.‚Äã Dramatize how bad the problem is
3.‚Äã Reveal the mechanism causing it
4.‚Äã Present your product as the inevitable solution

PART 1: ANGLE SPECIFICATION (OPTIONAL)
1.‚Äã What's their current limiting belief about this problem? What do they believe that allows
them to keep living with the problem? [FILL IN]
Examples for Problem-Aware:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"Snoring is genetic. Some people just snore. There's nothing you can really do about it."
"Cats just don't drink much water - that's normal for cats."
"Back pain is part of getting older - you just have to live with it."
"Some kids struggle with reading - they'll catch up eventually."

2.‚Äã What NEW INFORMATION shifts that belief? (THIS IS YOUR ANGLE) What truth/insight
makes their current belief impossible to maintain? [FILL IN]
Choose which TIER your new information falls into:

TIER 1 - Reframe Existing Observable Problem (MOST POWERFUL)
Take something they already experience and explain it differently. Makes them realize "I've been
looking at this wrong the whole time."
Problem-Aware Examples:
‚óè‚Äã "Snoring isn't genetic - it's caused by cervical spine misalignment during sleep. When
your neck isn't properly supported, your airway collapses. It's positional, not genetic. Fix
the positioning, stop the snoring."
‚óè‚Äã "Cats aren't 'low-thirst animals' - they're biologically designed to get hydration from prey
fluid (70% water + minerals). Plain water lacks these minerals, so their kidneys don't
recognize it as proper hydration. Their 'low thirst drive' is a response to the wrong type of
water."
‚óè‚Äã "Back pain isn't age - it's hip flexor tightness from sitting 8+ hours daily. Tight hip flexors
pull your pelvis forward, compressing your lower spine. The pain is mechanical, not
aging."
Format: "It's not [what they think], it's actually [mechanism they can observe/verify]"
Power: Highest. Reframes something they experience daily.

TIER 2 - Explain Why Previous Solutions Failed (VERY POWERFUL)
Reveal why the casual things they tried couldn't work. Validates their experience.
Problem-Aware Examples:
‚óè‚Äã "Moving water bowls doesn't work because plain water (filtered or not, any location) still
lacks the electrolytes cats need. The bowl location doesn't change the water
composition."
‚óè‚Äã "Different pillows don't fix snoring because they don't address cervical spine alignment they just change head height. Your airway still collapses if your neck isn't properly
supported."

‚óè‚Äã "Stretching doesn't fix back pain if you're only stretching your back. The problem is tight
hip flexors pulling on your pelvis - you have to release the hip flexors, not stretch the
symptom area."
Format: "[Casual attempt] doesn't work because [mechanism that wasn't addressed]"
Power: Very high. Explains why their efforts failed. Removes self-blame.

TIER 3 - Introduce New Mechanism for Familiar Problem (POWERFUL)
Show there's a different way to solve the problem they know they have. Reveals a mechanism
they didn't know existed.
Problem-Aware Examples:
‚óè‚Äã "Prey-model electrolytes (sodium/potassium ratios found in prey blood) trigger cats'
biological hydration recognition. Their taste receptors evolved to detect these specific
minerals - when present, they signal 'proper hydration' and the cat drinks willingly."
‚óè‚Äã "Positional cervical traction - maintains natural neck curve during sleep, keeps airway
open. The mechanism isn't elevating the head, it's supporting the cervical spine
alignment so the airway can't collapse."
‚óè‚Äã "Targeted hip flexor release - 3 specific positions that release psoas tension in under 10
minutes. Releases the pull on your pelvis, decompresses the spine, eliminates the pain
source."
Format: "[New mechanism/approach] works by [process they didn't know about]"
Power: Good. Offers new path they didn't know existed.

TIER 4 - Create Awareness of Problem They Didn't Know Existed (LESS POWERFUL FOR
PROBLEM-AWARE)
Introduces a consequence or aspect they weren't aware of. Lower power for Problem-Aware
because they already know they have a problem.
Problem-Aware Examples:
‚óè‚Äã "Chronic dehydration causes kidney damage years before symptoms appear - by the
time cats show signs, 75% of kidney function is gone."
‚óè‚Äã "Sleep apnea from snoring increases heart attack risk by 30% - most people don't realize
snoring is a cardiovascular issue, not just a noise issue."
‚óè‚Äã "Sitting-induced back pain leads to disc degeneration within 3-5 years if untreated - the
mechanical stress compounds daily."

Format: "[Problem they know] is actually causing [consequence they don't know about]"
Power: Lower for Problem-Aware. They already recognize the problem - adding new problems
can feel like fear-mongering.
Best to avoid unless: The hidden consequence is the REAL motivation (e.g., snoring ‚Üí heart
risk for someone whose parent died of heart attack)

FOR PROBLEM-AWARE:
Best: Tier 1 or Tier 2
‚óè‚Äã Tier 1: Reframe the problem they experience (most powerful)
‚óè‚Äã Tier 2: Explain why casual attempts failed (validates experience)
Good: Tier 3
‚óè‚Äã Introduces new mechanism for known problem
Avoid: Tier 4
‚óè‚Äã They already know they have a problem - piling on more problems feels manipulative
Ideal sequence for Problem-Aware:
1.‚Äã Tier 1 or 2 (reframe problem OR explain failed attempts)
2.‚Äã Tier 3 (your mechanism)

Which tier is your new information? [FILL IN: Tier 1, 2, 3, or 4]
Write your new information here: [FILL IN]

3.‚Äã What's the revelation moment? After learning this new information, what do they
suddenly realize? [FILL IN]
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã "Oh my god - this isn't something I have to live with forever. It's not his genetics. It's
fixable through proper neck support while sleeping. We've been sleeping in separate
rooms for nothing."
‚óè‚Äã "I thought she just didn't like water. But plain water isn't what her kidneys are designed to
process. That's why she ignores it. It's not her - it's the water itself."

‚óè‚Äã "My back pain isn't because I'm getting old. It's because I sit 10 hours a day and my hip
flexors are pulling my pelvis forward. This is mechanical - I can fix this."

4.‚Äã Why is action urgent? Once they accept this new belief, what becomes costly about
doing nothing? [FILL IN]
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã "Every night of poor sleep compounds. The relationship deteriorates a little more. The
pattern of sleeping separately hardens. Early intervention is simple - years of separate
bedrooms becomes the new normal that's hard to reverse."
‚óè‚Äã "Every day she doesn't get proper hydration, kidney damage accumulates. Early kidney
disease is reversible for 6-12 months. After that, it's permanent. By the time symptoms
show, 75% of function is gone."
‚óè‚Äã "Every day of sitting tightens hip flexors more. The mechanical stress on the spine
compounds. What's reversible with 10 minutes daily now becomes chronic disc issues
within 3-5 years."

5.‚Äã What's the hidden fear or feeling beneath the surface? What are they feeling but won't
say out loud? [FILL IN]
Common hidden emotions: Fear, Guilt, Shame, Grief, Inadequacy, Loss
Examples:
‚óè‚Äã "Grief for the intimacy they've lost. Fear that they've waited too long and can't get it back.
Shame that they're becoming roommates and neither has the courage to say it out loud."
‚óè‚Äã "Guilt that she thought she was being a good cat parent but might be failing. Fear that
kidney damage has already started and she missed the window. Dread of losing her cat
to something preventable."
‚óè‚Äã "Fear that he's 'broken' somehow - everyone else his age isn't in constant pain. Shame
that he can't play with his kids without his back hurting. Inadequacy - feeling older than
he is."

PART 3: MANUAL RESEARCH INPUT (Option B)
If you didn't upload documents, answer these 15 questions:

Q1: The Problem They Recognize
What problem do they have that they immediately recognize when mentioned? How do THEY
describe it? [FILL IN]
Example: "My cat barely drinks any water. She's 8 years old and I see her drink maybe once a
day. I know cats need water but she just doesn't seem interested in it."

Q2: The Mechanism Behind The Problem

What is the ROOT CAUSE of this problem? Why does this problem happen? (Be technical explain the actual mechanism) [FILL IN]
Example: "Cats evolved in desert climates to get 70-80% of their hydration from prey (mice,
birds). Prey contains water PLUS electrolytes (sodium, potassium, magnesium). Cat kidneys
evolved to process this mineralized fluid efficiently. Plain water lacks these minerals - it doesn't
taste natural to them AND their kidneys don't absorb it as efficiently. That's why cats have 'low
thirst drives' - they're biologically wired to get hydration from food, not plain water."

Q3: What Have They Casually Tried?
What have they tried on their own to deal with this problem? (Not formal "solutions" - just things
they've done) [FILL IN]
Example: "Moved the water bowl to different spots in the house. Bought a second bowl. Tried
filtered water instead of tap. Put the bowl away from her food. Cleaned it more often. Got a
wider bowl because I read cats don't like their whiskers touching the sides."

Q4: Why Didn't Those Things Work?
Why didn't those attempts help? What mechanism were they missing? [FILL IN]
Example: "None of those address the actual problem - plain water (filtered or not, in any bowl)
still lacks the mineral composition cats are biologically designed to drink. Moving the bowl or
changing the bowl type doesn't change the fact that plain water isn't appealing to their taste
receptors and their kidneys don't absorb it efficiently. The mechanism (lack of electrolytes)
wasn't addressed."

Q5: The Emotional Cost
What is this problem costing them emotionally? What do they worry about? What can't they do
or enjoy? What do they say publicly vs. feel privately? [FILL IN]
Example: "Constant low-level anxiety. Every time she sleeps too long I worry - is she
dehydrated? Lethargic? I Google 'cat kidney disease symptoms' at 2am. Can't just enjoy her
without monitoring. Publicly: 'She's healthy, just doesn't drink much.' Privately: Terrified of
waking up to a kidney disease diagnosis and feeling like I failed her."

Q6: The Product & Its Mechanism

What is your product? What is the MECHANISM of how it works? (Be technical - how does it
address the root cause?) [FILL IN]
Example: "HydraBoost - water additive powder with electrolytes (sodium, potassium,
magnesium) in ratios that mimic prey fluid composition. When added to water, it recreates the
mineral profile that cat kidneys evolved to process. The electrolytes make water taste natural to
cats (triggers their biological preference for mineralized fluid) AND increases cellular water
absorption in the kidneys by 3-4x compared to plain water. Addresses both the palatability issue
and the absorption mechanism."

Q7: Mass Desire (Two Levels - CRITICAL)
Level 1 - Surface Want: What would they say they want? [FILL IN] Example: "I want my cat to
drink more water"
Level 2 - Deep Emotional Drive: What do they ACTUALLY want beneath that? [FILL IN]
Example: "Stop living in constant fear of kidney disease. Stop checking if she's breathing at
night. Stop Googling symptoms. Be able to just enjoy my time with her instead of monitoring her.
Peace of mind that she'll be healthy and I won't lose her to something preventable. Not feel like
a failing cat parent."
The Pivot Moment: When does the surface problem reveal the deeper fear? [FILL IN] Example:
"When she realizes it's not about the water bowl - it's about waking up at 3am terrified she'll die
of kidney disease and it'll be her fault for not doing enough."

Q8: Before State (Living With Problem)
Describe their current daily reality - physical sensations, when it's worst, emotional state, what
they tell themselves. [FILL IN]
Example: "Pit in stomach when walking past the full water bowl. Chest tightness when cat
sleeps too deeply. Worst at: bedtime (checking on cat), mornings (bowl still full). What they tell
themselves: 'She's fine, I'm overthinking it,' 'Some cats just don't drink much,' 'I'm being
paranoid.' But they can't shake the anxiety."

Q9: After State (Problem Solved)
What becomes possible when the problem is solved? What feeling returns? What specific
moments become available? [FILL IN]

Example: "Can watch her sleep peacefully without checking if she's breathing. Can enjoy
cuddling without mentally calculating her hydration levels. Relief when seeing her drink multiple
times a day. Confidence at vet checkups instead of dread. Can just BE with her instead of
monitoring her. Peace of mind that her kidneys are protected."

Q10: Transformation Timeline
Describe the progression - what changed first, what came later, typical journey from start to 3
months? [FILL IN]
Example: "Day 1: Cat drank from bowl 3 times (vs. usual once). Owner couldn't believe it. Week
1: Drinking 4-5 times daily, coat shinier, more energetic. Month 1: Vet bloodwork shows
excellent kidney values. Owner stops Googling symptoms at night. Month 3: Cat's energy like a
younger cat, owner's anxiety completely gone, vet confirms kidneys in optimal health."

Q11: Their Actual Language
Paste 5-10 real quotes from Reddit, forums, reviews about this problem. [FILL IN]
Example: "My cat just doesn't drink much water" "I worry about dehydration but what can you
do" "She's always been like this" "I've tried everything - different bowls, locations, nothing works"
"Is this normal? Should I be worried?" "Some cats just aren't big drinkers I guess"

Q12: Discovery Story
How did someone discover this solution exists? What was the "wait, what?" moment? How did
they verify it worked? [FILL IN]
Example: "Friend mentioned her cat (who also barely drank) was now drinking 5x per day.
'How?' I asked. She explained about the electrolyte mechanism - cats need mineralized water,
not plain. I was skeptical but tried it. Day 1, my cat drank 3x. Week later, drinking consistently
throughout the day. Verified by stopping for 2 days - cat went back to barely drinking. Started
again - back to 5x per day. It was clearly the mechanism."

Q13: Authority Validation (Optional)
Did you consult experts? What did they explain about the mechanism? [FILL IN]

Example: "Vet explained cats evolved to get hydration from prey, which contains electrolytes.
Their kidneys process mineralized fluid 3-4x more efficiently than plain water. That's why they
have 'low thirst drives' - biological mismatch. When water has proper mineral ratios, they drink it
willingly and kidneys absorb it properly. The mechanism made perfect sense."

Q14: The Offer
Price, what's included, bonuses, guarantee, payment options. [FILL IN]
Example: "$39 for 2-month supply. Includes: powder, measuring scoop, hydration guide. Bonus:
Cat health checklist. 60-day money-back guarantee - if cat doesn't drink more or you don't see
improvement, full refund including return shipping. Subscribe and save 15%."

Q15: Your Credibility
Why should they trust YOU? Pick one: (1) "I had this problem too," (2) "I'm an
expert/researcher," (3) "I discovered this solution." Then your story. [FILL IN]
Example: "I had this problem too. My cat Mia barely drank water for 8 years. I tried everything different bowls, locations, fountains. Nothing worked. Then I learned about the electrolyte
mechanism from a veterinary nutritionist. Once I understood WHY plain water doesn't work for
cats (biological mismatch with their prey-based hydration system), the solution was obvious.
Created HydraBoost based on prey fluid composition. Mia went from 1 drink per day to 5. Her
kidney values are better at 10 than they were at 8."
END OF USER INPUTS

TEXT.

Step 2: Build The One Belief
The One Belief Formula: "[PROBLEM] is caused by [MECHANISM] - [YOUR PRODUCT] solves
it through [SOLUTION MECHANISM] - giving you [LEVEL 2 EMOTIONAL DESIRE]"
Extract:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Problem: [From Q1]
Mechanism Behind Problem: [From Q2]
Solution: [From Q6]
Mechanism Behind Solution: [From Q6]
Emotional Desire: [From Q7 Level 2]
Belief-Shift Angle: [From Part 1 or identified from research]

Example: "Cats not drinking water (problem) is caused by biological mismatch - plain water
lacks the electrolytes their kidneys evolved to process (mechanism) - HydraBoost (solution)
recreates prey fluid composition (solution mechanism) - giving you peace of mind that your cat's
kidneys are protected and you're not failing as a cat parent (emotional desire)"

Step 2b: Engineer The Gradualization Sequence
Using the belief shift from their current understanding ‚Üí new belief about mechanism/solution,
YOU must engineer the gradualization path.
DO NOT allow the copy to state the new belief directly. Break the belief shift into discoverable
micro-steps.
REQUIRED: Map out 5-7 micro-steps that lead from old belief to new belief:
Micro-Step 1: Introduce Small Doubt
‚óè‚Äã What tiny anomaly challenges their current understanding?
‚óè‚Äã Frame as confusion, not revelation
‚óè‚Äã Example: "But I noticed Sarah's cat was on wet food and STILL got diagnosed..."
Micro-Step 2: Show Pattern
‚óè‚Äã The anomaly isn't isolated‚Äîit happens to others
‚óè‚Äã Example: "Started asking around. Lot of cats on wet food still get kidney disease."
Micro-Step 3: Create Question
‚óè‚Äã What question does this pattern raise?
‚óè‚Äã Example: "If wet food prevents it, why are so many cats on wet food still getting sick?"
Micro-Step 4: Introduce New Information (CRITICAL - Choose the Right Tier)
The TYPE of new information determines how powerful the belief shift is.
HIERARCHY OF NEW INFORMATION (Most Powerful to Least):
Tier 1 - Observable Proof in Their Daily Life
‚óè‚Äã Reframe something they see every day but misinterpret
‚óè‚Äã Makes them realize "I've been looking at this wrong the whole time"
‚óè‚Äã Examples:
‚óã‚Äã "Why cats on wet food diets still get kidney disease"
‚óã‚Äã "Why your skinny friend can eat whatever and stay thin"
‚óã‚Äã "Why you feel more tired after 8 hours of sleep than 6"
‚óè‚Äã Power: They can verify it themselves immediately

‚óè‚Äã Test: Can they look at their own life right now and see the proof?
Tier 2 - Reframe Existing Observable Problem
‚óè‚Äã Take pain they already feel and explain it differently
‚óè‚Äã Shows the problem isn't what they thought it was
‚óè‚Äã Examples:
‚óã‚Äã "It's not an eating problem, it's a hydration problem"
‚óã‚Äã "It's not that you lack discipline, it's that your insulin is spiked 16 hours a day"
‚óã‚Äã "It's not that you need more traffic, it's that your funnel has conversion leaks"
‚óè‚Äã Power: Reframes known pain, removes self-blame
‚óè‚Äã Test: Does this make their existing struggle make sense?
Tier 3 - Explain Why Previous Solutions Failed
‚óè‚Äã Reveals why everything they tried couldn't work
‚óè‚Äã Shows it wasn't their fault‚Äîthe solutions were incomplete
‚óè‚Äã Examples:
‚óã‚Äã "Water fountains don't work because cats avoid still water biologically, not
because water is boring"
‚óã‚Äã "Calorie restriction fails because it slows metabolism, not because you lack
willpower"
‚óã‚Äã "Cold outreach doesn't work because you're interrupting, not because your script
is bad"
‚óè‚Äã Power: Removes shame, validates their experience
‚óè‚Äã Test: Does this explain their pattern of failed attempts?
Tier 4 - Introduce New Mechanism for Familiar Problem
‚óè‚Äã Shows there's a different way to solve what they already know needs solving
‚óè‚Äã Reveals a mechanism/process they didn't know existed
‚óè‚Äã Examples:
‚óã‚Äã "Electrolytes change how cats' kidneys absorb water at a cellular level"
‚óã‚Äã "16:8 fasting flips your body from fat-storage mode to fat-burning mode"
‚óã‚Äã "Attention residue is why you can't focus even when you have time blocked"
‚óè‚Äã Power: Offers new path forward for known problem
‚óè‚Äã Test: Does this reveal a mechanism they can't access with current solutions?
Tier 5 - Create Awareness of Problem They Didn't Know Existed
‚óè‚Äã Introduces entirely new problem they weren't worried about
‚óè‚Äã Hardest to sell because requires building awareness first
‚óè‚Äã Examples:
‚óã‚Äã "Microplastics in your water bottles are disrupting hormones"
‚óã‚Äã "Your standing desk is actually damaging your knees"
‚óã‚Äã "AI is writing 40% of your competitor's content"

‚óè‚Äã Power: Lowest (requires most education, highest resistance)
‚óè‚Äã Test: Were they worried about this before you mentioned it? (If no = Tier 5)
YOUR JOB in Step 2b:
1.‚Äã Identify which tier the new information [from mechanism explanation] falls into
2.‚Äã If it's Tier 4 or 5, see if you can reframe it as Tier 1, 2, or 3
3.‚Äã Tier 1 is almost always strongest (observable proof they can verify now)
4.‚Äã State which tier you're using and why
Format: "The new information is Tier [X]: [Type] This works because: [1-2 sentence explanation
of why this tier is right for this market]"
Example: "The new information is Tier 1: Observable Proof in Their Daily Life This works
because cat owners can immediately look at their cat's water bowl, see it's barely touched,
check their wet food label showing 42% moisture, and realize the gap exists in their own home
right now. They don't need to trust me‚Äîthey can verify it in 60 seconds."
After identifying the tier, continue mapping Micro-Steps 4-7:
Micro-Step 4 continued: How to present this new information
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

How will you present this information based on its tier?
What can they observe/verify themselves?
What authority validates it? [Q13]
What makes it undeniable?
Example: "Found UC Davis study: Cats need 70% moisture for kidney function. Checked
my cat's wet food label: 42% moisture."

Micro-Step 5: Connect to Their Situation
‚óè‚Äã How does new information apply to their specific case?
‚óè‚Äã Example: "Checked my cat's wet food label: 42% moisture. Even premium brands top
out at 45%."
Micro-Step 6: Show Gap
‚óè‚Äã What's the difference between what they thought and what's true?
‚óè‚Äã Example: "Wet food is BETTER than dry, but still leaves a 25-30% moisture gap."
Micro-Step 7: State New Belief as Discovery
‚óè‚Äã Frame as realization, not claim
‚óè‚Äã Example: "That's when I realized: wet food alone isn't enough. The gap is still there."
Each micro-step will become 3-5 paragraphs in the actual copy. Total pacing: Spend paragraphs
11-30 walking through these steps.

CRITICAL: The reader should feel like they're discovering this WITH you, not being sold TO.

Before writing, check:

‚úì Does headline name the need? [Q1]

‚úì Is the new information Tier 1, 2, or 3? (Tier 4-5 require more market sophistication)
[Q2]

‚úì Does it explain why casual attempts failed? [Q3, Q4]

Step 4: Map To Problem-Aware Structure
Schwartz's Problem-Aware Framework (Enhanced with Belief-Shift):
Step 1: Name The Need [Q1]
‚óè‚Äã Headline names the problem they recognize
‚óè‚Äã Opening establishes "I had this too"
Step 2: Dramatize The Need [Q5, Q8]
‚óè‚Äã Show how bad the problem is
‚óè‚Äã Emotional cost, what they can't do
‚óè‚Äã Make them realize how badly they need a solution
Step 3: Reveal The Mechanism + Belief-Shift [Q2, Q3, Q4, Angle]
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Explain WHY the problem happens
Show why casual attempts failed
Deliver the belief-shift from the angle
Blame the mechanism, not them

Step 4: Present Product As Inevitable Solution [Q6, Q9, Q10]
‚óè‚Äã Show how your mechanism solves their mechanism
‚óè‚Äã Proof of transformation
‚óè‚Äã Make solution feel inevitable

PART 6: WRITING INSTRUCTIONS
CORE STRUCTURE: The Mechanism Revelation (With Belief-Shift)
Your copy must reveal WHY the problem happens, then present your product as the inevitable
solution - all structured around the belief-shift angle.
Paragraphs 1-10: NAME & DRAMATIZE THE PROBLEM
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Open with "I had this problem too"
Name the specific problem [Q1]
Dramatize the emotional cost [Q5, Q8]
Show what it prevents them from doing/enjoying

Paragraphs 11-30: REVEAL THE MECHANISM + DELIVER BELIEF-SHIFT
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Explain WHY the problem happens [Q2]
Show what they've tried [Q3]
Explain why those things failed [Q4]
Deliver the belief-shift from your angle
Blame the mechanism, not them
"It's not your fault - you didn't know about the mechanism"

Paragraphs 31-50: THE DISCOVERY & SOLUTION MECHANISM
‚óè‚Äã

Before/After [Q8, Q9]
The offer [Q14]
Close with credibility [Q15]

LENGTH & PACING
Target: 2,000-2,500 words
Problem-Aware is SHORTER than Unaware:

‚óè‚Äã They already recognize the problem (no need to build awareness)
‚óè‚Äã Focus is on mechanism revelation + solution presentation
‚óè‚Äã Efficient, direct, informative
Pacing discipline:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Problem dramatization: 3-5 examples
Mechanism explanation: One clear explanation
Failed attempts: 2-4 examples
Discovery/authority: One story
Transformation: 4-5 data points

HEADLINE: Name The Need
From Schwartz: "Here you start by naming the need and/or its solution in your headline."
Use the headline selected from Part 3.
CRITICAL: The headline must make them immediately think "Yes, that's my problem."

OPENING: Problem Recognition Story Lead
Structure:
Paragraphs 1-3: ESTABLISH SHARED PROBLEM
From Great Leads - Story Lead for Problem-Aware:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Opens with "I had this problem too"
Names the specific problem
Shows recognition ("That's exactly what I'm dealing with")
Establishes credibility through shared experience

Example Opening:
"My cat Mia barely drank any water.
For eight years, I'd see her drink maybe once a day. Sometimes not at all.
I knew cats needed hydration. I worried about it constantly. But she just wasn't interested in
water.
I tried everything I could think of: moved her bowl to different spots, bought multiple bowls, tried
filtered water, got a wider bowl so her whiskers wouldn't touch the sides.
Nothing worked.

She'd walk past the water bowl a dozen times a day and never stop to drink.
I told myself 'some cats just don't drink much' and tried not to worry.
But I did worry.
Every time she slept longer than usual, I'd panic. Was she lethargic? Dehydrated? I'd Google
'cat kidney disease symptoms' at 2am and spiral.
I couldn't just enjoy her. I was always monitoring.
And then I learned something that changed everything.
It wasn't Mia's fault she wasn't drinking.
It wasn't my fault for not trying hard enough.
It was the water itself.
Plain water - filtered or not, in any bowl - isn't what cats are biologically designed to drink.
And once I understood WHY, the solution became obvious."
THIS IS DIFFERENT FROM:
‚óè‚Äã Unaware: Building awareness they have a problem
‚óè‚Äã Solution-Aware: Comparing to what they've already tried
‚óè‚Äã Problem-Aware: "I had this problem too. Here's why it happens."
Paragraphs 4-10: DRAMATIZE THE NEED
Don't just name the problem. Show how bad it is: [Use Q5, Q8]
"But here's what living with a cat who 'just doesn't drink much' actually meant:
[Show Level 2 emotional cost] [Show what they can't do/enjoy] [Show the worry, the monitoring,
the fear]"
Example:
"But here's what living with a cat who 'just doesn't drink much' actually meant:
I couldn't watch her sleep without checking if she was breathing too shallowly.
I couldn't enjoy cuddling without mentally tracking when she'd last had water.
Every vet visit felt like waiting for bad news. I'd calculated that treating kidney disease would
cost $3,000-8,000 - money I didn't have.

I'd read that by the time kidney disease symptoms show up, 75% of kidney function is already
gone. And that early-stage is reversible, but late-stage isn't.
So I lived with this constant low-level terror that I was missing the window. That by the time I
knew something was wrong, it would be too late.
When friends asked how Mia was doing, I'd say 'Great! She's healthy.'
I didn't tell them I was terrified I was failing her."

THE MECHANISM REVELATION SECTION (WITH GRADUALIZATION)
GRADUALIZATION EXECUTION (Paragraphs 11-30 - CRITICAL)
You mapped the gradualization sequence in Step 2b. Now execute it in the copy.
Paragraphs 11-30: Walk through your 5-7 micro-steps
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Each micro-step = 3-5 paragraphs
Use ABT structure within each step (And-But-Therefore)
Frame as investigation/discovery, not claims
Include physical details, dialogue, specific moments
Use their actual language [Q11]

PACING DISCIPLINE:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

DO NOT state the new belief before paragraph 28
If you're explaining mechanism before paragraph 25, you're going too fast
Each step must feel like natural progression of thought
The doubt must build gradually across 15-20 paragraphs

FORBIDDEN patterns in paragraphs 11-30:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚ùå "I was wrong about [old belief]"
‚ùå "The truth is [new belief]"
‚ùå "Here's what I discovered: [new belief]"
‚ùå Jumping from doubt ‚Üí new belief in under 10 paragraphs
‚ùå Stating the mechanism before building sufficient doubt

REQUIRED patterns:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

‚úì "But I noticed something weird..." (doubt)
‚úì "That didn't make sense..." (confusion)
‚úì "So I started investigating..." (curiosity)
‚úì "Found a study that stopped me cold..." (new information)
‚úì "I checked my own [situation]..." (personal application)

‚óè‚Äã ‚úì "That's when it clicked..." (realization)
MICRO-STEP EXECUTION GUIDE:
Micro-Step 1 (Introduce Small Doubt) - Paragraphs 11-13:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Share the specific anomaly that didn't fit
Frame as confusion, not criticism of old belief
Make it concrete: names, situations, specifics
Reader should think: "Huh, that IS weird"

Micro-Step 2 (Show Pattern) - Paragraphs 14-16:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Show this isn't isolated‚Äîhappened to others too
Use actual examples or research [Q12]
Build from "weird coincidence" to "consistent pattern"
Reader should think: "Why does this keep happening?"

Micro-Step 3 (Create Question) - Paragraphs 17-19:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Articulate the question the pattern raises
Show you investigating, not knowing the answer yet
Include failed attempts to explain it with old belief
Reader should think: "Yeah, what DOES explain this?"

Micro-Step 4 (Introduce New Information) - Paragraphs 20-23:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Present the new information using the tier you identified in Step 2b
If Tier 1 (Observable Proof): Show them what to look at in their own life right now
If Tier 2 (Reframe Problem): Explain their pain differently than they've heard before
If Tier 3 (Why Solutions Failed): Reveal the mechanism that makes past solutions
incomplete
Use authority figure dialogue if available [Q13]
Make it verifiable‚Äîthey should be able to test/observe this
Frame as discovery, not argument
Keep it simple, one key insight per paragraph
Reader should think: "That would explain it..." OR "Holy shit, I can see that right now"

TIER 1 EXECUTION (Observable Proof): Guide them to look at something in their immediate
environment:
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì "Check your cat's water bowl right now. How much is gone?"
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì "Look at your wet food label. What's the moisture percentage?"
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì "Do the math: If your cat eats 6oz of wet food at 42% moisture, that's 2.5oz of
water..."

Reader can verify in 60 seconds = belief shift is instant and sticky
TIER 2-3 EXECUTION (Reframe/Failed Solutions): Present research, authority insight, or
mechanism:
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Use authority dialogue [Q13] to explain mechanism
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Connect to their pattern of experience
‚óè‚Äã ‚úì Show why this explains what they've been seeing
Reader recognizes pattern = belief shift through understanding
Micro-Step 5 (Connect to Their Situation) - Paragraphs 24-26:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Apply new information to your specific case
Show the verification moment [Q12]
Include concrete details (checking labels, doing math, testing)
Reader should think: "Wait, that applies to ME too"

Micro-Step 6 (Show Gap) - Paragraphs 27-29:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Reveal the difference between assumption and reality
Quantify the gap if possible
Explain why old belief seemed right but wasn't complete
Show why casual attempts failed [Q3, Q4]
Reader should think: "Oh my god, I didn't realize..."

Micro-Step 7 (State New Belief as Discovery) - Paragraph 30:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

"That's when I realized [new belief]"
Frame as shared discovery
Connect back to their original problem/fear [Q1, Q8]
Reader should think: "Of course! That's why nothing worked!"

SCHWARTZ'S TEST at each micro-step: "Could the reader reject this thought?"
‚óè‚Äã If YES ‚Üí add more setup, slow down, provide more proof
‚óè‚Äã If NO ‚Üí the step is small enough, continue to next step
By paragraph 30, the new belief should feel like the reader's conclusion, not your pitch. The next
sections (mechanism, offer) simply provide the solution to the belief they now hold.

THE DISCOVERY & SOLUTION SECTION
Paragraphs 31-43: THE DISCOVERY STORY
Pattern for Problem-Aware Discovery:

‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Someone mentions the solution exists
Immediate disbelief/curiosity
They explain the mechanism
Realization: "That explains everything"
Investigation/verification

Example:
"I only learned about this because my friend Rachel mentioned her cat Luna - who'd also been a
non-drinker - was now drinking 5-6 times per day.
'How?' I asked.
'Electrolytes,' she said. 'Cats need mineralized water, not plain water. Once I added electrolytes
to Luna's water, she started drinking it like crazy.'
I stared at her.
'What do you mean, electrolytes?'
'The minerals that are in prey fluid. Sodium, potassium, magnesium. Cat kidneys evolved to
process water WITH those minerals. Plain water doesn't have them. That's why cats don't drink
it.'
It was like something clicked.
Of course.
Cats evolved to get hydration from prey. Prey contains minerals. Their entire system is built
around processing mineralized fluid.
Plain water isn't what they're designed to drink.
That's why Mia would walk past her bowl a hundred times without stopping. It wasn't
stubbornness. It wasn't low thirst drive.
It was biology."
Paragraphs 44-50: AUTHORITY EXPLAINS THE MECHANISM
Authority figure validates the mechanism [Q13]:
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã Authority enters naturally
‚óè‚Äã They explain the mechanism behind the problem [Q2]
‚óè‚Äã They explain the mechanism behind the solution [Q6]

‚óè‚Äã In plain language
‚óè‚Äã Makes solution feel inevitable
Example:
"I brought it up at Mia's next vet appointment.
'Is it true cats need electrolytes in their water?'
Dr. Chen nodded. 'Yes. Most people don't know this, but cats evolved to get hydration from prey
- which is about 70-80% water plus minerals. Their kidneys are optimized for processing that
specific mineral profile.'
She pulled up a chart.
'When cats drink plain water, their kidneys have to work harder to extract what they need. It's
not efficient. Plus, plain water doesn't trigger the same palatability response as mineralized fluid.
That's why cats have what we call low thirst drives - they're not actually low-thirst animals,
they're just not interested in drinking something their bodies aren't designed to process.'
'So if I add electrolytes...'
'Their kidneys will absorb it 3-4x more efficiently than plain water. And the mineral content
makes it taste natural to them - like what they'd get from prey. They'll drink it willingly.'
She paused.
'Most vets don't mention this because it's not a pharmaceutical intervention. There's no
prescription. But from a biochemical standpoint, it makes complete sense. Cats need
mineralized hydration, not plain water.'"

THE MECHANISM EXPLANATION SECTION
Explain your product's mechanism in plain language:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

What actually causes the problem [Q2]
How your mechanism addresses the root cause [Q6]
Why it works where other things failed [Q4]
Make it simple and inevitable

Example:
"Here's what HydraBoost does:
It adds the electrolytes (sodium, potassium, magnesium) that cats evolved to drink - in the same
ratios found in prey fluid.

When you add it to their water, you're recreating what their bodies are designed to process.
The minerals make it taste natural to them. Their taste receptors recognize it as 'real' hydration not plain water.
And their kidneys absorb it efficiently - 3-4x better than plain water - because it matches the
mineral profile their kidneys evolved to handle.
It's not complicated. It's not medication. It's not behavior modification.
It's just giving them what their biology expects.
Plain water + prey-matched minerals = what cats are designed to drink.
That's it."

SCHWARTZ PRINCIPLES (Apply Throughout)
Principle 1: Name The Need Clearly
From Schwartz: "Start by naming the need"
‚óè‚Äã Be direct about the problem they recognize
‚óè‚Äã Don't dance around it
‚óè‚Äã Make them think "Yes, that's exactly my problem"
Principle 2: Dramatize The Need Vividly
From Schwartz: "Dramatize the need so vividly that the prospect realizes just how badly he
needs the solution"
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Show emotional cost [Q5]
Show what they can't do/enjoy [Q8]
Make them FEEL how bad this problem is
"I thought I was just being paranoid. But I couldn't stop worrying."

Principle 3: Blame The Mechanism, Not Them
From RMBC (Stefan Georgi):
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

The problem isn't their fault
The mechanism is to blame
"You didn't know about the biological mismatch"
"Nobody told you cats need mineralized water"
Removes guilt, builds trust.

Principle 4: Present Product As Inevitable Solution

From Schwartz: "Present your product as the inevitable solution"
‚óè‚Äã Once mechanism is understood, solution is obvious
‚óè‚Äã "Of course - cats need mineralized water. That's what HydraBoost provides."
‚óè‚Äã Don't "sell" it, make it feel inevitable
Principle 5: Deliver The Belief-Shift
Use the angle from Part 1 to structure the revelation:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Current limiting belief [Angle Q1]
New information [Angle Q2]
Revelation moment [Angle Q3]
Urgency [Angle Q4]
Make the belief-shift feel like a natural discovery, not a sales pitch.

TRANSFORMATION TIMELINE
[Use timeline from Q10]
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã

Day 1: First observable change
Week 1: Consistent pattern
Month 1: Verification/proof
Month 3: Full transformation

Example:
"I ordered HydraBoost that night.
Here's what happened:
Day 1: Added it to Mia's water. She walked over, sniffed, and drank. Then came back an hour
later and drank again. By end of day, she'd drunk 3 times - more than I'd seen in a week.
Week 1: Drinking 4-5 times per day consistently. Her coat looked shinier. She had more energy.
I realized what I'd been calling 'she's just getting older' was actually mild chronic dehydration.
Month 1: Took her for bloodwork. Dr. Chen called that afternoon. 'Her kidney values are
excellent. Better than they were a year ago. Whatever you're doing, keep doing it.'
Month 3: Mia's energy is like when she was younger. I don't check if she's breathing at night
anymore. The constant anxiety is gone. Her kidneys are protected - actually protected, not just
'hopefully okay.'
And I wish I'd known about this eight years ago."

OFFER SECTION
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã Acknowledge the mechanism made sense - "Once I understood the mechanism, the
solution was obvious"
‚óè‚Äã State the offer [From Q14] - Price, included, guarantee
‚óè‚Äã Make it accessible - "It's simple. It works. It's not expensive."
‚óè‚Äã Remove risk - Guarantee details
Example:
"Once I understood the mechanism - that cats need mineralized water, not plain - the solution
was obvious.
Get HydraBoost. Add it to their water. Give their kidneys what they're designed to process.
$39 for a two-month supply.
Includes:
‚óè‚Äã Electrolyte powder (60-day supply)
‚óè‚Äã Measuring scoop
‚óè‚Äã Hydration guide
60-day money-back guarantee: If your cat doesn't drink more water, or you don't see
improvement in energy/coat quality, send it back for a full refund. Including return shipping.
You risk nothing.
Either your cat starts drinking more water and their kidneys get the hydration they're designed to
have...
Or you get your money back.
[CTA: Get HydraBoost - 60-Day Guarantee]"

CLOSING: The Mechanism Made It Obvious
Problem-Aware closing emphasizes: "Once I understood WHY, the solution was inevitable."
Structure:
‚óè‚Äã Restate the mechanism - "The problem isn't that cats don't drink. It's that plain water isn't
what they're designed to drink."
‚óè‚Äã Make solution feel obvious - "Once you know that, the solution is simple: give them
mineralized water."

‚óè‚Äã No pressure, just logic - "You know the problem. Now you know the mechanism. The
solution is up to you."
‚óè‚Äã Cost of continuing without this knowledge - "I spent 8 years not knowing. Those are 8
years I can't get back."
Example:
"Look, I can't make you try this.
But I can tell you what I wish someone had told me eight years ago:
The problem isn't that your cat doesn't drink enough water.
The problem is that plain water isn't what their kidneys are designed to process.
Cats evolved to get hydration from prey - water plus minerals. Their entire system is built around
that.
When you give them plain water, you're giving them something that doesn't taste right and that
their kidneys don't absorb efficiently.
That's why they don't drink it.
Once I understood that, the solution was obvious: give Mia water with the minerals her kidneys
expect.
That's what HydraBoost does.
And it worked. Day 1.
I spent eight years worrying. Eight years of checking if she was breathing. Eight years of
Googling kidney disease symptoms at 2am.
All because I didn't know about the mechanism.
You know now.
The choice is yours.
[CTA: Try HydraBoost Risk-Free]
To understanding the mechanism,
Sarah Mitchell Mia's Human
P.S. ‚Äî The 60-day guarantee means you risk nothing. Try it. See if your cat drinks more. See if
the worry fades. If not, you get your money back. But if it works - if the mechanism makes sense
for your cat like it did for mine - you'll wish you'd known about this sooner."
