Hair Loss in PCOS: The Biological Root
Causes Explained
Introduction to PCOS and Hair Loss
Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a hormonal disorder that often underlies hair changes in
women. Many PCOS sufferers notice scalp hair thinning or increased shedding, a distressing
symptom linked to the syndrome‚Äôs hormonal imbalances. Research shows that PCOS-related
hair loss is usually driven by a combination of hormonal disruptions ‚Äì notably excess
male hormones (androgens) and metabolic issues like insulin resistance‚Äã
mdpi.com
. These factors create an environment that favors hair thinning (female pattern hair loss) in
affected women. In fact, female pattern hair loss (FPHL) is now recognized as a common
manifestation of PCOS; one study found it in about 22% of women meeting PCOS criteria‚Äã
mdpi.com
. This type of hair loss can significantly impact quality of life, making it important to understand
the root physiological causes.

Androgen Excess: The Primary Culprit
Elevated androgens (male hormones) are the key driver of hair loss in PCOS. Women
normally produce small amounts of androgens (like testosterone), but in PCOS these levels can
be abnormally high‚Äã
medicalnewstoday.com
. High androgen levels are responsible for many PCOS symptoms ‚Äì including unwanted body
hair (hirsutism), acne, and scalp hair thinning. Excess testosterone and its potent derivative
DHT (dihydrotestosterone) lead to a type of hair loss known as androgenetic alopecia
(female pattern hair loss)‚Äã
medicalnewstoday.com

. DHT is a hormone that, in normal amounts, has various roles in hair growth. However, in
PCOS the overabundance of DHT has a paradoxical effect on scalp hair: instead of
promoting growth, it causes susceptible follicles on the scalp to shrink. Essentially, androgens
bind to hair follicle cells and disrupt the normal hair growth cycle, shortening the growth
phase and causing follicles to produce progressively thinner, weaker hairs‚Äã
pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov
. Over time this hormonal onslaught miniaturizes the hair follicles, so they yield only fine
‚Äúpeach fuzz‚Äù (vellus hairs) instead of normal strands‚Äã
mdpi.com
‚Äã
pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov
. Women with hyperandrogenic conditions like PCOS often develop this pattern of hair thinning
at a younger age, underscoring the strong link between androgen imbalance and female hair
loss‚Äã
mdpi.com
.

How Androgens Affect the Hair Follicle
Illustration of how excess androgens (DHT) affect a hair follicle. DHT binding to receptors in the
dermal papilla cell alters signaling pathways (like Wnt/Œ≤-catenin, crucial for hair growth) and
releases factors (DKK1, TGF-Œ≤1, IL-6) that shorten the growth phase and miniaturize the follicle.
The result is a transition from normal thick hair to short, thin, unpigmented hair over time.‚Äã
mdpi.com
‚Äã
mdpi.com
Under normal conditions, scalp hair follicles cycle through growth and rest phases regulated by
hormones. Androgens (like DHT) help control this cycle, but too much DHT throws it off
balance‚Äã
medicalnewstoday.com
. In PCOS, androgen excess ‚Äúoverstimulates‚Äù the follicles in a harmful way. The illustration
above shows that when DHT binds to the hair follicle‚Äôs dermal papilla cells, it alters internal
signals. One major pathway affected is the Wnt/Œ≤-catenin pathway, which is essential for
maintaining the hair‚Äôs growth phase (anagen). Excess androgen signaling increases
inhibitors like DKK1 (which blocks Wnt) and raises local levels of growth-suppressing cytokines
(e.g. TGF-Œ≤1 and IL-6). These changes cause the follicle to exit the growth phase too early and
enter the resting phase (telogen) more quickly‚Äã
pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov
. As a result, hairs become shorter, spend less time growing, and shed sooner, leading to a
visible thinning. Over multiple cycles, the follicles progressively shrink (a process called
miniaturization), producing thinner and lighter hairs‚Äã

mdpi.com
. This mechanism explains why women with PCOS notice diffuse thinning on the top of the scalp
‚Äì hair strands become wispy and many follicles lie dormant. Notably, androgen-sensitive scalp
areas (the crown and mid-scalp) are affected most: women often see a wider part or reduced
volume in these regions, while the frontal hairline is usually preserved‚Äã
mdpi.com
. It‚Äôs the same process seen in male-pattern baldness, highlighting DHT as the root hormonal
trigger for hair follicle regression in PCOS‚Äã
pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov
.

Insulin Resistance and Hormonal Synergy
Many women with PCOS also have insulin resistance, a condition where the body‚Äôs cells don‚Äôt
respond well to insulin. To compensate, the pancreas produces more insulin, leading to
hyperinsulinemia (excess insulin in the blood). This is important because insulin excess further
fuels the androgen overload. High insulin levels lower the production of sex
hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) in the liver‚Äã
mdpi.com
. SHBG is a protein that normally binds up testosterone and DHT, keeping their free levels in
check. When SHBG is low (as is common in PCOS), more testosterone is available in its
active form, amplifying its effects on hair follicles‚Äã
medicalnewstoday.com
. Additionally, insulin can work together with luteinizing hormone (LH) in the ovaries to boost
androgen production by ovarian theca cells‚Äã
mdpi.com
‚Äã
mdpi.com
. In essence, insulin resistance creates a vicious cycle: it raises free androgens, which then
worsen PCOS symptoms (including hair loss), and those same hormonal imbalances can
worsen insulin resistance further. The net effect is that insulin issues magnify the
androgen-driven hair follicle changes. For example, studies indicate that women with PCOS and
insulin resistance have higher free testosterone levels contributing to hair thinning‚Äã
medicalnewstoday.com
. By increasing testosterone availability and ovarian androgen output, insulin resistance
ensures that hair follicles in PCOS are exposed to an even greater hormonal assault,
accelerating the thinning and shedding process‚Äã
mdpi.com
.

Genetic Susceptibility of Hair Follicles

While androgen excess is the main cause, not every woman with PCOS will experience
noticeable hair loss ‚Äì and this often comes down to genetics and follicle sensitivity. Research
shows that female pattern hair loss requires a genetic predisposition: hair follicles in certain
individuals are genetically primed to be more sensitive to androgens‚Äã
mdpi.com
‚Äã
mdpi.com
. In PCOS, this means two women with the same hormone levels might have very different hair
outcomes. Genes influencing the androgen receptor (AR) can heighten a follicle‚Äôs
response to hormones. Some women inherit variants of the AR gene (or related genes) that
make their hair follicles extra responsive to DHT, leading to hair thinning even if their hormone
levels are only mildly elevated‚Äã
mdpi.com
. Moreover, studies have found higher activity of 5Œ±-reductase (the enzyme that converts
testosterone to DHT) in the scalp of those predisposed to hair loss‚Äã
pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov
. This means a woman with PCOS who has this genetic trait might produce more DHT locally in
her scalp hair follicles, intensifying the miniaturization process. There is also evidence linking
variants in the estrogen receptor gene (ESR2) to susceptibility for female pattern hair loss‚Äã
mdpi.com
, suggesting that a complex interplay of hormone receptors determines how hair follicles react.
All these genetic factors explain why some PCOS patients have severe hair thinning while
others do not: the root cause (androgens) is the same, but the follicular sensitivity to those
hormones differs from person to person. Notably, dermatology experts have observed that a
significant number of women with PCOS and hair thinning show clinical signs of androgen
excess (like acne or hirsutism) even when blood tests are normal‚Äã
mdpi.com
. This implies their hair follicles are responding strongly to even normal-range hormones ‚Äì again
highlighting the role of sensitivity. In summary, PCOS creates the ‚Äúperfect storm‚Äù of high
androgens, and genetics determine how destructively a woman‚Äôs hair follicles weather
that storm.

Chronic Inflammation and Other Contributing Factors
Beyond hormones, scientists are exploring other factors that may contribute to
PCOS-related hair loss, especially chronic inflammation. PCOS is often accompanied by a
state of low-grade inflammation in the body, and emerging evidence suggests this could
aggravate hair follicle damage. For instance, researchers have found higher levels of
inflammatory signaling molecules in balding scalp areas. One example is prostaglandin
D2 (PGD‚ÇÇ) ‚Äì a compound discovered to be elevated in the scalp of individuals with androgenetic
alopecia. PGD‚ÇÇ can inhibit hair growth and promote follicle miniaturization, and applying it
to skin has been shown to shrink hair follicles in experimental studies‚Äã

mdpi.com
. Moreover, biopsies of affected scalp often reveal a micro-inflammation ‚Äì a subtle infiltration
of inflammatory cells around the hair follicles‚Äã
mdpi.com
. This inflammation might further stress the follicles and shorten their growth phase,
compounding the direct hormonal effects. Interestingly, there have even been rare cases of
female pattern hair loss in individuals who lack androgen receptors, suggesting that
non-androgen pathways (like inflammatory pathways) can sometimes lead to similar hair loss
outcomes‚Äã
mdpi.com
. In PCOS patients, factors like obesity (common in PCOS) can heighten inflammatory
cytokines, and high androgen levels themselves may induce some pro-inflammatory factors ‚Äì
creating a feedback loop. Nutrient deficiencies (such as low iron or vitamin D, sometimes seen
in PCOS) and thyroid disorders can also affect hair health, though these are secondary factors
and not the primary PCOS mechanism. It‚Äôs important to note that while inflammation and other
hormones (thyroid, estrogen, etc.) can influence hair, in PCOS these are minor contributors
compared to the dominant effect of androgen excess. Thus, the best-supported scientific
view is that hair loss in PCOS fundamentally stems from the androgen-driven disruption
of the hair cycle, with inflammation possibly acting as an additional stressor on vulnerable
follicles‚Äã
mdpi.com
.

Key Takeaways
‚óè‚Äã Androgens (Male Hormones) Trigger PCOS Hair Loss: Excess testosterone and DHT
in PCOS women bind to scalp hair follicles and shrink them, causing hairs to become
thin and fall out. This leads to female pattern hair loss ‚Äì diffuse thinning on the crown
with an intact frontal hairline‚Äã
mdpi.com‚Äã
‚Äã
mdpi.com‚Äã
.‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã Disrupted Hair Growth Cycle: DHT shortens the hair‚Äôs growth phase (anagen) and
prolongs the resting/shedding phase (telogen). Over time, follicles produce only fine,
short hairs. In other words, too many androgens ‚Äúflip the switch‚Äù in follicles to stop
growing hair normally, resulting in increased shedding and reduced density‚Äã
pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov‚Äã
‚Äã
pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov‚Äã
.‚Äã

‚óè‚Äã Insulin Resistance Amplifies Androgen Effects: Many PCOS patients have insulin
resistance, which raises insulin levels. High insulin lowers SHBG (the protein that
tethers hormones), freeing more testosterone in the bloodstream‚Äã
mdpi.com‚Äã
. It also stimulates the ovaries to make even more androgens‚Äã

. The outcome is a hormonal double-hit on hair follicles: more circulating DHT to harm
them‚Äã
medicalnewstoday.com‚Äã
‚Äã
mdpi.com‚Äã
.‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã Genetic Sensitivity Matters: Not all women with PCOS go bald ‚Äì genetics determine
each person‚Äôs follicular sensitivity. Variations in genes (like the androgen receptor)
mean some women‚Äôs hair follicles are extremely responsive to even slight hormone
increases‚Äã
mdpi.com‚Äã
‚Äã
mdpi.com‚Äã
. Those with a family predisposition to pattern hair loss will be most affected when PCOS
raises their androgen levels.‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã Scientifically Proven Mechanisms Only: Importantly, credible research does not
support alternative ‚Äúmystery‚Äù causes for PCOS-related hair loss. The consensus is
that androgen excess is the root cause, with insulin resistance and genetics as key
modifiers. While healthy lifestyle changes, balanced diets, and proper medical
management can help slow hair loss (by improving insulin sensitivity and lowering
androgens), unproven treatments or supplements that don‚Äôt address hormones are
unlikely to be effective. The focus remains on the hormonal and biological pathways
described above ‚Äì a cascade starting from excess androgens and ending in hair follicle
miniaturization‚Äã
mdpi.com‚Äã
‚Äã
pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov‚Äã
.‚Äã

Conclusion
Hair loss in women with PCOS can be traced to concrete, evidence-based biological
mechanisms. At its core, the problem is hormonal imbalance ‚Äì especially too much
androgen ‚Äì that disrupts the hair growth cycle. PCOS creates a perfect storm of high
testosterone/DHT and metabolic issues (like insulin resistance) that together tip the scales

against healthy hair growth. These hormones shorten the growth phase of hair, trigger follicle
miniaturization, and lead to gradual thinning (often noticed as a widening part or less volume on
top). Genetic predisposition dictates how severely an individual‚Äôs hair is affected, and factors
like inflammation may add further pressure on the follicles. It‚Äôs crucial to note that these
explanations are grounded in rigorous medical research and reflect the consensus of
endocrinology and dermatology experts ‚Äì not myths or marketing claims. By understanding
that androgen excess is the true root cause of PCOS-related hair loss‚Äã
pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov
, patients and doctors can better focus on treatments that target these hormonal pathways. This
might include therapies to reduce androgen levels or block their effect on hair follicles, alongside
managing insulin resistance. In summary, women with PCOS experiencing hair loss aren‚Äôt
facing an unsolvable mystery: the culprit is known (androgens), and it operates through
well-characterized biological pathways. Recognizing this can guide effective, evidence-based
approaches to protect and restore hair health in PCOS.

‚úÖ The Link Between PCOS and Inflammation (and How It Impacts Hair
Loss)

1. Systemic Inflammation in PCOS ‚Äî What Causes It?
Women with PCOS often show signs of chronic low-grade inflammation, even if they‚Äôre not
visibly sick. Here's what triggers it:
‚óè‚Äã Insulin resistance: Common in PCOS, it leads to elevated insulin and blood sugar,
which activate immune cells and inflammatory signaling.‚Äã

‚óè‚Äã Abdominal fat (visceral adiposity): Many PCOS sufferers store fat around their
organs, which secretes pro-inflammatory cytokines like IL-6, TNF-Œ±, and CRP.‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã Hyperandrogenism: High androgens (like testosterone) can stimulate immune
responses and promote inflammation in tissues.‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã Gut dysbiosis: Emerging research suggests that PCOS is linked with changes in gut
microbiota, which can increase intestinal permeability and lead to systemic
inflammation.‚Äã

üìö Key markers elevated in PCOS women:‚Äã

C-reactive protein (CRP), interleukin-6 (IL-6), tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-Œ±),
and oxidative stress markers like malondialdehyde (MDA).

2. How Inflammation Affects the Hair Growth Cycle
Even if inflammation is systemic (whole-body), it can have localized effects ‚Äî including in the
scalp:
‚óè‚Äã Hair follicle cycling is highly sensitive to inflammation.‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã Inflammatory cytokines like IL-1, IL-6, TNF-Œ±, and prostaglandin D2 (PGD2) have been
shown to:‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Shorten the anagen (growth) phase‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Push hairs prematurely into telogen (resting/shedding) phase‚Äã
‚óã‚Äã Trigger mini-inflammation around follicles, contributing to miniaturization‚Äã

These factors disrupt the natural growth rhythm, leading to thinning and excessive shedding
(called telogen effluvium or pattern loss).

3. Is There Evidence of Local Scalp Inflammation in PCOS?
There‚Äôs no conclusive proof that PCOS directly causes scalp-specific inflammation like in
autoimmune diseases (e.g., alopecia areata). However:

‚óè‚Äã Women with androgenetic alopecia (common in PCOS) show microinflammation in
scalp biopsies, especially around shrinking follicles.‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã Pro-inflammatory mediators like PGD2 are elevated in balding scalp areas ‚Äî they‚Äôre
believed to inhibit hair growth.‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã This local inflammation could be exacerbated by systemic inflammation in PCOS,
even if the scalp isn't visibly inflamed.‚Äã

üßÝ Translation: PCOS doesn‚Äôt "cause scalp inflammation" directly, but the
body-wide inflammatory environment it creates can worsen hair follicle
sensitivity and push follicles into early shedding.

4. Summary: Inflammation‚Äôs Role in PCOS Hair Loss

Inflammation + androgens hit harder

üß© Bottom Line:
PCOS-related inflammation starts systemically (from insulin resistance, fat cells,
hormones), and while it may not cause obvious scalp inflammation, it still disrupts
the hair growth cycle by altering the environment hair follicles live in. When
combined with androgens and genetic sensitivity, it creates the perfect storm for
hair thinning.

If you'd like, I can turn this into a compelling content piece or even visual explainers for your
brand. Let me know how you'd like to use this next!

Research to image Ads SOP

üîç STEP 1: Understand the Priority Pyramid

‚óè‚Äã Regrow my hair‚Äã

RESEARCH THE ROOT CAUSE
OF THE PROBLEM

The root cause explains why the prospect is experiencing the problem in the first place.
It reveals what‚Äôs truly happening beneath the surface ‚Äî not just the symptoms, but the
deeper trigger behind them.
By identifying the root cause, we‚Äôre able to show the prospect why other solutions
haven‚Äôt worked ‚Äî because they fail to address the real issue.
This not only builds trust (we understand exactly what‚Äôs causing their struggle), but also
creates a clear path to a solution that actually works ‚Äî one that targets the problem at
its source.
‚Äã
‚Äã
‚Äã
‚Äã
‚Äã

‚Äã
‚Äã

üß¨ THE 3-PART ROOT REVIVAL MECHANISM‚Ñ¢
This solution mechanism tackles every major root cause behind PCOS hair loss ‚Äî not just
symptoms.

1. DHT Neutralization
‚Üí Target the ‚Äúweed killer‚Äù directly.
DHT is the core villain. It binds to hair follicles and shrinks them over time.

‚úÖ The mechanism: Naturally inhibit 5-alpha reductase, the enzyme that turns testosterone
into DHT ‚Äî and increase SHBG (Sex Hormone Binding Globulin) to mop up excess free
testosterone.‚Äã
This cuts off the weed killer at the source ‚Äî protecting your scalp from future damage.

2. Insulin Rebalancing
‚Üí Break the cycle that keeps pouring DHT on your scalp.
PCOS-induced insulin resistance supercharges your DHT production. It‚Äôs a feedback loop:
More insulin ‚Üí more testosterone ‚Üí more DHT ‚Üí more hair loss.

‚úÖ The mechanism: Restore insulin sensitivity by supporting blood sugar regulation (via
nutrition, stress balance, targeted herbs/minerals).‚Äã
This calms the hormonal chaos, bringing testosterone and DHT back into balance.

3. Follicle Desensitization & Inflammation Repair
‚Üí Protect the scalp from damage even when hormone levels are ‚Äúnormal.‚Äù
Some women with PCOS lose hair even with normal testosterone ‚Äî because their follicles
are hypersensitive or inflamed.

‚úÖ The mechanism: Reduce chronic inflammation and deliver nutrients that ‚Äúdesensitize‚Äù
follicles to androgen stress.‚Äã
This includes antioxidant repair compounds, stress adaptogens, and scalp-nourishing
micronutrients that reverse the miniaturization process and restart the growth cycle.

üîÅ Why This Feels New (and Works Better Than Anything They‚Äôve Tried):
‚óè‚Äã It doesn‚Äôt rely on topicals or empty promises ‚Äî it fixes the real, invisible problem
inside.‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã It doesn‚Äôt treat just one hormone ‚Äî it calms the full hormonal-metabolic storm.‚Äã
‚óè‚Äã It recognizes the genetic sensitivity factor, and still provides a pathway to regrow and
protect.‚Äã

üí° Emotional Framing (For Future Marketing Copy):

This isn‚Äôt about forcing hair to grow ‚Äî it‚Äôs about removing what‚Äôs been silently
killing it.‚Äã

‚úÖ PHASE 1: UNDERSTAND THE MECHANISM
(Technically + Logically)

üå± The Root Revival Mechanism‚Ñ¢
This is a three-pronged biological system reset that works by addressing the hormonal and
metabolic dysfunctions inside the body ‚Äî all of which silently block healthy hair growth.
It works by:
1.‚Äã Stopping DHT from attacking the follicles (DHT is a potent form of testosterone that
shrinks hair follicles and makes hair thinner).‚Äã
2.‚Äã Reducing insulin resistance, which is responsible for triggering excess DHT
production.‚Äã
3.‚Äã Repairing inflammation and follicle sensitivity, which allows the hair to grow normally
again ‚Äî even in women whose hormones seem ‚Äúnormal‚Äù on lab tests.‚Äã

This mechanism doesn‚Äôt ‚Äústimulate‚Äù hair growth artificially.‚Äã

üßÝ PHASE 2: EXPLAIN THE MECHANISM IN PLAIN BUT
CREDIBLE LANGUAGE

Here‚Äôs what the Root Revival Mechanism actually does ‚Äî in real-world language:
1.‚Äã It lowers DHT, a hormone that shrinks and weakens your hair follicles over time. By
slowing down the conversion of testosterone into DHT, this mechanism protects your
follicles from damage.‚Äã
2.‚Äã It improves insulin sensitivity, which helps your body avoid overproducing the

3.‚Äã It reduces inflammation and re-sensitizes the hair follicles, so your scalp is no

üåø Metaphor:
